cases.title: Burning With Urination for Two Days
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of painful, frequent urination.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with two days of burning during urination, urinary frequency, and a persistent urge to void even after emptying her bladder. She estimates she is urinating every hour and notes mild suprapubic discomfort. She denies fever, chills, flank pain, nausea, or vomiting. There is no vaginal discharge, pruritus, or new-onset dyspareunia. She is sexually active with a single male partner and uses a diaphragm with spermicide for contraception. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and she has no possibility of pregnancy concerns but agrees to testing. She has had one similar episode 18 months ago that resolved with antibiotics. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She takes no daily medications. She reports a rash with sulfa drugs (hives). She does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and works as a graduate student. Family history is noncontributory. She has no history of kidney stones, urinary instrumentation, or recent travel.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and in no distress. She is afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Abdominal exam reveals mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation without rebound or guarding. There is no costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness bilaterally. Bowel sounds are normal. External genitalia are unremarkable with no vaginal discharge, lesions, or erythema noted; the patient declined a full pelvic exam given absence of vaginal symptoms. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate next step to evaluate her symptoms?
options: Obtain a urinalysis (with urine dipstick) from a clean-catch midstream sample
Send a urine culture and withhold treatment until results return
Obtain a renal ultrasound to evaluate for obstruction
Order a non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis
stages.explanation: A clean-catch midstream urinalysis with dipstick is the appropriate first test in a young, healthy, nonpregnant woman with classic lower urinary tract symptoms; leukocyte esterase and nitrites help confirm infection at the point of care. Withholding all treatment pending culture is wrong because culture is not required in uncomplicated cystitis and would unnecessarily delay relief; empiric therapy is standard. A renal ultrasound is unnecessary without signs of obstruction, retention, or stones. A non-contrast CT is for suspected nephrolithiasis (colicky flank pain, hematuria), not isolated dysuria and frequency, and exposes her to radiation without benefit.

stages.label: URINALYSIS
stages.content:
Color: yellow, cloudy
Leukocyte esterase: positive
Nitrites: positive
WBC: 35 /hpf
RBC: 4 /hpf
Nitrite-producing bacteria: present
Epithelial cells: few
Urine pregnancy test (β-hCG): negative
Urine culture: pending (not routinely required)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history, exam, and urinalysis, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute pyelonephritis
Vaginitis
Uncomplicated cystitis
Urethritis from a sexually transmitted infection
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated cystitis is the diagnosis: a young nonpregnant woman with dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic tenderness, and a urinalysis showing pyuria with positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites. Acute pyelonephritis is wrong because she has no fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, or systemic symptoms. Vaginitis is unlikely given no vaginal discharge or pruritus and a positive nitrite test pointing to bacterial UTI. Urethritis from an STI typically causes dysuria with discharge and is associated with pyuria but negative nitrites; her positive nitrites strongly favor a nitrate-reducing uropathogen such as E. coli.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering her allergy history, which is the most appropriate first-line empiric antibiotic?
options: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily for 3 days
Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
Ciprofloxacin 250 mg twice daily for 3 days
Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 7 days
stages.explanation: Nitrofurantoin is a recommended first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis and is appropriate here because she has a documented sulfa allergy. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, although first-line in many patients, is contraindicated by her sulfa hypersensitivity (hives). Fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin are effective but should be reserved due to collateral resistance and serious adverse effects (tendinopathy, QT prolongation), making them inappropriate as initial therapy for uncomplicated cystitis. Amoxicillin alone has high E. coli resistance rates and poor efficacy, so it is not recommended for empiric UTI treatment.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: She asks how to reduce future infections. Which counseling point is most evidence-based?
options: Begin daily prophylactic antibiotics indefinitely
Stop all sexual activity permanently
Consider switching from a diaphragm-spermicide method and stay well hydrated
Routinely obtain urine cultures every month even when asymptomatic
stages.explanation: Diaphragm-with-spermicide use is an established modifiable risk factor for recurrent UTIs, so counseling her about alternative contraception plus adequate hydration is the most appropriate evidence-based advice. Indefinite daily antibiotic prophylaxis is reserved for women with frequent recurrences and is not first-line after a single uncomplicated episode. Permanently stopping sexual activity is neither necessary nor realistic; postcoital voiding is a reasonable measure. Routine surveillance cultures in asymptomatic patients are not recommended (asymptomatic bacteriuria should not be treated except in pregnancy or before urologic procedures) and would lead to unnecessary antibiotic use.

cases.diagnosis: Uncomplicated cystitis
pearls: In a young, nonpregnant, afebrile woman with dysuria, frequency, and urgency, classic symptoms plus a positive dipstick are enough to treat empirically without a culture.
Positive nitrites indicate a nitrate-reducing uropathogen (commonly E. coli); positive leukocyte esterase reflects pyuria.
First-line agents are nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if no sulfa allergy and local resistance <20%), or fosfomycin; reserve fluoroquinolones.
Absence of fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness distinguishes cystitis from pyelonephritis.

cases.title: Fever and Right-Sided Back Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of fever, chills, and worsening right flank pain.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with one day of high fever, shaking chills, and progressively worsening right-sided back pain. Three days ago she developed burning with urination and increased frequency, which she initially ignored. Since this morning she has had nausea with two episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting and feels generally exhausted. She denies vaginal discharge, diarrhea, or recent travel. She is sexually active and uses oral contraceptive pills; her last menstrual period was three weeks ago. She has no prior history of urinary tract infections, kidney stones, or urologic surgery. Past medical history is unremarkable. Her only medication is a combined oral contraceptive. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. She works as a teacher and has had no sick contacts. She reports no dysuria-free intervals since symptom onset and has been unable to keep down fluids today.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable, flushed, and mildly dehydrated. She is febrile and tachycardic. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal aside from tachycardia. Abdomen is soft with mild right lower quadrant and suprapubic tenderness, no rebound or guarding, and normal bowel sounds. There is marked right costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness; left CVA is non-tender. Mucous membranes are dry. There is no rash, no calf tenderness, and no vaginal discharge on limited inspection. Capillary refill is brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial workup is most appropriate for this febrile patient with flank pain?
options: Urinalysis and urine culture plus blood cultures and a basic metabolic panel with CBC
Urine dipstick only, then discharge with oral antibiotics
Immediate non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis before any labs
Pelvic ultrasound to evaluate the ovaries
stages.explanation: A febrile, tachycardic patient with flank pain and CVA tenderness warrants urinalysis with culture, blood cultures, and labs (CBC, BMP) to confirm upper-tract infection, guide antibiotics, and assess for sepsis and renal function. Dipstick-only with immediate discharge is inadequate for someone with systemic illness and vomiting who may need IV therapy and admission. Immediate CT is not required for uncomplicated pyelonephritis; imaging is reserved for suspected obstruction, abscess, or failure to improve. Pelvic ultrasound targets gynecologic pathology, which is less likely given urinary prodrome, pyuria, and CVA tenderness.

stages.label: LABS & URINALYSIS
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (84% neutrophils)
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Na 137 mEq/L
K 3.9 mEq/L
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Glucose 102 mg/dL
Urinalysis: leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive
Urine WBC: >50 /hpf
WBC casts: present
Urine RBC: 8 /hpf
Urine pregnancy test: negative
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which urinalysis finding most specifically localizes the infection to the kidney (upper tract)?
options: Positive nitrites
Positive leukocyte esterase
White blood cell casts
Microscopic hematuria
stages.explanation: White blood cell casts form within the renal tubules and therefore specifically localize inflammation to the kidney, supporting pyelonephritis rather than lower-tract cystitis. Positive nitrites indicate a nitrate-reducing uropathogen but occur in both cystitis and pyelonephritis, so they do not localize the infection. Leukocyte esterase reflects pyuria from anywhere in the urinary tract and is nonspecific for level. Microscopic hematuria is common in many urinary conditions including cystitis and stones and does not pinpoint the kidney. WBC casts plus fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness clinch upper-tract disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her vomiting, tachycardia, fever, and inability to tolerate oral intake, what is the best initial management?
options: Discharge on oral ciprofloxacin with next-day follow-up
Admit for IV fluids and IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) with antiemetics
Observe without antibiotics pending culture results
Start oral nitrofurantoin and arrange outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: She has acute pyelonephritis with systemic toxicity and intolerance of oral intake (vomiting, tachycardia, hypotension trend), so admission for IV fluids, IV antibiotics such as ceftriaxone, and antiemetics is appropriate. Discharge on oral ciprofloxacin is unsafe when a patient cannot keep down fluids or medications and shows signs of early sepsis. Observing without antibiotics risks progression to sepsis and renal damage; empiric coverage should not be delayed. Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate renal parenchymal or serum concentrations and is contraindicated for pyelonephritis, making it the wrong choice here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 48 hours of IV antibiotics she remains febrile with persistent flank pain. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge
Add an antifungal agent empirically
Obtain contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
Repeat urinalysis only and continue current management
stages.explanation: Failure to defervesce after 48-72 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics raises concern for a complication such as a perinephric or intrarenal abscess or an obstructing stone, so contrast-enhanced CT is the best next step to identify a drainable focus or obstruction. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharging a still-febrile patient ignores treatment failure and is unsafe. Empiric antifungals are not indicated without evidence of fungal infection or specific risk factors. Repeating only a urinalysis will not reveal an abscess or obstruction and does not address the lack of clinical response; imaging is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Acute pyelonephritis
pearls: Acute pyelonephritis = fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness, often with a preceding cystitis prodrome; WBC casts on urinalysis localize infection to the kidney.
Toxic-appearing patients, those vomiting, pregnant, or with comorbidities warrant admission for IV fluids and IV antibiotics.
Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin do NOT reach therapeutic renal/serum levels and should not be used for pyelonephritis.
Failure to improve within 48-72 hours should prompt CT imaging to look for abscess or obstruction.

cases.title: Getting Up Multiple Times a Night to Urinate
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening nighttime urination and a weak urinary stream over the past year.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents to clinic frustrated by urinary symptoms that have gradually worsened over the past year. He now wakes three to four times nightly to urinate and describes a weak, intermittent stream with hesitancy at the start and dribbling at the end. He feels he never fully empties his bladder and must strain at times. He denies dysuria, gross hematuria, fever, flank pain, or weight loss. He has had no acute episodes of complete inability to void. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily; he recently started an over-the-counter antihistamine for seasonal allergies. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks two cups of coffee daily, and has a remote 10 pack-year smoking history, quit 20 years ago. Family history is notable for a brother with prostate issues; no known prostate cancer. He reports no bone pain and a stable appetite. He is sexually active with mild erectile changes he attributes to age.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdominal exam is soft and non-tender with no palpable suprapubic mass and no bladder distension to percussion. There is no CVA tenderness. Genital exam shows a normal circumcised phallus and bilaterally descended, non-tender testes without masses. Digital rectal examination reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, rubbery, non-tender prostate without discrete nodules, induration, or asymmetry. Sphincter tone is normal. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities, including perineal sensation, is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond history and DRE, which initial workup is most appropriate for his lower urinary tract symptoms?
options: Urinalysis and a serum PSA, with a symptom-severity questionnaire
Immediate transrectal prostate biopsy
CT urogram of the abdomen and pelvis
Cystoscopy as the first-line test
stages.explanation: Initial evaluation of male LUTS includes a urinalysis (to exclude infection or hematuria), a serum PSA when life expectancy and shared decision-making support it, and a validated symptom score (such as the AUA/IPSS) to gauge severity and guide therapy. Immediate prostate biopsy is inappropriate without a concerning DRE or elevated PSA. CT urogram is reserved for hematuria, suspected stones, or upper-tract concerns, none of which are present. Cystoscopy is not a first-line test for uncomplicated LUTS and is reserved for hematuria, suspected stricture, or surgical planning.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: clear, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no blood
Urine WBC: 1 /hpf
PSA: 2.4 ng/mL
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL
Post-void residual (bladder scan): 90 mL
AUA Symptom Index: 19 (moderate-to-severe)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding best supports a diagnosis of benign prostatic hyperplasia rather than prostate cancer?
options: A markedly elevated PSA above 10 ng/mL
A hard, irregular nodule on the prostate
A symmetrically enlarged, smooth, rubbery prostate with a normal PSA
Microscopic hematuria on urinalysis
stages.explanation: A symmetrically enlarged, smooth, rubbery, non-tender prostate together with a normal PSA is the classic benign picture supporting BPH. A markedly elevated PSA (>10 ng/mL) would raise concern for malignancy and prompt further workup. A hard, irregular, or asymmetric nodule on DRE is suspicious for prostate cancer and is not consistent with simple hyperplasia. Microscopic hematuria is nonspecific and, if present, would itself warrant evaluation for stones or urothelial cancer rather than confirming BPH. His exam and labs together point clearly to benign disease causing bladder outlet obstruction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He wants relief from his bothersome symptoms. Which medication provides the fastest symptomatic improvement?
options: Finasteride, a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor
Tamsulosin, an alpha-1 blocker
Oxybutynin, an antimuscarinic
Continued over-the-counter antihistamine
stages.explanation: Alpha-1 blockers such as tamsulosin relax smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck and improve symptoms within days, making them the fastest-acting option for moderate-to-severe BPH. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors like finasteride shrink the prostate over months and are best for larger glands or to reduce progression, but they act slowly. Antimuscarinics like oxybutynin target overactive bladder/storage symptoms and can worsen emptying or precipitate retention in a man with obstruction and elevated residual. The antihistamine he is taking has anticholinergic properties that can aggravate his obstruction and should be stopped, not continued.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Before starting tamsulosin, which counseling point is most important?
options: It commonly causes orthostatic hypotension and dizziness; rise slowly
It will lower his PSA by about 50%
It will shrink his prostate within two weeks
It must be combined with an antimuscarinic for any benefit
stages.explanation: Alpha-1 blockers can cause orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, and (with tamsulosin) retrograde ejaculation and intraoperative floppy iris syndrome, so counseling about postural symptoms and rising slowly is most important, especially in an older man on amlodipine. Lowering PSA by roughly 50% is a property of 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride/dutasteride), not alpha-blockers. Alpha-blockers relax smooth muscle and relieve symptoms quickly but do not shrink the prostate. Combining with an antimuscarinic is not required for benefit and could risk retention in an obstructed patient, so it is not appropriate baseline advice.

cases.diagnosis: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
pearls: BPH causes obstructive (hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying) and storage (frequency, nocturia, urgency) LUTS; DRE shows a smooth, symmetrically enlarged gland.
Initial workup: urinalysis, PSA (with shared decision-making), symptom score (AUA/IPSS), and post-void residual.
Alpha-1 blockers give rapid symptom relief; 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors shrink large glands over months and lower PSA by ~50%.
Anticholinergics, antihistamines, and decongestants can worsen obstruction and precipitate acute urinary retention.

cases.title: Sudden Inability to Pass Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Eight hours of being unable to urinate with worsening lower abdominal pain.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because he has been unable to pass any urine for about eight hours and has progressively worsening, severe lower abdominal pain and a constant urge to void. He reports a long history of weak stream, nocturia, and incomplete emptying that he never had evaluated. Two days ago he developed a cold and began taking an over-the-counter combination product containing a decongestant (pseudoephedrine) and an antihistamine (diphenhydramine). He denies fever, hematuria, prior catheterization, back trauma, or saddle anesthesia, and reports no leg weakness. Past medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications are lisinopril 10 mg daily and as-needed ibuprofen. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired electrician, drinks alcohol rarely, and has a distant smoking history. Family history is noncontributory. He denies recent constipation or new neurologic symptoms. The suprapubic pain has become intolerable over the last two hours despite no further fluid intake.
cases.exam: The patient is visibly uncomfortable and restless from pain. He is afebrile but hypertensive and tachycardic, likely from distress. The lower abdomen reveals a tender, firm, palpable suprapubic mass that is dull to percussion, consistent with a distended bladder reaching near the umbilicus. There is no CVA tenderness and no abdominal rigidity or rebound. Digital rectal exam shows a diffusely enlarged, smooth, non-tender prostate; sphincter tone is normal and the rectal vault is empty. Perineal sensation is intact and lower-extremity strength is normal. No flank masses are appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Order a non-contrast CT before any intervention
Place a urethral (Foley) catheter to decompress the bladder
Give IV furosemide to increase urine output
Start empiric IV antibiotics and observe
stages.explanation: This is acute urinary retention; immediate bladder decompression with a urethral catheter is both diagnostic and therapeutic and provides rapid relief of pain and pressure. Obtaining a CT first needlessly delays decompression in an obviously distended, painful bladder. IV furosemide would increase urine production into an already obstructed, full bladder, worsening distension and pain without relieving the outflow obstruction. Empiric antibiotics are not indicated absent infection and do nothing to relieve the mechanical obstruction. After catheterization, output should be measured and the underlying cause (likely BPH plus offending medications) addressed.

stages.label: POST-CATHETER FINDINGS
stages.content:
Immediate urine drained on catheterization: 1,150 mL
Urine appearance: clear yellow, no gross blood
Urinalysis: no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no blood
Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL)
Bladder ultrasound pre-catheter: markedly distended bladder
Pain: substantially relieved after drainage

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Which factor most likely precipitated this episode of acute urinary retention?
options: His ACE inhibitor (lisinopril)
The over-the-counter decongestant-antihistamine combination
Occasional alcohol use
His osteoarthritis
stages.explanation: The over-the-counter cold product is the likely precipitant: pseudoephedrine (an alpha-agonist) increases bladder-neck and prostatic smooth-muscle tone, and diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) impairs detrusor contraction, together tipping a man with underlying BPH into retention. Lisinopril does not cause urinary retention. Occasional alcohol may transiently affect voiding but is not the driver here and was not recently increased. Osteoarthritis itself does not cause retention, though analgesics (especially opioids, not his NSAID) can. Recognizing and stopping the offending agents is central to preventing recurrence.

stages.label: POST-OBSTRUCTIVE CARE
stages.question: After draining over 1 liter, which complication must you monitor for in the hours that follow?
options: Post-obstructive diuresis with volume depletion and electrolyte disturbance
Immediate acute pulmonary edema from the catheter
Rapid rebound oliguria requiring dialysis
Hyperkalemia from the bladder decompression
stages.explanation: After relief of a chronically obstructed, high-volume bladder, patients can develop post-obstructive diuresis, producing large urine volumes that may cause hypovolemia and electrolyte derangements, so monitoring urine output, vitals, and electrolytes with judicious fluid replacement is essential. Acute pulmonary edema is not a direct consequence of catheter placement. Rebound oliguria requiring dialysis is not the expected pattern; his creatinine bump should improve with decompression. Bladder decompression itself does not cause hyperkalemia; if anything, brisk diuresis can lead to potassium loss. Diuresis is defined as >200 mL/hr for consecutive hours.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His pain resolves and diuresis is mild. What is the most appropriate discharge plan?
options: Remove catheter and discharge with no further therapy
Discharge with the catheter in place, start an alpha-blocker, and arrange urology follow-up for a voiding trial
Schedule immediate transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) tonight
Resume the decongestant-antihistamine product and follow up as needed
stages.explanation: Standard care after a first episode of BPH-related retention is to discharge with an indwelling catheter (or perform a voiding trial), start an alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin to improve the chance of successful voiding, and arrange urology follow-up for a trial without catheter. Removing the catheter and discharging with no therapy risks immediate re-retention. Emergent TURP is not indicated for a first uncomplicated episode and is an elective decision after evaluation. Resuming the offending decongestant-antihistamine would predictably cause recurrence and must be avoided; he should be counseled to stop these agents.

cases.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention
pearls: Acute urinary retention presents with sudden inability to void, suprapubic pain, and a palpable, percussion-dull bladder; immediate catheterization is diagnostic and therapeutic.
Common precipitants in men with BPH: anticholinergics, antihistamines, decongestants (alpha-agonists), opioids, and recent surgery.
Watch for post-obstructive diuresis (>200 mL/hr) after draining a large-volume bladder; monitor electrolytes and volume status.
Start an alpha-blocker before a trial without catheter to improve the likelihood of successful voiding.

cases.title: Severe Wave-Like Flank Pain Radiating to the Groin
cases.chiefComplaint: Three hours of sudden, severe right flank pain radiating to the groin with blood in the urine.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with three hours of abrupt-onset, severe right flank pain that comes in waves and radiates down toward his right groin and testicle. He is restless and cannot find a comfortable position, pacing and writhing on the stretcher. He has had two episodes of vomiting from the pain and noticed pink-tinged urine. He denies fever, dysuria initially, though now reports some urinary urgency, and has no prior similar episodes. He had one kidney stone in his twenties that passed spontaneously. Past medical history includes obesity and gout. Medications are allopurinol 100 mg daily, which he admits he takes inconsistently. He has no known drug allergies. He works in construction in a hot climate and acknowledges he drinks little water during shifts. He consumes red meat frequently and several sodas daily. He does not smoke and drinks beer on weekends. Family history includes a father with kidney stones. He reports no flank trauma and no recent illness.
cases.exam: The patient is in obvious distress, writhing and unable to lie still, in contrast to the guarding seen with peritonitis. He is afebrile and mildly hypertensive and tachycardic from pain. Abdomen is soft with mild right-sided tenderness but no rebound or guarding; bowel sounds are present. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness. The right testicle is normal in size and lie with no tenderness, swelling, or erythema, and the cremasteric reflex is intact bilaterally. There is no inguinal hernia or lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which imaging study is the gold standard to confirm a suspected ureteral stone?
options: Non-contrast helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis
Abdominal plain radiograph (KUB)
Contrast-enhanced CT urogram
Renal MRI
stages.explanation: Non-contrast helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the gold standard for diagnosing nephrolithiasis; it detects nearly all stones regardless of composition, defines size and location, and reveals hydronephrosis without contrast risk. A plain KUB radiograph misses radiolucent (uric acid) stones and small calculi and lacks sensitivity. A contrast CT urogram adds radiation and contrast and can obscure stones against contrast in the collecting system, so it is not first-line for acute colic. MRI is poor at detecting calcifications and is generally reserved for pregnant patients (where ultrasound is first-line) or when CT is contraindicated.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: RBC 50 /hpf, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase
Urine pH: 5.2
Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL
WBC: 8.4 ×10³/µL
Non-contrast CT: 5 mm obstructing calculus at the right distal ureter (ureterovesical junction)
Findings: mild right hydronephrosis and hydroureter
No perinephric abscess or free fluid

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 5 mm distal ureteral stone in a stable, afebrile patient, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Emergent ureteroscopy with stone extraction tonight
Conservative management: analgesia, antiemetics, hydration, and an alpha-blocker for medical expulsive therapy
Immediate shock wave lithotripsy in the ED
Percutaneous nephrostomy tube placement
stages.explanation: A 5 mm distal ureteral stone in a hemodynamically stable, afebrile patient with preserved renal function has a high likelihood of spontaneous passage, so conservative management with analgesia (NSAIDs unless contraindicated, plus opioids as needed), antiemetics, hydration, and an alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) for medical expulsive therapy is appropriate. Emergent ureteroscopy is reserved for refractory pain, infection, or failed passage, not first-line for a passable stone. Shock wave lithotripsy is an elective procedure, not an ED intervention. Percutaneous nephrostomy is for obstruction with infection (urosepsis) or a solitary/transplanted kidney, none of which apply here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which clinical change would make this an emergency requiring urgent urologic decompression?
options: Development of fever, rigors, and a rising WBC (obstruction with infection)
Mild persistent microscopic hematuria
Resolution of pain after analgesia
A urine pH that remains acidic
stages.explanation: An obstructing stone combined with infection (fever, rigors, leukocytosis, pyuria) constitutes obstructive pyelonephritis/urosepsis, a urologic emergency requiring urgent decompression with a ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy plus antibiotics. Mild persistent microscopic hematuria is expected with a stone and is not an emergency. Resolution of pain after analgesia is reassuring, not alarming, and may even indicate stone passage. An acidic urine pH is consistent with uric acid stone formation and is relevant for prevention but is not itself an emergency. Recognizing infected obstruction promptly is critical because it can rapidly progress to septic shock.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: He passes the stone, and analysis shows uric acid composition. Which prevention strategy is most appropriate?
options: Restrict dietary calcium aggressively
Increase fluid intake, reduce purine-rich foods, and alkalinize the urine (e.g., potassium citrate)
Start a thiazide diuretic as the primary therapy
Begin a high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet
stages.explanation: Uric acid stones form in persistently acidic, concentrated urine, so prevention centers on increasing fluid intake to dilute the urine, reducing purine-rich foods (red meat, organ meats, seafood), and alkalinizing the urine with potassium citrate to raise pH above ~6.5; optimizing gout/allopurinol adherence also helps. Aggressive calcium restriction is counterproductive and is not the strategy for uric acid stones (and even paradoxically raises oxalate absorption). Thiazides reduce urinary calcium and are used for calcium stones with hypercalciuria, not uric acid stones. A high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet increases acid load and uric acid excretion, worsening stone risk.

cases.diagnosis: Nephrolithiasis (obstructing ureteral stone)
pearls: Renal colic is classically severe, colicky flank-to-groin pain with hematuria and a patient who cannot lie still; non-contrast CT is the diagnostic gold standard.
Stones <5 mm usually pass spontaneously; offer medical expulsive therapy with an alpha-blocker for distal stones up to ~10 mm.
Obstruction PLUS infection (fever, leukocytosis, pyuria) is a urologic emergency needing urgent decompression (stent or nephrostomy) and antibiotics.
Uric acid stones are radiolucent on plain film, form in acidic urine, and are prevented with hydration, reduced purines, and urinary alkalinization.

cases.title: Gradually Worsening Scrotal Pain and Swelling
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of gradually worsening left scrotal pain and swelling.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man presents to clinic with three days of gradually worsening left scrotal pain and swelling. The pain began as a dull ache and has slowly intensified; he also notes mild dysuria and some clear urethral discharge over the past few days. He denies sudden severe onset, nausea, vomiting, fever, or trauma. He is sexually active with multiple female partners and reports inconsistent condom use; his last unprotected encounter was about two weeks ago. He has no prior urologic problems. Past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no daily medications. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He reports that the pain is somewhat improved when he supports or elevates the scrotum. He has not noticed blood in the urine or semen and denies penile lesions. He has had no recent urinary instrumentation and no symptoms of urinary obstruction.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and in no acute distress. The left hemiscrotum is erythematous and mildly swollen. There is focal tenderness localized to the posterolateral aspect of the left testis where the epididymis is markedly tender and indurated; the testis itself is in a normal vertical lie and is less tender than the epididymis. Elevation of the affected testis relieves the pain (positive Prehn sign). The cremasteric reflex is intact bilaterally. There is no high-riding testis. A small amount of clear urethral discharge is expressible. The right hemiscrotum is normal, and there is no inguinal hernia or significant lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most helps distinguish epididymitis from testicular torsion in this patient?
options: Presence of scrotal erythema
Intact cremasteric reflex with relief of pain on testicular elevation
Mild dysuria
Tenderness anywhere in the scrotum
stages.explanation: An intact cremasteric reflex together with relief of pain on testicular elevation (positive Prehn sign) and a normal testicular lie favors epididymitis over torsion. Scrotal erythema occurs in both conditions and does not discriminate. Mild dysuria suggests an associated urethritis/UTI but is not specific for distinguishing the two. Generalized scrotal tenderness is nonspecific; what matters is that the epididymis is the point of maximal tenderness in epididymitis, whereas torsion causes a high-riding, transversely oriented, diffusely exquisitely tender testis with an ABSENT cremasteric reflex. When doubt remains, urgent Doppler ultrasound is indicated to rule out torsion.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Urinalysis: leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites negative
Urine WBC: 20 /hpf
Urethral/NAAT: pending for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis
Scrotal Doppler ultrasound: increased blood flow to the left epididymis (hyperemia)
Testicular blood flow: present and symmetric bilaterally
No torsion; no abscess

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his age, sexual history, and findings, what is the most likely cause of his epididymitis?
options: Enteric organisms (E. coli) from urinary tract obstruction
Sexually transmitted Chlamydia trachomatis and/or Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Tuberculous epididymitis
Mumps orchitis
stages.explanation: In a sexually active man under 35 with urethral discharge and risk factors, sexually transmitted Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are the most likely pathogens causing epididymitis. Enteric organisms such as E. coli predominate in older men, those with bladder outlet obstruction, or after instrumentation, which does not fit this young patient. Tuberculous epididymitis is rare and usually chronic with a 'beaded' vas deferens, not an acute presentation. Mumps orchitis affects the testis (not primarily the epididymis), typically follows parotitis, and would be unusual in a vaccinated adult; his localized epididymal tenderness and urethritis point clearly to an STI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen for likely STI-related epididymitis in this man?
options: Oral fluoroquinolone alone for 10 days
Intramuscular ceftriaxone plus oral doxycycline
Oral nitrofurantoin for 7 days
Topical antibiotics and scrotal support only
stages.explanation: For sexually transmitted epididymitis, empiric therapy covers both gonorrhea and chlamydia: a single intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone plus a course of oral doxycycline, along with scrotal elevation, NSAIDs, and partner treatment with abstinence until both are treated. A fluoroquinolone alone does not reliably cover gonorrhea given resistance and is reserved for enteric-organism cases (men >35 or insertive anal intercourse, often combined appropriately). Nitrofurantoin does not treat STIs and does not penetrate the epididymis adequately. Topical antibiotics and scrotal support alone are inadequate for a bacterial STI and would risk progression, abscess, or infertility.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which additional measure is most important to include in his management?
options: Recommend immediate orchiectomy to prevent complications
Counsel on partner notification/treatment and offer testing for other STIs including HIV and syphilis
Advise against any scrotal support or rest
Schedule routine annual scrotal ultrasounds for life
stages.explanation: Because this is a sexually transmitted infection, partner notification and treatment, abstinence until therapy is complete, and screening for coexisting STIs (HIV, syphilis, and others) are essential to prevent reinfection and identify concurrent infections. Orchiectomy is not indicated for uncomplicated epididymitis; surgery is reserved for abscess or refractory cases. Scrotal support, rest, ice, and NSAIDs are beneficial supportive measures, so advising against them is wrong. Routine lifelong scrotal ultrasounds are unnecessary; follow-up is clinical, and persistent symptoms or a palpable mass after treatment would prompt re-evaluation for other pathology.

cases.diagnosis: Epididymitis
pearls: Epididymitis presents with gradual-onset, posterolateral scrotal pain, a positive Prehn sign, and an INTACT cremasteric reflex - features that help separate it from torsion.
Men <35: think STI (chlamydia, gonorrhea) - treat with ceftriaxone IM plus doxycycline; men >35 or obstruction: think enteric organisms (E. coli) - treat with a fluoroquinolone.
Any uncertainty about torsion mandates urgent color Doppler ultrasound - torsion is a surgical emergency.
Always treat partners, screen for other STIs, and add supportive care (scrotal elevation, NSAIDs).

cases.title: Sudden Severe One-Sided Testicle Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Two hours of sudden, severe left testicular pain with nausea and vomiting.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother with two hours of abrupt, severe left testicular and lower abdominal pain that woke him from sleep. The pain was maximal at onset and is associated with nausea and one episode of vomiting. He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, fever, or trauma, though he recalls a similar but milder episode last month that resolved on its own within an hour. He is not sexually active. Past medical history is unremarkable, he takes no medications, and he has no known drug allergies. He is a high-school student and plays soccer. Family history is noncontributory. He reports the pain has not improved with lying still and is not relieved by any position. There is no abdominal trauma, no recent illness, and no prior surgeries. His mother notes the scrotum looks swollen and he is reluctant to let anyone touch it. Time of onset was approximately two and a half hours ago.
cases.exam: The patient is in significant distress, pale, and diaphoretic from pain. The left hemiscrotum is swollen and erythematous. The left testis is exquisitely tender, high-riding, and lies in a horizontal (transverse) orientation compared with the normally positioned right testis. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left and intact on the right. Elevation of the testis does not relieve the pain (negative Prehn sign). There is no urethral discharge. The abdomen is soft with no peritoneal signs, and there is no inguinal hernia. The right testis and epididymis are normal and non-tender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the classic presentation, what is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Obtain a scrotal Doppler ultrasound and wait for results before any consult
Immediately consult urology for emergent surgical exploration
Start IV antibiotics for presumed epididymitis
Order a urinalysis and discharge with urology follow-up
stages.explanation: This is a textbook testicular torsion (sudden severe pain, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign) in a young male; the testis is most salvageable within ~6 hours, so immediate urology consultation for emergent surgical exploration is the priority - clinical diagnosis should not be delayed. Waiting on ultrasound when the diagnosis is clinically obvious wastes the salvage window; imaging is for equivocal cases. IV antibiotics treat epididymitis, which is contradicted by the absent cremasteric reflex and high-riding testis. Discharging with follow-up would risk irreversible testicular necrosis and is unacceptable for a surgical emergency.

stages.label: DOPPLER ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Color Doppler ultrasound (obtained without delaying surgical consult):
Left testis: absent intratesticular arterial blood flow
Left testis: heterogeneous echotexture, enlarged
Right testis: normal symmetric blood flow
'Whirlpool sign' of the spermatic cord: present on the left
No hydrocele or abscess

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While the operating room is being prepared, what bedside maneuver may be attempted, and what is the definitive treatment?
options: Manual detorsion as a temporizing measure; definitive treatment is surgical detorsion with bilateral orchiopexy
Apply ice and discharge once pain improves
Manual detorsion only, with no need for surgery if pain resolves
Immediate orchiectomy of both testes
stages.explanation: Manual detorsion (often 'opening the book,' rotating the testis outward/laterally) can be attempted at the bedside as a temporizing measure to restore flow, but definitive treatment is surgical exploration with detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy - the contralateral testis is fixed because the underlying anatomic 'bell-clapper' deformity is usually bilateral. Ice and discharge would allow necrosis and is wrong. Manual detorsion alone is never definitive because retorsion is likely without fixation, so surgery is still required. Bilateral orchiectomy is inappropriate; the goal is salvage, and orchiectomy is reserved only for a clearly nonviable testis found at exploration.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor most strongly determines whether the affected testis can be salvaged?
options: The patient's age in years
The duration of symptoms before detorsion
Whether the patient vomited
The presence of scrotal erythema
stages.explanation: Salvage depends primarily on the duration of ischemia: detorsion within about 6 hours yields high salvage rates (>90%), while delays beyond 12-24 hours markedly reduce viability, making time-to-detorsion the dominant determinant of outcome. Age, while relevant epidemiologically (torsion peaks in neonates and adolescents), does not by itself dictate salvage for a given event. Vomiting reflects pain severity and autonomic response but does not predict testicular viability. Scrotal erythema is a nonspecific sign present in many scrotal conditions and does not gauge salvageability. This is why torsion is a clinical emergency where rapid action saves the testis.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Why is the contralateral (right) testis surgically fixed even though it is asymptomatic?
options: To biopsy it for occult cancer
Because the predisposing anatomic deformity is usually bilateral, putting it at risk of future torsion
To improve future fertility directly
Because antibiotics cannot reach it otherwise
stages.explanation: The contralateral testis is fixed (orchiopexy) because the bell-clapper deformity - inadequate posterior fixation of the testis within the tunica vaginalis - is typically bilateral, so the unaffected side is also predisposed to torsion and should be secured prophylactically. Fixation is not performed to biopsy for cancer; torsion is unrelated to a cancer screening indication. Orchiopexy prevents future torsion but does not directly 'improve fertility'; preserving the testes does protect overall reproductive potential indirectly. Antibiotic delivery is irrelevant here because torsion is an ischemic, not infectious, process. Bilateral fixation is standard of care at the time of exploration.

cases.diagnosis: Testicular torsion
pearls: Testicular torsion is a clinical surgical emergency: sudden severe pain, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign - do NOT delay surgery for imaging when the diagnosis is clear.
The salvage window is ~6 hours; viability falls sharply after 12-24 hours, so time-to-detorsion drives prognosis.
Definitive treatment is surgical detorsion with BILATERAL orchiopexy because the bell-clapper deformity is usually bilateral.
Color Doppler ultrasound (absent intratesticular flow, 'whirlpool sign') is for equivocal cases and should never delay operative management.

cases.title: Abnormal Screening Blood Test in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for an elevated PSA found on routine screening.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man is referred to urology after a routine screening serum PSA returned elevated at 6.8 ng/mL (prior value two years ago was 1.9 ng/mL). He is asymptomatic, denying urinary obstruction, hematuria, bone pain, or weight loss. He has no recent urinary infection, ejaculation within 48 hours, or vigorous bicycle riding that might transiently raise PSA, and he has never had a prostate biopsy or catheterization recently. Past medical history includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and losartan 50 mg daily; he takes no 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired teacher, never smoked, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is significant: his father was diagnosed with prostate cancer at age 70, and a brother has prostate cancer. He is of African American ancestry. He has a good functional status and a life expectancy estimated at well over 10 years. He reports normal erectile and bladder function.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no palpable bladder or masses. There is no CVA tenderness. Genital exam is normal with bilaterally descended, non-tender testes and no masses. Digital rectal examination reveals a firm, discrete, hard nodule on the right lobe of the prostate; the gland is otherwise mildly enlarged. The remainder of the prostate is rubbery without induration. There is no perirectal mass and sphincter tone is normal. There is no lower-extremity edema, and the spine is non-tender to palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the rising PSA and a palpable hard nodule, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat the PSA in one year and reassure him
Multiparametric prostate MRI and/or prostate biopsy to obtain tissue diagnosis
Start an alpha-blocker and reassess symptoms
Begin androgen-deprivation therapy empirically
stages.explanation: An elevated, rising PSA combined with a hard, discrete nodule on DRE is suspicious for prostate cancer, so a tissue diagnosis is needed; multiparametric MRI (to localize/target) followed by prostate biopsy is the appropriate next step. Simply repeating PSA in a year and reassuring him is inappropriate when an abnormal DRE and significant PSA rise are already present. An alpha-blocker treats BPH symptoms and does nothing to evaluate a suspicious nodule. Starting androgen-deprivation therapy empirically is wrong because treatment should never precede a histologic diagnosis and staging; ADT also carries significant morbidity and is not first-line for localized disease.

stages.label: BIOPSY & STAGING
stages.content:
Multiparametric prostate MRI: PI-RADS 5 lesion in the right peripheral zone, no extracapsular extension
TRUS-guided biopsy: adenocarcinoma in 4 of 12 cores, right lobe
Gleason score: 4+3 = 7 (Grade Group 3)
PSA: 6.8 ng/mL
Clinical stage: cT2a
Bone scan and CT: no evidence of metastasis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which scoring system is used to grade the aggressiveness of his prostate adenocarcinoma?
options: The TNM stage alone
The Gleason score (Grade Group)
The AUA symptom index
The PI-RADS score
stages.explanation: The Gleason score, now reported as Grade Groups, grades prostate adenocarcinoma based on histologic architecture (the two most prevalent patterns), reflecting tumor aggressiveness and guiding prognosis and treatment. TNM stage describes anatomic extent (local, nodal, metastatic) but is not the grading system, though both are used together for risk stratification. The AUA symptom index quantifies lower urinary tract symptom severity in BPH, unrelated to cancer grade. PI-RADS scores the likelihood of clinically significant cancer on MRI imaging - it guides biopsy decisions but does not grade the actual tissue. His Gleason 4+3=7 (Grade Group 3) indicates intermediate-to-higher-risk disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has localized, intermediate-risk prostate cancer (Grade Group 3, cT2a) and a life expectancy >10 years. Which is an appropriate definitive treatment option?
options: Active surveillance with serial PSA and biopsies only
Radical prostatectomy or radiation therapy (definitive local treatment)
Watchful waiting with no monitoring
Chemotherapy with docetaxel as first-line
stages.explanation: For localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer in a man with >10-year life expectancy, definitive local therapy - radical prostatectomy or radiation therapy (often with short-course ADT for higher intermediate risk) - is appropriate and offers cure. Active surveillance is generally reserved for low-risk (Grade Group 1) and select favorable intermediate-risk disease, not Grade Group 3, where deferring treatment risks progression. Watchful waiting (treating only symptoms, no curative intent) is for men with limited life expectancy or significant comorbidity, not this healthy patient. Chemotherapy such as docetaxel is used for metastatic/castration-resistant disease, not as first-line therapy for localized cancer.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He chooses radical prostatectomy and asks about expected side effects. Which two are most important to counsel about?
options: Hearing loss and hyperthyroidism
Erectile dysfunction and urinary incontinence
Permanent hyperglycemia and blindness
Hair loss and severe diarrhea
stages.explanation: The two most important and common adverse effects of radical prostatectomy are erectile dysfunction (from injury to the neurovascular bundles) and urinary incontinence (from disruption of the sphincteric mechanism); nerve-sparing technique and pelvic floor rehabilitation can mitigate these. Hearing loss and hyperthyroidism are unrelated to prostate surgery. Permanent hyperglycemia and blindness are not expected surgical complications; his diabetes is a separate comorbidity. Hair loss and severe diarrhea are typical of cytotoxic chemotherapy, not prostatectomy. Honest counseling about ED and incontinence is essential for informed decision-making and realistic expectations.

cases.diagnosis: Prostate cancer (adenocarcinoma)
pearls: An elevated/rising PSA plus a hard, discrete nodule on DRE is suspicious for prostate cancer and warrants MRI and biopsy for tissue diagnosis.
The Gleason score (Grade Group) grades histologic aggressiveness; combine with PSA and stage for risk stratification.
Localized intermediate/high-risk disease in men with >10-year life expectancy is treated definitively with radical prostatectomy or radiation; active surveillance suits low-risk disease.
Key complications of radical prostatectomy are erectile dysfunction and urinary incontinence; prostate cancer classically metastasizes to bone (osteoblastic lesions).

cases.title: Painless Blood in the Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Two episodes of painless, visibly bloody urine over the past week.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to clinic after noticing his urine turned bright red twice over the past week. The bleeding is painless, occurred throughout the urinary stream, and he passed a few small clots. He denies dysuria, frequency, urgency, fever, flank pain, or recent trauma, and has had no recent vigorous exercise. He notes no weight loss or bone pain. Past medical history includes hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and a tiotropium inhaler; he takes no anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He is a current smoker with a 45 pack-year history and worked for many years in a job involving aniline dye and rubber processing. He drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior episodes of gross hematuria and no history of kidney stones or urinary infections. He reports his urinary stream is otherwise normal without obstructive symptoms, and the bleeding has now stopped.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no palpable masses, and there is no suprapubic fullness or palpable bladder. There is no CVA tenderness bilaterally. Genital exam is unremarkable with no penile lesions and bilaterally descended, non-tender testes. Digital rectal examination reveals a mildly enlarged, smooth, non-tender prostate without discrete nodules. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no lower-extremity edema. Lungs reveal mildly prolonged expiration but are otherwise clear. There is no flank mass.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After confirming hematuria on urinalysis and excluding infection, what is the most appropriate workup for painless gross hematuria in this smoker?
options: Reassurance and repeat urinalysis in 6 months
CT urography plus cystoscopy
Renal ultrasound alone
Empiric antibiotics for presumed UTI
stages.explanation: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker is bladder cancer until proven otherwise; the standard evaluation is upper-tract imaging with CT urography PLUS cystoscopy to directly visualize the bladder and urethra. Reassurance and delayed repeat testing would dangerously postpone diagnosis of a potential malignancy. Renal ultrasound alone is insufficient because it does not reliably evaluate the urothelium of the bladder or ureters, and cystoscopy is required to see the bladder mucosa. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate when there is no evidence of infection and would delay cancer workup; gross painless hematuria mandates a full hematuria evaluation.

stages.label: IMAGING & CYSTOSCOPY
stages.content:
Urinalysis: RBC too numerous to count, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites
Urine culture: no growth
Urine cytology: atypical/malignant urothelial cells present
CT urography: bladder wall thickening with a polypoid mass on the right lateral wall; upper tracts normal
Cystoscopy: 2.5 cm papillary, frond-like tumor on the right lateral bladder wall
No hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which procedure both diagnoses (provides tissue and staging information) and can treat the visualized bladder tumor?
options: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT)
Percutaneous renal biopsy
Radical cystectomy as the first step
Repeat urine cytology only
stages.explanation: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) is both diagnostic and therapeutic: it removes the visible tumor, provides tissue for histologic grading, and - by sampling the muscularis propria - determines whether the tumor invades muscle, which drives staging and subsequent management. Percutaneous renal biopsy targets the kidney, not a bladder tumor. Radical cystectomy is a major operation reserved for muscle-invasive disease and is not the initial diagnostic step; you must first stage with TURBT. Repeat urine cytology adds nothing definitive when a mass is already seen and tissue is required for diagnosis, grade, and depth of invasion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: TURBT shows high-grade urothelial carcinoma confined to the lamina propria without muscle invasion (non-muscle-invasive). What is the most appropriate adjuvant therapy?
options: Immediate radical cystectomy
Intravesical BCG (bacillus Calmette-Guerin) immunotherapy
Systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone
Pelvic radiation therapy
stages.explanation: For high-grade non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, intravesical BCG immunotherapy after complete TURBT reduces recurrence and progression and is the standard adjuvant treatment, with ongoing surveillance cystoscopy. Immediate radical cystectomy is reserved for muscle-invasive disease or BCG-refractory high-risk cases, so it is overtreatment here as a first step. Systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy is used as neoadjuvant therapy for muscle-invasive disease, not for non-muscle-invasive tumors. Pelvic radiation (as part of bladder-preservation/chemoradiation) is an option for select muscle-invasive disease, not for non-muscle-invasive cancer. Recognizing depth of invasion is the pivotal branch point in management.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which of his exposures is the single most important modifiable risk factor for his bladder cancer?
options: His tiotropium inhaler
Cigarette smoking
Occasional alcohol use
His amlodipine
stages.explanation: Cigarette smoking is the single most important and most common modifiable risk factor for urothelial (bladder) cancer, accounting for roughly half of cases, so smoking cessation is critical for prognosis and reducing recurrence. Occupational exposure to aromatic amines (aniline dyes, rubber) is also a well-established contributor in his history but smoking carries the largest attributable risk overall. The tiotropium inhaler and amlodipine are not bladder carcinogens. Occasional alcohol is not a recognized major risk factor for bladder cancer. Counseling and supporting smoking cessation is an essential, high-yield intervention in any patient with urothelial carcinoma.

cases.diagnosis: Bladder cancer (urothelial carcinoma)
pearls: Painless gross hematuria in an older adult, especially a smoker, is bladder cancer until proven otherwise and requires CT urography PLUS cystoscopy.
Smoking is the leading modifiable risk factor; occupational aromatic amines (aniline dyes, rubber, leather) are classic exposures.
TURBT is both diagnostic and therapeutic and must sample muscularis propria to determine muscle invasion - the key staging branch point.
Non-muscle-invasive high-grade tumors are treated with intravesical BCG; muscle-invasive disease warrants neoadjuvant chemotherapy and radical cystectomy.

cases.title: Difficulty With Sexual Performance
cases.chiefComplaint: About a year of gradually worsening difficulty achieving and maintaining erections.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to clinic, somewhat reluctantly, with about a year of gradually worsening difficulty achieving and maintaining erections sufficient for intercourse. The onset was insidious and progressive rather than sudden, and he reports diminished morning and nocturnal erections as well. He denies loss of libido, relationship conflict, or new psychological stressors, and the problem occurs across different situations. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 8.4%), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily; he is on no nitrates. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke, drinks two to three beers most evenings, and is sedentary with a BMI of 32. Family history includes a father with myocardial infarction at age 58. He reports mild exertional calf discomfort when walking. He has had no pelvic surgery or trauma.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight and in no distress. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs; peripheral exam shows diminished dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally and a faint femoral bruit. Abdomen is soft and non-tender without bruits over the aorta. Genital exam shows a normal circumcised phallus without plaques or curvature (no Peyronie disease) and bilaterally descended, normal-sized, non-tender testes without masses. There is no gynecomastia. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, non-tender prostate. Neurologic exam shows intact perineal sensation and normal lower-extremity strength with slightly reduced distal sensation to monofilament.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of his history most strongly suggests an organic (rather than purely psychogenic) cause of erectile dysfunction?
options: Sudden onset tied to a specific stressful event
Gradual, progressive onset with loss of nocturnal/morning erections in all situations
Preserved spontaneous erections with selective failure
Onset only with a new partner
stages.explanation: A gradual, progressive course with loss of nocturnal and morning erections occurring across all situations points to an organic etiology (vascular, neurogenic, endocrine), since the absence of spontaneous erections argues against a primarily psychogenic mechanism. Sudden onset tied to a specific stressor, preserved spontaneous/nocturnal erections with situational failure, and onset only with a new partner all suggest a psychogenic cause, where erections are intact in some contexts. In this patient with diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, smoking, and peripheral arterial findings, vasculogenic ED is most likely, and ED can be an early warning sign of systemic atherosclerosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Fasting glucose: 172 mg/dL
HbA1c: 8.4%
Total cholesterol: 232 mg/dL
LDL: 152 mg/dL
HDL: 34 mg/dL
Morning total testosterone: 410 ng/dL (normal)
TSH: 2.1 µIU/mL (normal)
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his risk factors and exam, his erectile dysfunction is best regarded as an early marker of which underlying process?
options: Primary hypogonadism
Systemic endothelial dysfunction/cardiovascular disease
Hypothyroidism
Benign prostatic hyperplasia
stages.explanation: Vasculogenic ED reflects systemic endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis; because the penile arteries are small, they often manifest disease earlier than the coronary arteries, so ED can be a sentinel marker of cardiovascular disease and warrants cardiovascular risk assessment. Primary hypogonadism is excluded by his normal morning testosterone. Hypothyroidism is excluded by a normal TSH. Benign prostatic hyperplasia causes voiding symptoms, not erectile dysfunction directly, though they often coexist in aging men. Recognizing ED as a cardiovascular red flag - here with diabetes, dyslipidemia, smoking, diminished pulses, and claudication - is the key teaching point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy, and what critical contraindication must be confirmed first?
options: Intracavernosal alprostadil injection; confirm no penile implant
A PDE5 inhibitor such as sildenafil; confirm he is not taking nitrates
Testosterone replacement; confirm normal PSA
A vacuum erection device; confirm no latex allergy
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for ED is a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor (e.g., sildenafil, tadalafil), and the critical contraindication to confirm is concurrent nitrate use, because co-administration can cause life-threatening hypotension; he is not on nitrates, so a PDE5 inhibitor is appropriate alongside risk-factor modification. Intracavernosal alprostadil is a second-line option, not first-line. Testosterone replacement is only indicated for documented hypogonadism, which he does not have (normal testosterone), and would not address his vasculogenic ED. A vacuum erection device is a reasonable non-pharmacologic option but is not the first-line pharmacologic therapy, and latex allergy is not the key safety concern here.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond medication, which intervention is most important for both his ED and his overall cardiovascular health?
options: Aggressive lifestyle modification: smoking cessation, glycemic and lipid control, weight loss, and exercise
Increasing his daily alcohol intake to relax
Avoiding all physical activity to prevent cardiac strain
Starting nitrate therapy preventively
stages.explanation: Aggressive risk-factor modification - smoking cessation, optimizing glycemic control (his HbA1c is 8.4%), lipid management, weight loss, and regular exercise - improves both erectile function and cardiovascular outcomes and is the most important intervention beyond pharmacotherapy. Increasing alcohol intake worsens ED and cardiovascular risk. Avoiding all physical activity is harmful; appropriately graded exercise improves endothelial function and reduces cardiovascular risk. Starting nitrates preventively is dangerous and contraindicated, especially alongside a PDE5 inhibitor, and there is no indication for nitrates here. Treating ED is also an opportunity to screen for and manage occult cardiovascular disease.

cases.diagnosis: Erectile dysfunction (vasculogenic)
pearls: Gradual, progressive ED with loss of nocturnal/morning erections across all situations suggests an organic (often vascular) cause; situational ED with preserved spontaneous erections suggests psychogenic.
ED is a sentinel marker of endothelial dysfunction and can precede coronary disease - use it to screen for and modify cardiovascular risk.
Check morning testosterone and TSH; reserve testosterone replacement for documented hypogonadism.
PDE5 inhibitors are first-line but are absolutely contraindicated with nitrates (risk of severe hypotension); lifestyle modification benefits both ED and the heart.

cases.title: Persistent Left Flank Pain and Blood in the Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of intermittent left flank pain and visible blood in the urine, worse with standing and exertion.
cases.history: A 25-year-old woman presents with 6 months of dull, intermittent left flank pain that radiates toward the left lower quadrant and is aggravated by prolonged standing, walking, and exercise. She also reports episodes of painless gross hematuria, sometimes with small clots, and a recent dull left pelvic ache during her menstrual periods. She denies dysuria, urgency, frequency, fever, or weight loss. Three prior urine cultures were negative, and a course of empiric antibiotics did not help. She has unintentionally lost about 4 kg over the past year and has a slim, asthenic build. PMH is unremarkable; she takes no chronic medications and uses only occasional ibuprofen 400 mg. No known drug allergies. She is a graduate student, nonsmoker, drinks alcohol socially, and is sexually active with one partner using condoms. Family history is notable for nephrolithiasis in her father but no glomerular disease, sickle cell trait, or malignancy. Menses are regular. She has no recent trauma, travel, or new exposures.
cases.exam: Thin, well-appearing woman in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. There is mild left costovertebral angle tenderness to deep percussion but no flank ecchymosis or bruit on auscultation. No suprapubic tenderness. No peripheral edema, rash, or palpable purpura. No left-sided varicocele equivalent or pelvic varicosities appreciated on limited pelvic inspection; external genitalia normal. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. There is no lower-extremity edema and pulses are intact. Reproducible discomfort is noted when she stands during the encounter, consistent with her positional symptoms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test best confirms the hematuria and helps direct further workup?
options: Urine dipstick alone, treating any blood as definitive hematuria
Urinalysis with microscopy to confirm and characterize red cells (dysmorphic vs. isomorphic) plus a urine culture
Immediate CT angiography of the abdomen
24-hour urine protein collection before any other test
stages.explanation: Urinalysis with microscopy is the correct first step: it confirms true hematuria, quantifies RBCs, and distinguishes dysmorphic (glomerular) from isomorphic (non-glomerular) red cells, which steers the workup. A culture excludes infection given her recurrent symptoms. Dipstick alone is wrong because it cannot distinguish hemoglobin/myoglobin from intact RBCs and gives false positives. Immediate CT angiography is premature and exposes a young woman to radiation before basic confirmation of hematuria and its source. A 24-hour protein collection targets glomerular disease and is not the appropriate first move when red cells appear isomorphic and symptoms are positional and unilateral.
stages.secondary: Her presentation (positional pain, unilateral hematuria, slim build) points toward a non-glomerular, anatomic cause rather than glomerulonephritis.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, 0 protein, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites
Urine microscopy: 30-40 isomorphic RBCs/hpf, no casts, no dysmorphic cells
Urine culture: no growth at 48 h
Urine cytology: negative for malignant cells
CBC: Hgb 12.6 g/dL, WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL, Plt 248 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²
Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.08 (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given isomorphic hematuria without proteinuria or infection and positional left flank pain, which imaging study is most appropriate to evaluate the suspected cause?
options: Renal Doppler ultrasound, with CT or MR venography if needed to assess the left renal vein
Non-contrast CT of the abdomen/pelvis (stone protocol) only
Renal biopsy to evaluate for IgA nephropathy
Voiding cystourethrogram to assess for reflux
stages.explanation: Doppler ultrasound is the appropriate noninvasive first imaging study to assess the left renal vein as it crosses between the aorta and superior mesenteric artery; it can show a high peak-velocity ratio at the compression point and a dilated hilar segment, with CT/MR venography confirming the aortomesenteric angle and any collaterals. A non-contrast stone CT would miss a vascular compression and is aimed at calculi, which are not suggested here. Renal biopsy is inappropriate because the urine is isomorphic and non-proteinuric, arguing against glomerular disease. VCUG evaluates reflux, which does not explain unilateral isomorphic hematuria with positional pain in an adult.
stages.secondary: The clinical picture is classic for compression of the left renal vein, so imaging should target the venous anatomy.

stages.label: VASCULAR IMAGING
stages.content:
Renal Doppler US: left renal vein peak velocity ratio 5.2 (aortomesenteric segment vs. hilum), markedly narrowed segment between aorta and SMA
CT venography: aortomesenteric angle 18° (normal >45°), beak sign at left renal vein, dilated left gonadal vein with pelvic collateral varicosities
No renal mass, no calculi, normal-appearing collecting systems bilaterally
Left renal vein-to-IVC pullback gradient (venography): 4 mmHg

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this young woman with mild-to-moderate symptoms, preserved renal function, and no significant anemia, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate left nephrectomy
Conservative management with observation, analgesia, and attention to weight/nutrition, reserving intervention for refractory or severe disease
Lifelong systemic anticoagulation
Bilateral ureteral stent placement
stages.explanation: Conservative management is first-line for younger patients with tolerable symptoms and preserved function, because some improve as they gain weight and develop venous collaterals; intervention (endovascular stenting or vein transposition) is reserved for severe pain, significant or worsening hematuria with anemia, declining renal function, or failure of conservative measures over ~3-6 months. Nephrectomy is far too aggressive for a salvageable kidney. Anticoagulation does not address mechanical compression and adds bleeding risk in someone already bleeding into the urine. Ureteral stents treat ureteral obstruction, which is not the problem—the pathology is venous compression, not ureteral.
stages.secondary: Weight gain and collateral formation can relieve compression over time, supporting a trial of conservative care first.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which additional finding would most specifically support this venous compression diagnosis and explain her menstrual pelvic ache?
options: New-onset hypertension with bilateral renal artery bruits
Left-sided pelvic congestion with gonadal vein reflux and vulvar/pelvic varicosities
Bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema
Microscopic hematuria with red cell casts
stages.explanation: Left renal vein compression raises pressure in the left gonadal (ovarian) vein, producing reflux and pelvic congestion syndrome with pelvic/vulvar varicosities and cyclic pelvic pain—directly explaining her menstrual ache and supporting the diagnosis. Renal artery bruits and hypertension suggest renovascular arterial disease, a different entity. Bilateral leg edema suggests IVC obstruction or systemic causes, not isolated left renal vein compression. Red cell casts indicate glomerular bleeding, which contradicts the isomorphic, non-proteinuric hematuria already demonstrated and would point toward nephritis instead.
stages.secondary: Gonadal vein reflux is the anatomic link between the renal vein compression and her pelvic symptoms.

cases.diagnosis: Nutcracker syndrome (left renal vein entrapment between the aorta and superior mesenteric artery)
pearls: Suspect nutcracker syndrome in a thin young patient with left flank pain, isomorphic non-glomerular hematuria, and sometimes pelvic congestion symptoms.
Doppler ultrasound (elevated peak velocity ratio) plus CT/MR venography (narrow aortomesenteric angle, 'beak sign') confirm left renal vein compression.
Isomorphic RBCs without proteinuria or casts argue against glomerular disease and point to an anatomic, non-glomerular source.
Younger patients with tolerable symptoms are managed conservatively first; reserve stenting or vein transposition for severe or refractory disease.

cases.title: Trouble Emptying the Bladder with Repeated Infections
cases.chiefComplaint: A sensation of incomplete emptying with the need to urinate again shortly after voiding, plus several recent urinary tract infections.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man reports about a year of weak urinary stream, hesitancy, and a strong sense of incomplete emptying. He notices that minutes after finishing he must return to void a second time, often producing another substantial volume ('double voiding'). Over the past 8 months he has had three culture-proven urinary tract infections with dysuria and cloudy, malodorous urine, treated with antibiotics each time. He denies gross hematuria, fever, flank pain, or weight loss. PMH includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly; he takes no anticholinergics. No known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago), and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is notable for benign prostatic enlargement in his brother and no bladder or prostate cancer. He has not had prior urologic surgery or catheterization and reports no neurologic symptoms such as leg weakness or saddle numbness.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-nourished man. Abdomen soft; mild suprapubic fullness without tenderness or palpable distended bladder after he reports recent voiding. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged, non-tender prostate without nodules or bogginess; normal sphincter tone. External genitalia normal with no meatal stenosis, penile lesions, or epididymal tenderness. Neurologic exam shows intact lower-extremity strength, normal sensation including perineal sensation, and normal gait. No lower-extremity edema. Urine in the cup appears slightly cloudy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial set of bedside and office tests for this man's voiding symptoms and recurrent infections?
options: Serum PSA and immediate cystoscopy only
Urinalysis with culture plus a post-void residual measurement by bladder ultrasound
CT urogram of the abdomen and pelvis first
Urodynamic pressure-flow study before any other testing
stages.explanation: Urinalysis with culture and a post-void residual (PVR) by bladder ultrasound are the correct, low-cost first steps: they confirm/identify infection and quantify retained urine, which directly links outlet obstruction to urinary stasis and recurrent UTIs. PSA and immediate cystoscopy are not the first move for uncomplicated obstructive symptoms; cystoscopy is reserved for hematuria or suspected anatomic lesions. CT urogram is unnecessary radiation/contrast for an initial obstructive evaluation. Formal urodynamics are second-line, used when the diagnosis is unclear or surgery is contemplated, not as the initial office test.
stages.secondary: An elevated PVR ties his obstructive symptoms to the urinary stasis driving recurrent infections.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, 50-100 WBC/hpf, few bacteria, no RBCs
Urine culture: >100,000 CFU/mL E. coli, pan-sensitive
Post-void residual (bladder US): 320 mL after a void he reported as complete
Uroflowmetry: maximum flow rate 8 mL/s with prolonged, intermittent pattern
