Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL; HbA1c 7.1%
Renal/bladder US: trabeculated bladder wall, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His PVR is markedly elevated and the bladder is trabeculated. What anatomic complication of chronic outlet obstruction best explains a persistent reservoir of stagnant urine and his double voiding?
options: Vesicoureteral reflux
An out-pouching of bladder mucosa through the muscular wall (bladder diverticulum) that fills and empties out of sync with the bladder
A urethral stricture in the bulbar urethra
Neurogenic detrusor overactivity
stages.explanation: Chronic high-pressure voiding against an obstructed outlet herniates mucosa through the detrusor, creating a bladder diverticulum. Because the diverticulum lacks muscle, it fills during voiding and empties poorly afterward—producing residual urine, the urge to void again (double voiding), and stasis that fosters recurrent UTIs. Vesicoureteral reflux causes upper-tract infection/scarring but not the double-voiding reservoir described. A bulbar urethral stricture would obstruct flow but would not by itself create a post-void reservoir, and trabeculation here reflects detrusor hypertrophy. Neurogenic detrusor overactivity causes urgency/incontinence, not a focal urine-trapping pouch.
stages.secondary: A muscle-free pouch fills and drains independently of the bladder, perfectly explaining double voiding and stasis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study best demonstrates the suspected pouch and its relationship to voiding dynamics?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
Voiding cystourethrogram (or CT cystogram), which shows the diverticulum filling and the post-void contrast retained within it
Nuclear renal scan (MAG3)
Retrograde urethrogram alone
stages.explanation: A voiding cystourethrogram (or CT cystogram) is ideal because it opacifies the bladder and shows the diverticulum filling during voiding and retaining contrast afterward, confirming the diagnosis and revealing the bladder neck/outlet. Cystoscopy can also visualize the diverticular neck. A plain KUB shows calcifications and bones but not soft-tissue out-pouchings or dynamics. MAG3 assesses split renal function and drainage of the upper tracts, not a bladder diverticulum. A retrograde urethrogram evaluates the urethra for stricture, not a bladder diverticulum or voiding dynamics of the bladder itself.
stages.secondary: Dynamic contrast imaging captures the pouch filling and the post-void retention that defines the lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy that addresses both the obstruction and the recurrent infections?
options: Long-term indwelling Foley catheter alone

Relieve the bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., alpha-blocker/5-alpha-reductase inhibitor or surgical prostate procedure) and treat the diverticulum surgically if it is large, symptomatic, or causes recurrent infection
Indefinite low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis without addressing the obstruction
Anticholinergic therapy to relax the bladder
stages.explanation: The root problem is outlet obstruction causing stasis, so management must relieve the obstruction (medical therapy or a prostate-reducing procedure such as TURP) and address the diverticulum surgically (diverticulectomy) when it is large, symptomatic, harbors stones/tumor, or drives recurrent UTIs. A permanent catheter is a last resort that does not treat the cause and perpetuates infection. Antibiotic prophylaxis alone ignores the persistent reservoir and obstruction, leading to recurrence and resistance. Anticholinergics worsen emptying and raise PVR, which is dangerous in an already obstructed, high-residual bladder.
stages.secondary: Fix the obstruction first; treat the diverticulum when it remains symptomatic or keeps the urine stagnant.

cases.diagnosis: Bladder diverticulum secondary to bladder outlet obstruction (benign prostatic enlargement) with urinary stasis and recurrent UTI
pearls: Bladder diverticula are usually acquired from chronic outlet obstruction; their muscle-free walls trap urine, causing double voiding, stasis, and recurrent UTIs.
Post-void residual and a trabeculated bladder on ultrasound are early clues; VCUG or CT cystogram demonstrates the pouch and its post-void retention.
Always relieve the underlying outlet obstruction—diverticulectomy alone fails if the obstruction persists.
Stasis within a diverticulum predisposes to stones and, rarely, malignancy, so persistent or large diverticula warrant cystoscopic surveillance.

cases.title: A Small Red Bump at the Urethral Opening
cases.chiefComplaint: A tender red growth at the urinary opening with occasional spotting noticed on toilet paper.
cases.history: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a few months of a small, tender red bump at her urethral opening. She notices occasional blood spotting on toilet paper and mild discomfort with prolonged sitting and during intercourse; voiding is otherwise normal without weak stream or retention. She denies frank hematuria, fever, weight loss, or vaginal bleeding from the cervix. She went through menopause at age 51 and has never used systemic or topical estrogen. PMH includes osteoporosis and hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 88 mcg daily, alendronate 70 mg weekly, and calcium/vitamin D; no anticoagulants. No known drug allergies. She is a widowed retired teacher, lifelong nonsmoker, rare alcohol use, and is sexually active with a new partner. Family history includes breast cancer in a sister but no bladder, urethral, or gynecologic malignancy. She has had no prior urologic procedures and no history of urinary tract infections in the past year. She is anxious because she read online that the lesion 'could be cancer.'
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. External genital exam shows atrophic vulvar and vaginal tissues with pale, thin mucosa consistent with estrogen deficiency. At the posterior margin of the external urethral meatus there is a soft, red, friable, polypoid lesion about 6 mm that bleeds slightly on gentle contact; it is mildly tender. The lesion is exophytic and confined to the distal urethra without extension along the anterior vaginal wall and without a palpable indurated mass or fixed base. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. No suprapubic tenderness. Bimanual exam reveals no anterior vaginal wall mass, fluctuance, or expressible discharge from the urethra.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in evaluating this distal urethral lesion in a postmenopausal woman?
options: Reassure and discharge without examination since it is likely benign
Careful physical examination with urinalysis/culture, recognizing that a friable distal urethral lesion can mimic malignancy and may need biopsy if atypical
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically for presumed infection
Order a CT urogram before any examination
stages.explanation: A focused exam plus urinalysis/culture is the correct first step: it characterizes the lesion, excludes infection, and acknowledges that a red friable distal urethral lesion can mimic urethral carcinoma, so biopsy is warranted if features are atypical (induration, rapid growth, fixation, ulceration). Reassuring and discharging without examining the lesion risks missing malignancy. Empiric antibiotics treat a presumptive infection that has not been demonstrated and delays diagnosis. CT urogram is unnecessary for a clearly distal, externally visible lesion and should not precede a basic exam.
stages.secondary: The key tension is benign caruncle versus malignancy, so direct inspection and a low threshold for biopsy guide everything.

stages.label: OFFICE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: trace blood, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites
Urine culture: no growth
Urine cytology: negative for malignant cells
Lesion: 6 mm, soft, red, polypoid at posterior meatus, friable, non-indurated, not fixed
Vaginal pH 6.5 with atrophic mucosa
No inguinal lymphadenopathy on exam

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a soft, non-indurated, friable polypoid lesion at the posterior meatus in an estrogen-deficient woman, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Urethral caruncle
Urethral (distal) squamous cell carcinoma
Urethral diverticulum
Condyloma acuminatum
stages.explanation: A urethral caruncle—a benign, estrogen-deficiency-related, fleshy, red, friable polyp at the posterior lip of the meatus—best fits a soft, non-indurated, mobile lesion in a postmenopausal woman. Urethral carcinoma is the key mimic but typically appears indurated, fixed, ulcerated, or rapidly enlarging, often with bleeding and sometimes lymphadenopathy, so it must be excluded by biopsy if atypical. A urethral diverticulum presents as a tender anterior vaginal wall mass with post-void dribbling, not an external meatal polyp. Condyloma is verrucous/papillomatous from HPV and usually multiple, not a solitary friable caruncle.
stages.secondary: The lesion's softness, mobility, and classic posterior-meatal location in an atrophic postmenopausal woman point to a benign caruncle.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which feature, if present, would most strongly prompt a biopsy to exclude malignancy rather than treating empirically?
options: Mild tenderness with sitting
Induration, fixation, ulceration, rapid growth, or failure to respond to topical estrogen
A soft, mobile lesion that bleeds slightly with contact
Atrophic vaginal mucosa with elevated vaginal pH
stages.explanation: Induration, fixation, ulceration, rapid enlargement, or non-response to a trial of topical estrogen are red flags for urethral carcinoma and mandate biopsy. Mild positional tenderness is nonspecific and common with a benign caruncle. A soft, mobile lesion that bleeds slightly is typical of a caruncle and not by itself an indication for biopsy. Atrophic mucosa with elevated vaginal pH simply confirms estrogen deficiency—the very condition that predisposes to caruncle—and is not a malignancy red flag.
stages.secondary: Hard, fixed, ulcerated, or treatment-resistant lesions are the ones that must be sampled.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a small, symptomatic but benign-appearing caruncle without red-flag features, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate wide local excision with urethral reconstruction
Conservative therapy with topical/vaginal estrogen and good hygiene, reserving excision for persistent, large, or symptomatic lesions or to confirm diagnosis
Systemic chemotherapy
Long-term oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: First-line therapy is conservative: topical/vaginal estrogen addresses the underlying atrophy and often shrinks the caruncle, combined with sitz baths and hygiene; excision is reserved for lesions that are persistent, large, very symptomatic, bleeding significantly, or when histologic confirmation is needed. Wide local excision with reconstruction is overtreatment for a small benign lesion. Systemic chemotherapy is for invasive malignancy, which has not been established. Long-term antibiotics are inappropriate because the lesion is not infectious; cultures were negative.
stages.secondary: Treat the estrogen deficiency first—many caruncles regress with topical estrogen, and surgery is held for refractory or diagnostically uncertain cases.

cases.diagnosis: Urethral caruncle (benign distal urethral lesion of estrogen deficiency, mimicking malignancy)
pearls: A urethral caruncle is a benign, friable, red polyp at the posterior meatus seen in postmenopausal, estrogen-deficient women.
Its main importance is mimicking urethral carcinoma—biopsy any lesion that is indurated, fixed, ulcerated, rapidly growing, or unresponsive to estrogen.
First-line treatment is topical/vaginal estrogen plus hygiene; excision is reserved for refractory, large, or diagnostically uncertain lesions.
Always confirm with urinalysis/culture and cytology to exclude infection and urothelial malignancy before committing to conservative therapy.

cases.title: Burning, Dribbling, and Painful Intimacy
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent burning with urination, dribbling after voiding, and pain during intercourse.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with about a year of intermittent dysuria, persistent post-void dribbling that wets her underwear minutes after she finishes voiding, and increasing pain with intercourse. She has been treated several times for 'urinary tract infections,' but cultures were frequently negative, and antibiotics provided only transient relief. She occasionally expresses a few drops of cloudy fluid from the urethral area when she presses on the front wall of her vagina. She denies gross hematuria, flank pain, fever, or incontinence with cough or sneeze. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; G2P2 with vaginal deliveries. Medications: only an oral contraceptive pill and occasional ibuprofen; no anticoagulants. No known drug allergies. She works as a nurse, does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and is in a monogamous relationship. Family history is noncontributory for urologic malignancy. She has not had pelvic surgery. She is frustrated by repeated antibiotic courses that fail to resolve her symptoms and the new dyspareunia.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Abdomen soft, nontender, no suprapubic mass. External genitalia normal. On speculum and bimanual exam there is a tender, smooth, cystic mass palpable along the anterior vaginal wall in the mid-urethral region; gentle compression of the mass expresses a few drops of cloudy fluid from the external urethral meatus. No anterior vaginal wall prolapse, no urethral hypermobility, and no stress leakage with cough. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. The mass is mobile, not fixed or hard. Costovertebral angles are non-tender. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which classic symptom triad, together with the exam finding, best characterizes this patient's likely diagnosis?
options: Urgency, frequency, and nocturia
Dysuria, post-void dribbling, and dyspareunia with a tender anterior vaginal wall mass
Hesitancy, weak stream, and incomplete emptying
Hematuria, flank pain, and fever
stages.explanation: The classic '3 Ds'—dysuria, post-void dribbling, and dyspareunia—combined with a tender, fluid-expressing anterior vaginal wall mass strongly suggest a urethral diverticulum. Urgency/frequency/nocturia describe overactive bladder, which lacks a mass and the expressible fluid. Hesitancy/weak stream/incomplete emptying suggest obstruction, more typical of men with prostatic enlargement. Hematuria/flank pain/fever point to upper-tract infection or stones, not a mid-urethral cystic mass. The expressible cloudy fluid and the anterior wall mass are the decisive findings.
stages.secondary: The '3 Ds' plus a tender anterior vaginal wall mass that expresses fluid is the textbook presentation.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: trace blood, few WBCs, no nitrites
Urine culture: no growth
Expressed fluid from mass: cloudy, purulent-appearing
Post-void residual (bladder US): 30 mL (normal)
No stress urinary incontinence demonstrated on cough stress test
Pelvic exam: mobile, tender, cystic mid-urethral anterior wall mass

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm a urethral diverticulum and define its anatomy for surgical planning?
options: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis
MRI of the pelvis (with dedicated urethral sequences), the most sensitive study to delineate the diverticulum and its ostium
Plain radiograph (KUB)
Renal ultrasound only
stages.explanation: Pelvic MRI with dedicated urethral imaging is the most sensitive modality to confirm a urethral diverticulum and map its size, configuration (including saddlebag/circumferential shapes), and the location of the ostium, all critical for surgical planning. Voiding cystourethrography and double-balloon positive-pressure urethrography are alternatives but less sensitive than MRI. Non-contrast CT poorly characterizes the soft-tissue diverticulum and ostium. A KUB shows only calcifications/bone. Renal ultrasound evaluates the upper tracts and does not image a mid-urethral diverticulum adequately.
stages.secondary: MRI gives the best soft-tissue detail of the diverticulum's shape and ostium for planning excision.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Pelvic MRI: 2.2 cm fluid-filled diverticulum arising from the mid-urethra, partially horseshoe-shaped, with an ostium on the dorsal urethra
No solid enhancing component or wall nodularity
No pelvic lymphadenopathy
Bladder and upper tracts normal
Urethral cytology: negative for malignancy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this symptomatic urethral diverticulum without features of malignancy or stone, what is the definitive management?
options: Indefinite suppressive antibiotics with no intervention
Transvaginal surgical excision (diverticulectomy) with watertight urethral closure and tension-free layered repair
Pelvic floor physical therapy alone
Placement of a urethral stent across the ostium
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of a symptomatic urethral diverticulum is transvaginal diverticulectomy with watertight urethral closure and a multilayer, tension-free repair (often with a Martius flap) to prevent fistula and recurrence. Indefinite antibiotics only suppress infection and ignore the anatomic pouch, leading to recurrent symptoms. Pelvic floor therapy treats pelvic pain/incontinence syndromes, not a fluid-filled diverticulum. A urethral stent does not eliminate the diverticular sac and risks erosion and infection; it is not standard therapy for diverticula.
stages.secondary: Excision of the sac with careful layered urethral closure is the standard curative procedure.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which postoperative complication is most specifically associated with urethral diverticulectomy?
options: Ureteral stricture
Urethrovaginal fistula
Renal artery stenosis
Vesicoureteral reflux
stages.explanation: Urethrovaginal fistula is the complication most specific to diverticulectomy, arising from breakdown of the urethral closure; a tension-free multilayer repair and an interposition flap (e.g., Martius) reduce this risk. Ureteral stricture relates to ureteral surgery, not urethral diverticulectomy. Renal artery stenosis is a renovascular process unrelated to this pelvic operation. Vesicoureteral reflux involves the ureterovesical junction and is not a recognized direct consequence of urethral diverticulum excision. Recurrent diverticulum and de novo stress incontinence are other recognized risks, but fistula is the classic, most specific one.
stages.secondary: Breakdown of the urethral repair creates a urethrovaginal fistula—the signature complication of this surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Urethral diverticulum (female), classically presenting with the triad of dysuria, post-void dribbling, and dyspareunia
pearls: Think urethral diverticulum in a woman with recurrent 'UTIs' (often culture-negative) and the 3 Ds—dysuria, dribbling, dyspareunia—plus a tender anterior vaginal wall mass that expresses fluid.
Pelvic MRI is the most sensitive test to define the diverticulum and its ostium for surgical planning.
Definitive treatment is transvaginal diverticulectomy with watertight, multilayer, tension-free urethral closure.
Urethrovaginal fistula is the signature complication; rarely, carcinoma (often adenocarcinoma) or stones arise within a diverticulum, so examine the specimen carefully.

cases.title: Not Passing Urine and Confused
cases.chiefComplaint: Almost no urine output for two days with nausea, fatigue, and new confusion.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man is brought in by his daughter for 2 days of markedly decreased urination progressing to near-anuria, along with nausea, poor appetite, generalized weakness, itching, and new confusion noted this morning. Over the prior 3 months he had worsening lower urinary tract symptoms, intermittent gross hematuria, dull low back and bony pelvic pain, and a 7 kg weight loss. He has not been able to see a doctor recently. PMH includes hypertension and gout. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily and allopurinol 100 mg daily; he occasionally takes ibuprofen for back pain. No known drug allergies. He is a retired machinist, former heavy smoker (40 pack-years), and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes prostate cancer in his father and a brother. He has had no prior urologic surgery and no known kidney disease. On arrival he is lethargic but arousable, oriented only to person, and complains of low back pain; his daughter reports he has been increasingly drowsy and 'not himself' over the past day.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic but arousable older man; oriented to person only. Mucous membranes dry. Cardiopulmonary exam notable for an irregular rhythm-free regular tachycardia and clear lungs; no pericardial rub. Abdomen soft with no palpable distended bladder and no flank fullness; mild bilateral CVA discomfort. Digital rectal exam reveals a hard, irregular, asymmetrically enlarged prostate with a fixed nodular surface and obliteration of the median sulcus. Lower extremities without edema; bony tenderness over the lumbar spine and pelvis. Neurologic exam shows asterixis and diffuse hyporeflexia but no focal deficit, intact perineal sensation, and normal anal tone. Skin shows excoriations from pruritus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this near-anuric, confused, uremic-appearing man, which immediate combination of tests is most appropriate?
options: Basic metabolic panel/renal function and a venous blood gas, plus an ECG and a renal/bladder ultrasound
Brain CT only to evaluate confusion
PSA level alone and discharge with urology follow-up
24-hour urine collection before any imaging
stages.explanation: He needs an immediate metabolic panel (creatinine, K⁺, bicarbonate), a blood gas, an ECG to screen for hyperkalemia, and a renal/bladder ultrasound to identify obstruction—these directly assess life-threatening uremia/hyperkalemia and localize the cause of anuria. A brain CT alone ignores the obvious metabolic/renal emergency driving his encephalopathy. Checking PSA and discharging is dangerously inappropriate in an anuric, encephalopathic patient. A 24-hour urine collection is impossible and irrelevant in near-anuria and would dangerously delay care; obstruction must be excluded urgently with ultrasound.
stages.secondary: Anuria plus confusion demands rapid assessment for hyperkalemia and obstruction simultaneously.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 134 mEq/L  K 6.4 mEq/L  HCO3 14 mEq/L  Cl 104 mEq/L
BUN 110 mg/dL  Creatinine 9.8 mg/dL (baseline ~1.1 four months ago)
Ca 11.2 mg/dL  Phos 6.8 mg/dL
Venous pH 7.26
ECG: peaked T waves, widening QRS
Renal/bladder US: severe bilateral hydronephrosis, empty bladder, no large post-void residual
PSA 184 ng/mL
Hgb 9.6 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His potassium is 6.4 with ECG changes. What is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Proceed directly to bilateral nephrostomy without treating potassium
Stabilize hyperkalemia immediately (IV calcium for cardiac membrane protection, insulin/glucose, etc.) while arranging urgent decompression and possible dialysis
Give a large normal saline bolus and recheck labs in 6 hours
Start an ACE inhibitor for his elevated blood pressure
stages.explanation: Life-threatening hyperkalemia with ECG changes is the first priority: IV calcium to stabilize the myocardium, then insulin/glucose (and other shifting/eliminating measures) while urgently arranging decompression of the obstruction and dialysis if needed. Proceeding to nephrostomy without treating K⁺ risks fatal arrhythmia during the procedure. A saline bolus does not address obstruction-driven anuria and could worsen volume overload without restoring output. An ACE inhibitor would worsen hyperkalemia and renal perfusion and is contraindicated in this setting.
stages.secondary: Protect the heart from hyperkalemia first, then decompress and dialyze as needed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis with an empty bladder. Given the hard fixed prostate, bone pain, and PSA 184, what is the mechanism of his acute kidney injury?
options: Pre-renal azotemia from dehydration only
Bilateral ureteral obstruction (post-renal AKI) from locally advanced prostate cancer involving the trigone/ureteral orifices
Acute tubular necrosis from contrast
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: Bilateral hydronephrosis with an empty bladder localizes the obstruction above the bladder neck—at the ureters/trigone—consistent with locally advanced prostate cancer invading the trigone and encasing both ureteral orifices, producing post-renal AKI and anuria. Pure pre-renal azotemia would not cause bilateral hydronephrosis or anuria of this degree. Contrast ATN is excluded—he has had no contrast and shows hydronephrosis, an obstructive sign. RPGN causes intrinsic renal failure with active urine sediment, not bilateral hydronephrosis with a high PSA and a fixed nodular prostate. The picture is obstructive uropathy from malignancy.
stages.secondary: Bilateral hydronephrosis with an empty bladder means the blockage is at the ureters/trigone, not the bladder outlet.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After medical stabilization, what is the most appropriate intervention to relieve the bilateral ureteral obstruction?
options: A single urethral Foley catheter, which will resolve the hydronephrosis
Urinary diversion via bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy tubes (or ureteral stents) to decompress both kidneys
Emergent radical prostatectomy
Observation with repeat ultrasound in one week
stages.explanation: Because the obstruction is at the ureters/trigone, decompression requires upper-tract diversion—bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy tubes (or retrograde ureteral stents, which are often difficult when the trigone is tumor-invaded). This relieves obstruction, reverses AKI, and allows oncologic treatment. A urethral Foley drains the bladder but cannot relieve ureteral-level obstruction with an already-empty bladder. Emergent radical prostatectomy is inappropriate and unsafe in metastatic, uremic disease. Observation risks ongoing uremia, hyperkalemia, and death; the obstruction must be relieved promptly.
stages.secondary: Ureteral-level obstruction needs upper-tract drainage (nephrostomy/stents)—a bladder catheter will not help.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After decompression, brisk diuresis begins. What complication must be anticipated and managed?
options: Post-obstructive diuresis with volume depletion and electrolyte losses requiring monitoring and replacement
Immediate need for lifelong dialysis regardless of recovery
Severe hyperkalemia worsening despite decompression
Acute urinary retention from the nephrostomy tubes
stages.explanation: Relief of bilateral obstruction commonly triggers post-obstructive diuresis: a large urine output from retained solute/water, impaired concentrating ability, and natriuresis, which can cause volume depletion and electrolyte derangements (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia). Management is close monitoring of output and electrolytes with judicious fluid/electrolyte replacement. Lifelong dialysis is not assumed—renal function often recovers substantially after decompression. Hyperkalemia typically improves once urine flows and dialysis (if used) corrects it. Nephrostomy tubes drain the kidneys directly and do not cause urinary retention.
stages.secondary: Expect a large diuresis after decompression and replace fluids/electrolytes to avoid overcorrection.

cases.diagnosis: Post-renal acute kidney injury from bilateral ureteral obstruction due to locally advanced prostate cancer
pearls: Bilateral hydronephrosis with an empty bladder localizes obstruction to the ureters/trigone—drain the upper tracts (nephrostomy/stents), not just the bladder.
In obstructive AKI, treat life-threatening hyperkalemia first (IV calcium, insulin/glucose) and arrange urgent decompression and possible dialysis.
A hard, fixed, nodular prostate with bone pain and a markedly elevated PSA suggests locally advanced prostate cancer invading the trigone.
Anticipate post-obstructive diuresis after decompression and replace fluids/electrolytes to prevent hypovolemia and hypokalemia.

cases.title: Vague Back and Flank Discomfort with Abnormal Kidney Tests
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of dull lower back and flank ache with fatigue and abnormal kidney function found on routine labs.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man presents with 4 months of dull, poorly localized lower back and bilateral flank aching, mild lower-extremity swelling, fatigue, and a 5 kg unintentional weight loss. He also notes new scrotal swelling and intermittent constipation. Routine labs ordered by his primary doctor showed a rising creatinine, prompting referral. He denies dysuria, gross hematuria, fevers, or night sweats but reports vague malaise. PMH includes hypertension and a 25 pack-year smoking history; he previously took an ergot-derived medication for migraines years ago. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and as-needed acetaminophen; no chronic NSAIDs currently. No known drug allergies. He works in construction, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has no recreational drug use. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior abdominal surgery, trauma, or radiation. He is concerned because his legs feel heavy and his back pain is constant, dull, and not clearly related to movement, which is unusual for his prior mechanical back issues.
cases.exam: Middle-aged man in no acute distress. Abdomen soft, mildly tender deep in the flanks without a pulsatile mass; no bruit auscultated. No costovertebral angle tenderness to light percussion but deep palpation reproduces a dull ache. Mild bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema and a non-tender left-sided scrotal fullness consistent with a varicocele/hydrocele. No palpable lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. Digital rectal exam reveals a benign, non-nodular prostate. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities is intact with normal strength and sensation. No skin rash; lower back without focal vertebral tenderness or step-off.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given rising creatinine with vague back/flank pain and leg edema, what is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Lumbar spine MRI only for the back pain
Renal function panel, urinalysis, inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), and a renal/abdominal ultrasound to look for hydronephrosis
Empiric NSAIDs and physical therapy for mechanical back pain
Immediate renal biopsy
stages.explanation: The right first step is to characterize the renal injury and look for obstruction: a renal panel and urinalysis assess function and sediment, inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) screen for an inflammatory/fibrotic process, and ultrasound detects hydronephrosis. A lumbar MRI alone targets the spine and misses a retroperitoneal cause of both the pain and the renal failure. Empiric NSAIDs are harmful to the kidneys and ignore the rising creatinine. Renal biopsy is premature and inappropriate before obstruction is excluded; it could even be hazardous and would not explain a peri-aortic fibrotic process.
stages.secondary: Rising creatinine with flank pain demands exclusion of obstruction and a screen for an inflammatory cause.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 one year ago), eGFR 30 mL/min/1.73m²
Urinalysis: bland sediment, no significant proteinuria or hematuria
ESR 78 mm/hr  CRP 62 mg/L
IgG4 mildly elevated
Renal/abdominal US: bilateral moderate hydronephrosis with proximal ureteral dilation
CBC: mild normocytic anemia, Hgb 11.4 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cross-sectional imaging is performed. Which finding is most characteristic of the suspected diagnosis?
options: Lateral displacement of the ureters with multiple renal cysts
A peri-aortic/peri-iliac soft-tissue rind that encases the ureters and causes medial deviation with bilateral hydronephrosis
A discrete renal mass with venous tumor thrombus
Bilateral staghorn calculi filling the collecting systems
stages.explanation: Retroperitoneal fibrosis classically shows a peri-aortic and peri-iliac fibroinflammatory soft-tissue 'rind' that envelops and medially deviates the ureters, producing bilateral hydronephrosis—matching his elevated inflammatory markers and bland urine. Lateral ureteral displacement suggests retroperitoneal adenopathy or other masses, the opposite of the medial deviation seen here. A discrete renal mass with venous thrombus describes renal cell carcinoma, not a diffuse periaortic rind. Bilateral staghorn calculi would be evident as dense stones and cause obstruction via stones, not a soft-tissue fibrotic process; his urine and history do not support stones.
stages.secondary: Medial deviation of the ureters by a periaortic soft-tissue rind is the hallmark of this fibroinflammatory disease.

stages.label: CROSS-SECTIONAL IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: confluent periaortic and periiliac soft-tissue mantle from the renal hila to the pelvic brim
Medial deviation of both ureters with bilateral hydronephrosis and proximal ureteral dilation
No discrete renal/adrenal mass, no lymphadenopathy, normal-caliber aorta without aneurysm
PET/biopsy (later): fibroinflammatory tissue with IgG4-positive plasma cells, no malignancy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After relieving the obstruction with ureteral stents (or nephrostomy), what is the cornerstone of medical therapy for idiopathic/IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis?
options: Lifelong broad-spectrum antibiotics
Corticosteroids (with or without steroid-sparing immunosuppression) to suppress the inflammatory fibrosis
Anticoagulation with warfarin
Long-term alpha-blocker monotherapy
stages.explanation: After decompressing the kidneys, the cornerstone is immunosuppression—corticosteroids, often with a steroid-sparing agent (e.g., mycophenolate, tamoxifen, or rituximab in IgG4-related disease)—to suppress the fibroinflammatory process, shrink the rind, and free the ureters. Antibiotics are irrelevant: this is sterile inflammation, not infection. Anticoagulation does not treat the fibrosis and adds bleeding risk. An alpha-blocker addresses bladder outlet symptoms, not periaortic fibrosis encasing the ureters; it has no role as primary therapy here.
stages.secondary: Relieve the obstruction first, then suppress the inflammation with steroids/immunosuppression.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which step is essential before attributing the periaortic rind to idiopathic/IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis?
options: Begin steroids empirically without any tissue or further evaluation
Exclude secondary causes (malignancy, lymphoma, infection, prior ergot/methysergide use, aneurysm) and obtain biopsy when imaging is atypical or malignancy is suspected
Schedule immediate radical surgical resection of the rind
Order a bone marrow biopsy as the first test
stages.explanation: Before labeling it idiopathic/IgG4-related, you must exclude secondary causes—malignancy (including lymphoma and metastatic desmoplastic tumors), infection, inflammatory aortic aneurysm, and drug exposure (ergot alkaloids/methysergide, which he previously took)—and biopsy when imaging is atypical, there is a dominant mass, or malignancy is suspected. Empiric steroids without excluding malignancy can mask or delay cancer diagnosis. Radical resection of the rind is not standard and risks ureteral/vascular injury; therapy is medical with ureterolysis reserved for selected cases. A bone marrow biopsy is not the appropriate first diagnostic step here.
stages.secondary: Malignant and drug-related mimics must be ruled out before calling it idiopathic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Retroperitoneal fibrosis (idiopathic/IgG4-related) causing bilateral ureteral obstruction with medial ureteral deviation
pearls: Retroperitoneal fibrosis presents with vague back/flank pain, leg edema, elevated ESR/CRP, and bilateral hydronephrosis from a periaortic soft-tissue rind.
Medial deviation of the ureters distinguishes it from adenopathy/malignancy, which typically deviate ureters laterally.
Relieve obstruction first (stents or nephrostomy), then treat with corticosteroids ± steroid-sparing agents; IgG4-related disease responds to rituximab.
Always exclude secondary causes—malignancy, infection, inflammatory aneurysm, and ergot/methysergide exposure—before diagnosing idiopathic disease.

cases.title: Burning with Urination That Keeps Coming Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated episodes of burning, frequency, and urgency despite multiple antibiotic courses this year.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents for evaluation of frequent urinary tract infections—she has had four culture-proven episodes in the past 12 months, each with dysuria, urgency, frequency, and suprapubic discomfort that resolved with antibiotics. The infections often follow sexual intercourse, and she notes she uses spermicide-coated condoms and a diaphragm for contraception. She denies fever, flank pain, gross hematuria, vaginal discharge, or symptoms between episodes. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant (recent negative test), and has no history of stones, prior urologic surgery, or abnormal imaging. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only a daily multivitamin; she takes no immunosuppressants. No known drug allergies. She works as a teacher, does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and is sexually active with one partner. Family history is noncontributory. She is frustrated by the recurrences and asks whether there is a way to prevent them rather than just treating each one. She voids about every 4-5 hours and admits she sometimes 'holds it' during the school day and drinks little water at work.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman in no distress. Abdomen soft and nontender with no suprapubic tenderness or palpable bladder at this asymptomatic visit. No costovertebral angle tenderness. External genitalia normal without atrophy; no vaginal discharge, lesions, or anterior vaginal wall mass. Bimanual exam unremarkable with no adnexal masses or cervical motion tenderness. No lower-extremity edema. Neurologic exam intact including normal perineal sensation, suggesting no neurogenic bladder contribution. No flank masses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How is recurrent uncomplicated UTI in a healthy non-pregnant woman best defined and initially evaluated?
options: Any two episodes of dysuria in a lifetime, requiring immediate cystoscopy and CT urogram
≥2 infections in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months; confirm with urine culture and take a history of risk factors, without routine upper-tract imaging or cystoscopy
A single episode that requires renal ultrasound in all patients
Symptoms alone, with no need for any urine culture
stages.explanation: Recurrent UTI is defined as ≥2 infections in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months; initial evaluation centers on confirming infections by culture and identifying modifiable risk factors (e.g., spermicide/diaphragm use, intercourse, voiding habits). Routine cystoscopy and CT urogram are not indicated for uncomplicated recurrent UTIs in healthy young women and are reserved for red flags (hematuria, suspected anatomic/stone disease, atypical organisms). A single episode does not meet the definition and does not require imaging. Treating on symptoms alone risks overuse of antibiotics and misses non-UTI causes; culture confirmation guides therapy.
stages.secondary: The definition is frequency-based, and the workup is history plus cultures—not routine invasive imaging.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Urinalysis (current, asymptomatic): negative leukocyte esterase, negative nitrites, no WBCs
Review of prior cultures: 3× E. coli, 1× Klebsiella, all sensitive to first-line agents
Post-void residual (bladder US): 20 mL (normal)
Renal/bladder ultrasound: normal kidneys and bladder, no stones or hydronephrosis
Fasting glucose normal; not pregnant
No vaginal atrophy; premenopausal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which behavioral and contraceptive counseling is most appropriate to reduce her recurrences?
options: Recommend routine post-coital antibiotics for everyone before any behavioral change
Advise switching away from spermicide/diaphragm, adequate hydration, not delaying voiding, and post-coital voiding
Advise prolonged urinary holding to 'train' the bladder
Recommend daily cranberry juice as definitive, proven cure
stages.explanation: Spermicide and diaphragm use are established, modifiable risk factors that alter vaginal flora and promote recurrent UTIs, so switching contraception, staying well hydrated, voiding regularly (not holding), and voiding after intercourse are appropriate first-line behavioral measures. Jumping straight to post-coital antibiotics for everyone skips reversible behavioral changes and risk-factor counseling. Prolonged holding promotes stasis and infection—the opposite of helpful. Cranberry products have weak, inconsistent evidence and are not a definitive cure, though some patients try them; they should not be presented as proven therapy.
stages.secondary: Target her specific risk factors—spermicide/diaphragm, dehydration, and infrequent voiding—before reaching for antibiotics.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If behavioral changes are insufficient, which antimicrobial prophylaxis strategy is most appropriate given her intercourse-associated pattern?
options: Continuous broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone prophylaxis as first choice
Post-coital (intercourse-related) low-dose prophylaxis, or continuous low-dose prophylaxis, using a first-line agent such as nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX
Long-term indwelling catheter to bypass infections
No prophylaxis is ever appropriate; only treat acute episodes
stages.explanation: For intercourse-associated recurrences, post-coital single-dose prophylaxis (or continuous low-dose prophylaxis) with a first-line agent such as nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX is preferred—effective and antibiotic-sparing relative to continuous broad coverage. Continuous fluoroquinolones are not first-line for prophylaxis given toxicity and resistance concerns and are reserved for specific situations. A long-term catheter would cause, not prevent, infections and is never appropriate here. Saying prophylaxis is never appropriate ignores guideline-supported options for women with frequent, bothersome recurrences after behavioral measures fail.
stages.secondary: Match prophylaxis to the trigger—post-coital dosing fits intercourse-associated recurrences and spares antibiotics.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which non-antibiotic option is reasonable to discuss for prevention, particularly relevant in postmenopausal women but worth knowing here?
options: Systemic estrogen for all premenopausal women
Vaginal (topical) estrogen for postmenopausal women with atrophy, and discussing methenamine or behavioral measures as antibiotic-sparing strategies
Routine prophylactic ureteral stenting
Chronic anticholinergics to prevent infection
stages.explanation: Non-antibiotic strategies worth discussing include vaginal (topical) estrogen for postmenopausal women with genitourinary atrophy (not needed in this premenopausal patient but high-yield to know) and agents such as methenamine hippurate, plus continued behavioral measures—these reduce antibiotic exposure. Systemic estrogen is not indicated to prevent UTIs in premenopausal women and carries unrelated risks. Prophylactic ureteral stenting is invasive, ineffective for prevention, and would promote infection. Anticholinergics treat overactive bladder, not infection, and can raise residual urine, worsening risk.
stages.secondary: Vaginal estrogen (postmenopausal) and methenamine are useful antibiotic-sparing prevention concepts to recognize.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent uncomplicated urinary tract infections in a premenopausal woman with modifiable risk factors (spermicide/diaphragm use, intercourse-associated)
pearls: Recurrent UTI = ≥2 infections in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months; uncomplicated cases in healthy women do not need routine cystoscopy or CT urogram.
Spermicide and diaphragm use, infrequent voiding, and dehydration are modifiable risk factors—address these before antibiotics.
For intercourse-associated recurrences, post-coital or continuous low-dose prophylaxis with nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX is preferred over broad fluoroquinolones.
Antibiotic-sparing options include vaginal estrogen (postmenopausal atrophy) and methenamine hippurate.

cases.title: Fever That Won't Break After Treatment for a Prostate Infection
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent fevers, pelvic pain, and difficulty voiding despite several days of antibiotics for prostatitis.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man admitted for acute bacterial prostatitis remains febrile on day 4 of appropriate IV antibiotics. He has persistent perineal and rectal pain, painful urination, urinary frequency, and now a sense of obstruction with poor stream. He reports rigors and malaise. He initially presented a week ago with dysuria, fever, and an exquisitely tender prostate. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%) and a recent urethral instrumentation for a stricture. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, basal insulin, and current IV ceftriaxone. No known drug allergies. He is a delivery driver, former smoker, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. He has no indwelling catheter currently but had one briefly after his procedure. He is increasingly uncomfortable sitting, and nursing notes document low-grade hypotension responsive to fluids and ongoing spiking fevers despite antibiotics that initially seemed to help, raising concern that the infection has become localized and walled off.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, diaphoretic man appearing ill. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic tenderness; bladder not grossly distended. Digital rectal exam reveals an exquisitely tender, enlarged prostate with a focal area of fluctuance and asymmetric swelling on the right lobe; the gland is boggy and warm. No perianal abscess or fistula seen externally; normal sphincter tone. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Genital exam normal without epididymal tenderness. Skin warm and flushed; no rash. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with clear lungs. Mild dry mucous membranes; capillary refill brisk after fluids.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A diabetic man with prostatitis remains febrile on appropriate antibiotics with focal prostatic fluctuance. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same antibiotics unchanged for another week and reassess
Obtain prostate imaging (transrectal ultrasound or pelvic CT/MRI) to evaluate for a drainable abscess and recheck blood/urine cultures
Vigorously massage the prostate to express purulent fluid
Discharge home on oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: Failure to defervesce on appropriate antibiotics plus focal fluctuance signals a possible prostate abscess, so imaging—transrectal ultrasound or pelvic CT/MRI—is the correct next step to identify a drainable collection, along with repeat blood and urine cultures. Continuing identical antibiotics ignores a likely walled-off collection that antibiotics alone cannot clear. Vigorous prostate massage in acute infection/abscess is contraindicated—it can precipitate bacteremia/sepsis. Discharging an ill, febrile, hypotensive diabetic patient is unsafe and misses the evolving abscess requiring drainage.
stages.secondary: Persistent fever despite correct antibiotics with focal fluctuance means image to find a drainable collection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Glucose 268 mg/dL; HbA1c 9.2%
Blood cultures: gram-negative rods pending speciation
Urine culture: >100,000 CFU/mL E. coli
Transrectal ultrasound: 3.2 cm hypoechoic, fluid-filled collection in the right prostate lobe with internal debris
Pelvic CT: rim-enhancing prostatic fluid collection consistent with abscess; no extension to rectum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging confirms a 3.2 cm rim-enhancing prostatic collection. What is the diagnosis and the key reason antibiotics alone failed?
options: Chronic prostatitis; antibiotics need more time to penetrate the gland
Prostate abscess; antibiotics poorly penetrate a walled-off purulent collection, so drainage is required
Benign prostatic hyperplasia; the fluid is normal glandular secretion
Prostate cancer; the collection is a necrotic tumor
stages.explanation: A rim-enhancing fluid collection in a febrile man failing antibiotics is a prostate abscess; antibiotics penetrate a walled-off purulent cavity poorly, so source control with drainage is needed for cure. Chronic prostatitis is not an acute, rim-enhancing collection and would not explain acute sepsis with fluctuance. BPH produces gland enlargement, not a rim-enhancing abscess or sepsis. Prostate cancer is not suggested by an acute febrile illness with a rim-enhancing fluid collection and positive cultures; this is infection, not malignancy. His diabetes and recent instrumentation are classic risk factors.
stages.secondary: A walled-off purulent cavity resists antibiotics—source control by drainage is the missing step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management of this prostate abscess?
options: Switch to oral antibiotics and observe
Drainage of the abscess (transrectal/transperineal aspiration or transurethral unroofing) combined with continued culture-directed antibiotics and glycemic control
Immediate radical prostatectomy
Pelvic radiation therapy
stages.explanation: Definitive care couples source control—image-guided transrectal/transperineal drainage or transurethral resection/unroofing—with continued culture-directed antibiotics and tight glycemic control in this diabetic patient. Switching to oral antibiotics and observing leaves the undrained abscess and risks worsening sepsis. Radical prostatectomy is not indicated for an infectious abscess and would be morbid and inappropriate in acute sepsis. Pelvic radiation treats malignancy, not infection, and has no role here. Small abscesses (<1-1.5 cm) may resolve with antibiotics alone, but a 3.2 cm septic collection requires drainage.
stages.secondary: Combine drainage (source control) with culture-directed antibiotics and diabetes control.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the most immediate life-threatening concern if the abscess is not adequately drained?
options: Erectile dysfunction
Progression to severe sepsis/septic shock with possible spontaneous rupture into the urethra or rectum
Urethral caruncle formation
Chronic kidney disease from contrast nephropathy
stages.explanation: An undrained prostate abscess can progress to severe sepsis/septic shock and may spontaneously rupture into the urethra, rectum, or perineum—the most immediate, life-threatening concern requiring prompt source control. Erectile dysfunction is a possible later sequela but not the acute danger. A urethral caruncle is an unrelated benign distal urethral lesion of postmenopausal women, not a complication of prostate abscess. Contrast nephropathy is a procedural risk but not the dominant, immediately life-threatening concern compared with ongoing uncontrolled sepsis from an undrained collection.
stages.secondary: The acute threat is uncontrolled sepsis and rupture—hence the urgency of drainage.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which underlying factors most contributed to this man's prostate abscess and should be addressed to prevent recurrence?
options: Well-controlled diabetes and a healthy diet
Poorly controlled diabetes and recent urethral instrumentation/catheterization
Regular aerobic exercise
Use of vaginal estrogen
stages.explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%) and recent urethral instrumentation/catheterization are the major predisposing factors for prostate abscess and must be addressed—optimizing glycemic control and minimizing/keeping aseptic any instrumentation reduce recurrence risk. Well-controlled diabetes and a healthy diet are protective, not contributory. Regular exercise is unrelated to abscess formation. Vaginal estrogen is irrelevant to a male patient and to prostate abscess pathophysiology. Diabetes and instrumentation are the classic, modifiable risk factors highlighted by this case.
stages.secondary: Optimize glycemic control and limit/aseptically perform instrumentation to prevent recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Prostate abscess complicating acute bacterial prostatitis (risk factors: poorly controlled diabetes, recent urethral instrumentation)
pearls: Suspect prostate abscess when a patient with prostatitis fails to defervesce on appropriate antibiotics or has focal prostatic fluctuance.
Diabetes and recent urethral instrumentation/catheterization are major risk factors; E. coli is the most common organism.
Diagnose with transrectal ultrasound or pelvic CT/MRI; abscesses generally require drainage (transrectal/transperineal or transurethral) plus culture-directed antibiotics.
Avoid vigorous prostate massage in acute infection—it can precipitate bacteremia; undrained abscess can progress to sepsis or rupture.

cases.title: Rising Creatinine and Falling Urine Output After a Kidney Transplant
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening kidney function tests and reduced urine output three weeks after a kidney transplant.
cases.history: A 46-year-old man who received a deceased-donor kidney transplant 3 weeks ago is admitted with a rising creatinine and decreasing urine output over several days. He had good initial graft function with a falling creatinine after surgery, then began making less urine. He reports mild fullness and discomfort over the graft site in the right lower quadrant but denies fever, dysuria, or gross hematuria. He has been adherent to immunosuppression. PMH includes end-stage renal disease from IgA nephropathy and hypertension. Medications: tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, prednisone, valganciclovir prophylaxis, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis; antihypertensives as needed. No known drug allergies. He is married, works in IT, does not smoke, and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is notable for renal disease. His tacrolimus trough has been within target range. He notes some clear fluid leaking from the surgical incision earlier in the week, and his urine output, initially robust, has progressively declined, prompting this evaluation by the transplant team.
cases.exam: Comfortable but mildly uncomfortable man. Abdomen soft; over the right iliac fossa transplant site there is mild fullness and tenderness with a possible fluid collection palpable adjacent to the graft. The incision is clean with a small amount of clear drainage noted. No graft bruit change documented. No costovertebral angle tenderness over the native kidneys. No peripheral edema beyond baseline. Genitourinary exam normal; the urethral catheter (if present) drains scant clear urine. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. No rash. The graft is non-erythematous without overlying warmth suggesting cellulitis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study to evaluate rising creatinine and falling urine output in a transplant kidney?
options: Non-contrast CT abdomen as the first test
Doppler ultrasound of the allograft to assess for hydronephrosis, perinephric fluid collections, and vascular flow
Immediate allograft biopsy without imaging
Nuclear bone scan
stages.explanation: Allograft Doppler ultrasound is the appropriate first study: it is rapid, noninvasive, and differentiates causes—detecting hydronephrosis (obstruction), perinephric collections (lymphocele, urinoma, hematoma) that may compress the ureter, and assessing arterial/venous flow and resistive indices. Non-contrast CT is not the first-line screen and adds radiation without the vascular assessment ultrasound provides. Immediate biopsy is invasive and premature before excluding obstruction or a compressive collection, which have different management. A bone scan is irrelevant to allograft dysfunction. Ultrasound efficiently narrows the differential before biopsy.
stages.secondary: Ultrasound is the first test because it separates obstruction and fluid collections from intrinsic causes before biopsy.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Allograft Doppler US: moderate hydronephrosis of the transplant kidney with dilated transplant ureter
A peritransplant fluid collection (4 cm) adjacent to the lower pole, abutting the ureter
Arterial waveforms present with normal resistive index; patent renal vein
Bladder: small volume, catheter draining scant urine
Creatinine 3.4 mg/dL (nadir 1.4 post-op)
Clear incisional drainage; fluid creatinine pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ultrasound shows hydronephrosis with a peritransplant collection and clear incisional leakage. How does ultrasound help differentiate the cause, and what is the most likely problem?
options: Acute rejection alone, which always shows hydronephrosis on ultrasound

Obstructive uropathy of the allograft—ultrasound shows hydronephrosis indicating mechanical obstruction (e.g., ureteral stricture/kink or a compressive collection such as a lymphocele/urinoma) rather than intrinsic rejection
Tacrolimus toxicity, diagnosed by hydronephrosis
Renal vein thrombosis, confirmed by hydronephrosis
stages.explanation: Hydronephrosis points to mechanical obstruction of the allograft—either intrinsic ureteral problems (stricture, kink, clot) or extrinsic compression by a peritransplant collection (lymphocele or urinoma). Ultrasound thus differentiates obstruction from non-obstructive causes (rejection, drug toxicity), which do NOT cause hydronephrosis. Rejection and tacrolimus toxicity present with rising creatinine but a non-dilated system, typically requiring biopsy/levels, not relieved by drainage. Renal vein thrombosis shows absent/reversed venous flow and a high-resistance arterial pattern, not isolated hydronephrosis; here the vein is patent with a normal resistive index. The clear leak suggests a possible urine leak/urinoma.
stages.secondary: Hydronephrosis = mechanical obstruction; rejection and drug toxicity do not dilate the collecting system.

stages.label: FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Peritransplant fluid creatinine: 28 mg/dL (serum creatinine 3.4 mg/dL)
Fluid-to-serum creatinine ratio markedly elevated, consistent with urine (urinoma)
No organisms on Gram stain
Nephrostogram/antegrade study: contrast extravasation at the ureterovesical anastomosis with downstream obstruction
Tacrolimus trough: within target range

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Fluid creatinine far exceeds serum, confirming a urinoma from anastomotic leak with associated obstruction. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Increase immunosuppression for presumed rejection
Decompress and divert the urine—percutaneous nephrostomy and/or ureteral stenting, drain the urinoma, and repair the leak if needed
Stop all immunosuppression immediately
Observe with serial creatinine and no intervention
stages.explanation: A urine leak with obstruction requires urinary decompression and diversion: percutaneous nephrostomy and/or ureteral stent to divert urine away from the leak, drainage of the urinoma, and surgical repair/reimplantation if the defect is significant. Increasing immunosuppression treats rejection, which is not the problem and would worsen infection/healing risk. Stopping all immunosuppression risks rejection and is not indicated for a mechanical/urologic complication. Observation alone allows ongoing leak, obstruction, urinoma expansion, graft injury, and infection. Source control plus diversion preserves the graft.
stages.secondary: Treat the urologic complication mechanically—divert urine, drain the collection, repair the leak—not with more immunosuppression.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which peritransplant fluid collection is classically distinguished from a urinoma by its timing and fluid chemistry?
options: A urinoma, which appears years later with low creatinine fluid
A lymphocele, which typically appears weeks to months later with fluid creatinine similar to serum (not elevated)
A hematoma, which has fluid creatinine far above serum
An abscess, which always has fluid creatinine above serum
stages.explanation: A lymphocele typically arises weeks to months post-transplant from disrupted lymphatics and has fluid creatinine similar to serum (lymph, not urine); it can compress the ureter and cause obstruction but is chemically distinct from a urinoma, whose fluid creatinine far exceeds serum. A urinoma usually presents early (as here) with high-creatinine fluid, not years later. A hematoma is blood, not high-creatinine urine. An abscess is defined by infection (organisms, leukocytes), not by elevated creatinine. Fluid creatinine relative to serum is the key discriminator: high = urinoma, equal = lymphocele.
stages.secondary: Fluid-to-serum creatinine ratio is the discriminator: markedly high = urinoma; roughly equal = lymphocele.

cases.diagnosis: Allograft obstruction from a urine leak (urinoma) at the ureteral anastomosis, differentiated from rejection/drug toxicity by ultrasound and fluid analysis
pearls: In allograft dysfunction, Doppler ultrasound first: hydronephrosis means mechanical obstruction, whereas rejection and calcineurin-inhibitor toxicity show a non-dilated system.
Peritransplant fluid creatinine far above serum indicates a urinoma (urine leak); fluid creatinine equal to serum suggests a lymphocele.
Urine leaks present early post-transplant; lymphoceles typically appear weeks to months later and can also compress the ureter.
Manage urologic obstruction with decompression/diversion (nephrostomy, stent) and drainage/repair—not by escalating immunosuppression.

cases.title: Stop-and-Start Stream and Pain That Changes with Position
cases.chiefComplaint: An intermittent urinary stream that stops and starts, with painful urination that varies with body position.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man reports several months of an intermittent urinary stream that abruptly stops mid-flow and then resumes, sometimes improving when he shifts his position or lies down. He has dysuria that is worse in certain positions, suprapubic discomfort, and occasional terminal hematuria. He has long-standing lower urinary tract symptoms with weak stream, hesitancy, and incomplete emptying, suggesting chronic outlet obstruction and retained urine. He denies fever, flank pain, or weight loss. PMH includes benign prostatic hyperplasia and hypertension. Medications: tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily; no anticoagulants. No known drug allergies. He is a retired carpenter, former smoker, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory for urologic malignancy. He has had no prior stone disease, urologic surgery, or catheterization. He finds the positional, stop-start nature of his symptoms peculiar, and he sometimes has to 'jiggle' or change position to finish voiding, which then relieves the blockage temporarily.
cases.exam: Comfortable older man. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic fullness, mildly tender, without a tensely distended bladder at present. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Digital rectal exam reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, non-tender prostate without nodules, consistent with benign enlargement; normal sphincter tone. External genitalia normal without meatal stenosis or penile lesions. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam intact with normal perineal sensation and lower-extremity strength. Urine in the cup appears slightly blood-tinged at the end of the sample.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which symptom is most classic for a free-floating object in the bladder obstructing the outlet intermittently?
options: Continuous dribbling incontinence regardless of position
Positional and intermittent (stop-start) urinary stream that changes when the patient moves or lies down
Severe colicky flank pain radiating to the groin
Painless gross hematuria with no other symptoms
stages.explanation: A mobile bladder stone can intermittently occlude the bladder outlet, producing a positional, stop-start stream that improves when the patient changes position or lies down (the stone rolls away from the outlet)—classic for bladder calculi. Continuous dribbling incontinence suggests overflow or sphincter problems, not a mobile obstructing object. Colicky flank-to-groin pain is typical of ureteral stones, not bladder stones. Painless gross hematuria alone raises concern for urothelial malignancy and lacks the positional, intermittent stream that points to a free object in the bladder.
stages.secondary: Positional, stop-start flow that a mobile object would cause is the hallmark clue here.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, few WBCs, no nitrites
Urine culture: pending
Post-void residual (bladder US): 180 mL
Uroflowmetry: low maximum flow with an interrupted pattern
Renal/bladder ultrasound: echogenic mobile focus in the bladder with posterior acoustic shadowing; trabeculated bladder wall; no hydronephrosis
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ultrasound shows a mobile shadowing focus in the bladder with a trabeculated wall and elevated PVR. What is the diagnosis and its usual underlying cause in this man?
options: Ureteral stone causing the bladder findings
Bladder stone (vesical calculus) forming on retained urine from bladder outlet obstruction/urinary stasis
Bladder cancer with calcification
Prostatitis
stages.explanation: A mobile, shadowing intravesical focus with a trabeculated bladder and elevated post-void residual indicates a bladder stone, which typically forms when chronic outlet obstruction (here BPH) causes urinary stasis and concentration, allowing crystal precipitation and stone growth. A ureteral stone would be in the ureter with possible hydronephrosis and colicky flank pain, not a free mobile bladder focus. Bladder cancer can calcify but is not a free mobile object and usually presents with painless hematuria and a mural mass. Prostatitis causes pain and fever, not a mobile shadowing bladder stone.
stages.secondary: Stasis from outlet obstruction lets a stone form and grow in the bladder—hence the mobile shadowing focus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management that addresses both the stone and its cause?
options: Increase fluids and observe the stone indefinitely
Remove the stone (e.g., transurethral cystolitholapaxy) AND relieve the bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., TURP or medical therapy)
Long-term antibiotics alone
Shock wave lithotripsy aimed at the kidney
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment removes the stone—commonly transurethral cystolitholapaxy (mechanical/laser fragmentation and extraction)—AND corrects the underlying outlet obstruction (e.g., TURP, or optimized medical therapy) to prevent recurrence by eliminating stasis. Observation leaves an obstructing stone causing symptoms, infection risk, and recurrence. Antibiotics alone do not remove the stone or fix obstruction and promote resistance. Shock wave lithotripsy aimed at the kidney is for renal/ureteral stones, not a bladder stone driven by outlet obstruction; treating the bladder stone and the obstruction together is the standard.
stages.secondary: Take out the stone and fix the obstruction that caused it—both are needed to prevent recurrence.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complication of chronic bladder stones and stasis should prompt cystoscopic evaluation and surveillance?
options: Development of squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder from chronic irritation
Renal artery stenosis
Urethral caruncle
Gout flares
stages.explanation: Chronic bladder stones cause persistent mucosal irritation, which over time is associated with squamous metaplasia and an increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder—warranting cystoscopic inspection and surveillance, especially with persistent hematuria. Renal artery stenosis is a renovascular condition unrelated to bladder stones. A urethral caruncle is a benign distal urethral lesion of postmenopausal women, unrelated to male bladder stones. Gout reflects uric acid metabolism systemically; while uric acid stones exist, gout flares are not a direct complication of bladder stones requiring cystoscopic surveillance.
stages.secondary: Chronic irritation from stones raises squamous cell carcinoma risk—evaluate the mucosa cystoscopically.

cases.diagnosis: Bladder stone (vesical calculus) forming on retained urine from bladder outlet obstruction (benign prostatic hyperplasia)
pearls: A positional, stop-start urinary stream that improves with position change suggests a mobile bladder stone intermittently blocking the outlet.
Bladder stones usually form from urinary stasis due to outlet obstruction (BPH), neurogenic bladder, or chronic catheters—elevated PVR and a trabeculated bladder are clues.
Treat both the stone (cystolitholapaxy) and the underlying obstruction (TURP/medical therapy) to prevent recurrence.
Chronic bladder stone irritation is associated with squamous cell carcinoma—evaluate persistent hematuria cystoscopically.

cases.title: Scrotal Fullness Found During a Fertility Workup
cases.chiefComplaint: A dull ache and a feeling of fullness in the left side of the scrotum, noticed while being evaluated for difficulty conceiving with his partner.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents after 14 months of unprotected intercourse without conception; his 24-year-old partner has had a normal gynecologic workup. He reports a long-standing dull, dragging discomfort in the left hemiscrotum that worsens after standing all day at his warehouse job and during exercise, and resolves when he lies down. He denies acute pain, fever, dysuria, urethral discharge, or trauma. He has noticed the left scrotum looks 'lumpy.' Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He has never had mumps, cryptorchidism, prior scrotal surgery, or sexually transmitted infections. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and uses no anabolic steroids or recreational drugs. He works in a hot warehouse and wears tight briefs. Family history is notable for a paternal uncle who required fertility treatment. He has normal libido and erectile function and shaves regularly. He is anxious about whether he will be able to have biological children.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, normal secondary sexual characteristics with normal body hair and gynecomastia absent. Abdomen soft and nontender without masses. While standing, the left hemiscrotum contains a soft, nontender, compressible mass superior to and surrounding the testis that feels like a 'bag of worms'; it becomes more prominent with Valsalva. Both testes are descended, normal in consistency, and nontender; the left testis is slightly smaller and softer than the right. When supine, the left scrotal mass decreases substantially in size. There is no transillumination of a fluid collection, no inguinal hernia palpated, and the cremasteric reflex is intact bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical exam maneuver best characterizes this scrotal finding?
options: Transillumination of the scrotum in a dark room
Examining the patient standing and with Valsalva, then repeating supine
Eliciting the cremasteric reflex by stroking the inner thigh
Applying steady traction to assess for testicular torsion
stages.explanation: A varicocele is dilated pampiniform plexus veins that engorge with upright posture and Valsalva and decompress when supine, so examining standing/with Valsalva then supine is the key maneuver and demonstrates the classic decompression. Transillumination characterizes a fluid-filled hydrocele, not a venous plexus, and would be negative here. The cremasteric reflex assesses for torsion and is normal in varicocele, so it does not characterize the mass. Steady traction is not a validated torsion test; torsion presents with acute severe pain, an absent cremasteric reflex, and a high-riding testis, none of which are present in this chronic, painless, posture-dependent process.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation should prompt additional evaluation for a secondary cause?
options: The mass decompresses when the patient lies down
The discomfort worsens after prolonged standing
An isolated right-sided varicocele or one that does not decompress when supine
The presence of mild left testicular atrophy
stages.explanation: Most varicoceles are left-sided because the left gonadal vein drains at a right angle into the left renal vein, making left predominance and posture-dependent decompression normal idiopathic findings. A right-sided or isolated varicocele, or one that fails to decompress supine, is a red flag for a retroperitoneal mass or renal/IVC pathology compressing venous drainage and warrants abdominal imaging. Posture-dependent decompression and exertional discomfort are typical benign features. Mild left testicular atrophy reflects the varicocele's effect on the ipsilateral testis and is an indication for treatment, not a sign of a secondary obstructive cause.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Scrotal duplex ultrasound: Dilated left pampiniform plexus veins, maximum diameter 3.8 mm (normal <2 mm)
Retrograde venous flow with Valsalva on the left
Left testicular volume 12 mL; right testicular volume 18 mL
No solid testicular mass; no hydrocele
Right pampiniform veins normal
Semen analysis: Volume 2.8 mL; concentration 9 million/mL (low); motility 28% (low); morphology 3% normal forms
Repeat semen analysis (4 weeks later): concentration 11 million/mL; motility 30%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the abnormal semen parameters, left testicular hypotrophy, and a palpable varicocele, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassurance and observation alone with no intervention
Empiric clomiphene to stimulate spermatogenesis
Refer for varicocele repair (varicocelectomy or embolization)
Start testosterone replacement therapy to improve fertility
stages.explanation: A clinically palpable varicocele with impaired semen parameters and ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy in a man pursuing fertility is a guideline-supported indication for repair; varicocelectomy or percutaneous embolization can improve semen parameters and pregnancy rates. Observation alone is appropriate for an incidental varicocele without abnormal semen or atrophy, but not here given documented subfertility. Clomiphene is not the standard intervention for a correctable anatomic cause and would not address the venous reflux. Exogenous testosterone suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and intratesticular testosterone, worsening spermatogenesis, and is contraindicated in men seeking fertility.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What should the patient be counseled to expect after varicocele repair?
options: Immediate normalization of fertility within 2 weeks
Improvement in semen parameters typically assessed at 3-6 months
Permanent loss of the affected testis is expected
Lifelong testosterone supplementation will be required
stages.explanation: Spermatogenesis takes roughly 70-90 days plus epididymal transit, so semen parameters are reassessed about 3-6 months after repair, when many men show improved count and motility. Fertility does not normalize within two weeks because a full spermatogenic cycle has not yet completed. Testicular loss is not an expected outcome; modern subinguinal microsurgical repair preserves the testis and arterial supply. Testosterone supplementation is not required after repair and would impair fertility; if anything, Leydig cell function and endogenous testosterone may improve following correction of the varicocele.

cases.diagnosis: Left-sided varicocele with associated subfertility and testicular hypotrophy
pearls: Varicoceles are almost always left-sided due to the left gonadal vein's perpendicular drainage into the left renal vein; a right-sided or non-decompressing varicocele warrants imaging for retroperitoneal or renal pathology.
The classic exam is a 'bag of worms' that enlarges with standing/Valsalva and decompresses supine; it does NOT transilluminate (that is a hydrocele).
Indications for repair include abnormal semen parameters, ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy, or pain, particularly in men pursuing fertility.
Reassess semen parameters at 3-6 months after repair because a full spermatogenic cycle takes about 3 months; never give exogenous testosterone to a man seeking fertility.

cases.title: Painless Scrotal Swelling in an Infant
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report painless swelling of the scrotum present since birth that seems to change in size during the day.
cases.history: A 3-month-old boy is brought in by his parents for scrotal swelling noticed since the first week of life. The swelling involves the right hemiscrotum, is not red or warm, and does not appear to cause the infant pain; he feeds well, has normal wet diapers, and is gaining weight along the 60th percentile. The parents report the swelling looks larger and tenser in the evening after he has been crying or active and is smaller and softer in the morning when he wakes. There is no vomiting, no abdominal distension, and no change in stool pattern. He was born at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal delivery; pregnancy was unremarkable and newborn screening was normal. He takes vitamin D drops and no other medications and has no known allergies. Immunizations are up to date for age. There is no family history of inguinal hernia repair in infancy or congenital scrotal disorders. He has no fever and no prior surgeries. The parents are worried about whether this requires an operation.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable infant in no distress. Abdomen soft, nondistended, nontender, without palpable masses or organomegaly. The right hemiscrotum is enlarged with a smooth, soft, nontender, fluctuant swelling that surrounds the testis; the testis is descended and nontender but difficult to palpate distinctly through the fluid. The swelling brightly transilluminates with a penlight in a darkened room. There is no overlying erythema or warmth. Gentle compression slightly reduces the swelling, and it does not reduce abruptly with an audible gurgle. No inguinal mass or bulge is appreciated at rest, and the cord above the swelling can be palpated without thickening. Left hemiscrotum and testis are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside finding most strongly supports a fluid-filled hydrocele over a solid scrotal mass?
options: The swelling is nontender to palpation
The swelling brightly transilluminates with a penlight
The testis is descended in the scrotum
The swelling is larger in the evening
