stages.explanation: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) is both diagnostic and therapeutic: it provides histology, grade, and crucially the depth of invasion (muscularis propria status) that drives all subsequent management. So TURBT is the correct next step. Radical cystectomy is reserved for muscle-invasive or high-risk disease and should never precede a tissue diagnosis and staging. Chemotherapy based on imaging alone is inappropriate without histologic confirmation. Intravesical BCG is an adjuvant therapy for high-risk non-muscle-invasive cancer given after TURBT, not before any biopsy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: TURBT shows high-grade urothelial carcinoma confined to the lamina propria without muscle invasion (T1, high grade). What adjuvant therapy is most appropriate?
options: No further therapy; routine surveillance only
Intravesical BCG immunotherapy
Immediate radical cystectomy for all such cases
Adjuvant pelvic external-beam radiation
stages.explanation: High-grade non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (including high-grade T1) carries substantial risk of recurrence and progression, so intravesical BCG immunotherapy after complete TURBT is the standard adjuvant treatment to reduce recurrence and progression. So BCG is correct. Surveillance alone undertreats high-grade disease. Radical cystectomy is an option for BCG-refractory or very high-risk cases but is not mandatory for all high-grade T1 tumors as the initial step. Adjuvant radiation is not standard for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer.

cases.diagnosis: Urothelial (bladder) carcinoma associated with occupational aromatic amine (aniline dye) exposure
pearls: Painless gross hematuria in an adult is bladder cancer until proven otherwise and requires CT urography plus cystoscopy, with cytology as an adjunct.
Aromatic amines (benzidine, 2-naphthylamine) from dye, rubber, and textile work, plus cigarette smoking, are major risk factors for urothelial carcinoma.
TURBT is the key diagnostic and therapeutic step; muscularis propria invasion status determines whether disease is non-muscle-invasive or muscle-invasive.
High-grade non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer is treated with TURBT followed by intravesical BCG to reduce recurrence and progression.

cases.title: Recurrent Bloody Urine in a Returned Traveler
cases.chiefComplaint: Long-standing intermittent blood in the urine with recent worsening and new urinary symptoms.
cases.history: A 50-year-old immigrant from rural Egypt presents with years of intermittent painless hematuria, now more frequent, with new urinary frequency, urgency, and lower abdominal discomfort. As a child and young adult he swam and worked in irrigation canals and recalls being told he had 'blood fluke' (schistosomiasis) but received incomplete treatment. He denies fever, flank pain, or urethral discharge. He has a 10 pack-year smoking history. PMH includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; no allergies. He works in landscaping in the US for the past eight years. He drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for mild fatigue and a 3 kg weight loss over three months. He has had recurrent episodes attributed to 'urinary infections' over the years, sometimes with terminal hematuria. He has had no prior cystoscopy. He is concerned because the bleeding now occurs almost daily and he sometimes passes small clots. No recent travel back to Egypt in five years.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic tenderness; no palpable masses or organomegaly. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Genital exam normal; digital rectal exam shows a smooth, nontender, normal-sized prostate without nodules. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. No peripheral edema. The remainder of the exam, including cardiopulmonary and neurologic systems, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has chronic hematuria with childhood schistosomiasis exposure. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach for the urinary symptoms?
options: Empiric praziquantel and reassess in three months
Urinalysis, urine cytology, and cystoscopy with imaging
Renal biopsy to evaluate for glomerulonephritis
Prostate MRI and PSA testing
stages.explanation: Chronic hematuria with new irritative symptoms in a patient with prior Schistosoma haematobium infection requires a full evaluation: urinalysis, urine cytology, upper-tract imaging, and cystoscopy to inspect for bladder mucosal changes and malignancy. So this combined workup is correct. Empiric praziquantel alone treats active infection but ignores the risk of bladder cancer in this high-risk patient and delays diagnosis. Renal biopsy targets glomerular disease, which is not suggested by the lower-tract symptoms and terminal hematuria. Prostate MRI/PSA addresses the prostate, but the prostate is normal and the symptoms localize to the bladder.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: >50 RBC/hpf, occasional WBC
Urine microscopy: terminal-spined ova consistent with S. haematobium
Urine cytology: atypical squamoid cells
Cystoscopy: irregular, ulcerated mass on the posterior bladder wall with surrounding 'sandy patches'
CT urography: focal bladder wall thickening, no hydronephrosis
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Eosinophils: mildly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: TURBT of the bladder mass is performed. Given his chronic schistosomiasis, which histologic type of bladder cancer is most likely?
options: High-grade urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma
Squamous cell carcinoma
Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Primary bladder adenocarcinoma
stages.explanation: Chronic inflammation from Schistosoma haematobium induces squamous metaplasia of the bladder urothelium, and the most strongly associated malignancy is squamous cell carcinoma. So squamous cell carcinoma is correct. Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma is the most common bladder cancer overall and the type linked to smoking and aromatic amines, but schistosomiasis specifically predisposes to squamous histology. Small cell carcinoma is a rare aggressive neuroendocrine tumor not characteristically caused by schistosomiasis. Primary adenocarcinoma is associated with urachal remnants and chronic irritation but is far less typical of schistosomal bladder cancer than squamous cell carcinoma.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Pathology confirms squamous cell carcinoma invading the muscularis propria. What is the most important next step in management planning?
options: Intravesical BCG immunotherapy
Repeat TURBT in three months only
Staging with cross-sectional imaging of chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Long-term suppressive antibiotics
stages.explanation: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer requires complete clinical staging—CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis plus chest imaging—to assess local extent, nodal involvement, and distant metastases before deciding on definitive therapy such as radical cystectomy. So staging imaging is correct. Intravesical BCG is for non-muscle-invasive urothelial cancer and is ineffective for muscle-invasive squamous cell carcinoma. Repeat TURBT alone undertreats invasive disease. Suppressive antibiotics do not treat cancer. Note that schistosomal squamous cell carcinoma also tends to be relatively chemoresistant and radioresistant, further emphasizing accurate staging for surgical planning.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows organ-confined, muscle-invasive squamous cell carcinoma without metastases. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Definitive treatment with radical cystectomy
Intravesical chemotherapy with mitomycin C
Cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone as definitive therapy
Active surveillance with serial cystoscopy
stages.explanation: For organ-confined, muscle-invasive bladder squamous cell carcinoma, radical cystectomy with urinary diversion is the definitive treatment of choice, particularly because this histology tends to respond poorly to chemotherapy and radiation. So radical cystectomy is correct. Intravesical mitomycin C is for non-muscle-invasive disease and cannot treat invasive cancer. Cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is not definitive for this relatively chemoresistant squamous histology, though multimodal approaches may be considered selectively. Active surveillance is dangerously inadequate for invasive cancer.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: On a global health level, what intervention would most reduce the incidence of this cancer in endemic regions?
options: Universal smoking cessation programs alone
Mass praziquantel treatment and schistosomiasis control
Routine cystectomy screening for all adults
Banning aromatic amine industrial exposure
stages.explanation: Because this squamous cell carcinoma is driven by chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection, the most effective population-level prevention is schistosomiasis control through mass praziquantel administration, sanitation, snail control, and reduced freshwater contact. So mass praziquantel and schistosomiasis control are correct. Smoking cessation reduces urothelial cancer but does not address the schistosomal driver of squamous cancer. Routine screening cystectomy is absurd and harmful. Banning aromatic amines targets occupational urothelial carcinoma, not schistosomiasis-related squamous cancer in endemic regions.

cases.diagnosis: Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder secondary to chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection
pearls: Chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection causes squamous metaplasia and predisposes to squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder, often in endemic regions like Egypt.
Look for terminal-spined ova in urine and cystoscopic 'sandy patches'; chronic painless hematuria warrants cytology, imaging, and cystoscopy.
Schistosomal squamous cell carcinoma is relatively chemo- and radioresistant; organ-confined muscle-invasive disease is best treated with radical cystectomy.
Population-level prevention relies on schistosomiasis control (praziquantel, sanitation, reduced freshwater exposure).

cases.title: A Sore on the Tip of the Penis That Will Not Heal
cases.chiefComplaint: A nonhealing ulcer on the head of the penis present for several months.
cases.history: A 65-year-old uncircumcised man presents with a nonhealing ulcerated lesion on the glans penis that has slowly enlarged over four months. It is mildly tender, occasionally bleeds, and has a foul-smelling discharge. He has long-standing phimosis that prevents full foreskin retraction, making hygiene difficult, and he admits to poor genital hygiene. He has a history of genital warts in his 30s (HPV). He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, or systemic symptoms but has noted a firm lump in the right groin. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history. PMH includes type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily; no allergies. He is sexually inactive currently and is a widower. He drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative for weight loss or bone pain. He delayed seeking care out of embarrassment and assumed the lesion was an infection. He has not been circumcised and has never had this evaluated before.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man. Genital exam reveals phimosis with inability to fully retract the foreskin; on partial retraction there is a 2 cm firm, ulcerated, exophytic lesion on the glans with raised irregular edges and surrounding induration. There is malodorous discharge under the foreskin. A firm, mobile, enlarged right inguinal lymph node (~2 cm) is palpable; left groin is normal. Testes and epididymides are normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender. No hepatomegaly. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has a nonhealing, indurated, ulcerated glans lesion with raised edges. What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Empiric antifungal cream and follow up in one month
Incisional or punch biopsy of the lesion
Course of empiric oral antibiotics
Topical corticosteroid for presumed balanitis
stages.explanation: A chronic, nonhealing, indurated ulcer with raised irregular edges on the glans is suspicious for malignancy, and biopsy (incisional or punch) is required for tissue diagnosis before treatment. So biopsy is correct. Empiric antifungal cream and topical steroids treat benign balanitis but dangerously delay diagnosis of a suspicious lesion. Empiric antibiotics likewise delay diagnosis; while secondary infection may be present, antibiotics will not heal a malignant ulcer. Any penile lesion that fails to heal must be biopsied rather than treated empirically.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which combination of factors in this patient most increases his risk for this malignancy?
options: Diabetes and cigarette smoking only
Older age and alcohol use only
Phimosis, poor hygiene, and HPV infection
Prior vasectomy and high testosterone
stages.explanation: Penile squamous cell carcinoma is strongly associated with phimosis (and lack of circumcision), poor genital hygiene with chronic inflammation, smegma retention, and high-risk HPV infection (especially types 16 and 18). So phimosis, poor hygiene, and HPV are the key risk combination here. Diabetes and smoking and older age and alcohol contribute to general cancer risk and smoking is a real risk factor, but the dominant, specific drivers of penile cancer are the foreskin/hygiene/HPV triad. Vasectomy and testosterone are not established risk factors for penile carcinoma.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Biopsy: invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated
HPV testing: positive for high-risk HPV-16
MRI penis: 2 cm glans lesion invading subepithelial connective tissue, no corporal invasion
FNA of right inguinal node: metastatic squamous cell carcinoma
Groin ultrasound: single enlarged right inguinal node
CT abdomen/pelvis: no distant metastases
HbA1c 8.4%
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms invasive penile squamous cell carcinoma confined to the glans. What is the most appropriate management of the primary tumor?
options: Topical 5-fluorouracil to the glans lesion
Penile-sparing surgical excision with negative margins
Observation with serial photographs
Immediate total penectomy for all invasive lesions
stages.explanation: For a small invasive penile cancer confined to the glans, organ-preserving (penile-sparing) surgery—such as wide local excision, glansectomy, or partial penectomy—achieving negative margins is preferred to maintain function while controlling the tumor. So penile-sparing surgery with negative margins is correct. Topical 5-FU is only for carcinoma in situ (Tis), not invasive disease. Observation is inappropriate for invasive cancer. Total penectomy is reserved for extensive tumors not amenable to penile-preserving surgery, not mandated for all invasive lesions, so it would overtreat this glans-confined tumor.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: FNA confirms a metastatic right inguinal node. What is the most appropriate management of the inguinal lymph nodes?
options: Inguinal lymph node dissection
Antibiotics for presumed reactive lymphadenitis
Radiation to the groin without surgery in all cases
Observation with repeat exam in three months
stages.explanation: Penile squamous cell carcinoma spreads first to the inguinal lymph nodes, and biopsy-proven nodal metastasis is an indication for therapeutic inguinal lymph node dissection, which has both staging and survival benefit. So inguinal lymph node dissection is correct. Antibiotics are wrong because FNA already proved metastasis, not infection. Radiation alone is not the standard for proven nodal disease where surgery is feasible; surgery is preferred for resectable nodes. Observation is unsafe given documented nodal metastasis, since untreated inguinal disease progresses and worsens prognosis.

cases.diagnosis: Penile squamous cell carcinoma with inguinal lymph node metastasis
pearls: Any chronic nonhealing penile ulcer—especially indurated with raised edges—must be biopsied; do not treat empirically as balanitis or infection.
Risk factors for penile SCC include phimosis, lack of circumcision, poor hygiene/smegma, chronic inflammation, smoking, and high-risk HPV (16/18).
Small invasive penile cancers can be treated with penile-sparing surgery with negative margins; topical 5-FU is only for carcinoma in situ.
Penile cancer spreads to inguinal nodes first; biopsy-proven nodal metastasis warrants therapeutic inguinal lymph node dissection.

cases.title: Rapidly Spreading Genital Pain and Swelling in the ICU
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe, rapidly worsening scrotal and perineal pain with swelling and a foul odor.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes is admitted to the ICU after presenting with 36 hours of escalating scrotal and perineal pain, swelling, redness, and a foul-smelling discharge. The pain is described as far out of proportion to early skin findings. He recently had a perianal abscess that was incompletely treated. He reports fevers, rigors, and progressive confusion over the last several hours. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 11.2%) on metformin and glipizide, obesity, and hypertension; no allergies. He drinks alcohol heavily and smokes. He lives alone and delayed care. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for malaise, nausea, and decreased urine output. On arrival he was hypotensive and tachycardic, requiring fluid resuscitation and now norepinephrine. He has no recent urinary instrumentation but has long-standing poor glycemic control. He appears toxic and lethargic, and family reports he was 'fine two days ago' before the rapid deterioration.
cases.exam: Toxic-appearing, lethargic man on vasopressors. Genital/perineal exam shows markedly edematous, dusky, and erythematous scrotal and perineal skin with patchy gray-black areas of necrosis and overlying skin blistering. Palpable subcutaneous crepitus extends from the scrotum onto the perineum and lower abdominal wall. Tenderness is severe and out of proportion early, with reduced sensation over necrotic areas. Foul, 'dishwater' malodorous discharge is present. Abdomen is mildly distended; no peritonitis. Mottled extremities and delayed capillary refill consistent with shock.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Crepitus, dusky necrotic skin, pain out of proportion, and septic shock in a diabetic point to a surgical emergency. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: CT pelvis first to delineate anatomy before any surgery
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the primary treatment
Urgent surgical debridement of necrotic tissue
Broad-spectrum antibiotics alone with close monitoring
stages.explanation: This is Fournier gangrene, a necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum and a surgical emergency; the single most important, life-saving intervention is urgent, aggressive surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue, alongside resuscitation and antibiotics. So emergent debridement is correct. Obtaining CT first must not delay surgery in a crashing patient with obvious clinical necrotizing infection—imaging is adjunctive. Hyperbaric oxygen is at most an adjunct and never replaces debridement. Antibiotics alone are necessary but insufficient; without removing necrotic tissue the infection progresses and the patient dies. Surgery is the cornerstone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While preparing for the operating room, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?
options: Oral metronidazole alone
Single-agent ceftriaxone
Narrow-spectrum penicillin only
Broad-spectrum coverage including anaerobes (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus clindamycin)
stages.explanation: Fournier gangrene is typically polymicrobial (gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms), so broad-spectrum coverage is essential—for example piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin (for MRSA) plus clindamycin (which suppresses toxin production). So broad-spectrum combination therapy is correct. Oral metronidazole alone covers only anaerobes and is wrong in septic shock. Single-agent ceftriaxone lacks anaerobic and MRSA coverage. Narrow-spectrum penicillin alone is inadequate for this polymicrobial, life-threatening infection. Antibiotics support but do not replace surgical debridement.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 23.6 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Glucose 412 mg/dL
Lactate 5.8 mmol/L
Na 130 mEq/L, K 5.2 mEq/L
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
CRP 280 mg/L
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL
CT (obtained after stabilization): subcutaneous gas tracking along perineal/scrotal fascial planes
Blood cultures: pending
ABG: pH 7.28

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the first debridement, the wound bed still shows areas of questionable viability. What is the most appropriate plan?
options: Planned serial re-exploration and repeat debridement as needed
Close the wound primarily and discharge to the floor
Stop antibiotics now that gross necrosis is removed
Defer further surgery and observe for one week
stages.explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis requires planned serial second-look operations with repeat debridement every 24-48 hours until all nonviable tissue is removed and the wound bed is clean, because necrosis often progresses beyond what is initially appreciated. So planned re-exploration is correct. Primary closure and discharge is dangerous with questionable tissue viability and ongoing critical illness. Stopping antibiotics is wrong while infection and necrosis persist. Deferring surgery for a week allows lethal progression; the principle is aggressive, repeated debridement, not watchful waiting.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which underlying factor most contributed to this patient's risk and must be optimized during his ICU course?
options: Mild seasonal allergies
Poorly controlled diabetes mellitus
Occasional caffeine intake
A remote childhood appendectomy
stages.explanation: Diabetes mellitus—especially poorly controlled, as evidenced by an HbA1c of 11.2% and glucose of 412 mg/dL—is the leading risk factor for Fournier gangrene, impairing immune function and microvascular perfusion; tight glycemic control is an essential part of management. So poorly controlled diabetes is correct. Seasonal allergies, caffeine intake, and a remote appendectomy are unrelated to necrotizing fasciitis risk. Other contributors here include obesity, alcohol use, and the recent perianal abscess, but diabetes is the dominant modifiable risk factor to optimize.

cases.diagnosis: Fournier gangrene (necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum)
pearls: Fournier gangrene is a surgical emergency: pain out of proportion, crepitus, dusky/necrotic skin, and rapid sepsis in a diabetic—do not delay debridement for imaging.
Treatment is emergent and repeated surgical debridement plus broad-spectrum (polymicrobial) antibiotics and aggressive resuscitation; antibiotics alone are insufficient.
The infection is typically polymicrobial; cover gram-positives (including MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes, and add clindamycin for toxin suppression.
Diabetes is the leading risk factor; serial second-look debridements and glycemic control are key to survival.

cases.title: Critically Ill Diabetic With Fever and Flank Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: High fever, severe right flank pain, and confusion in a poorly controlled diabetic.
cases.history: A 61-year-old woman with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with three days of high fever, rigors, severe right flank pain, nausea, vomiting, and progressive confusion. She has had dysuria and decreased urine output. She was started on oral antibiotics two days ago for a presumed urinary tract infection without improvement and has deteriorated. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 12.0%), chronic kidney disease stage 3, and hypertension; medications include insulin (poorly adherent), metformin, and lisinopril; no allergies. She lives with her daughter, does not smoke, and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is notable for diabetes. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, anorexia, and lightheadedness. On arrival she is hypotensive and tachycardic and is transferred to the ICU. She has no history of nephrolithiasis or urinary instrumentation. Her daughter reports she has been increasingly drowsy and 'not herself' over the past day, with poor oral intake and only small amounts of dark urine.
cases.exam: Acutely ill, lethargic, and confused (oriented to person only). Dry mucous membranes. Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness with guarding. Abdomen soft with right-sided tenderness; no rebound. No suprapubic mass. No flank crepitus appreciated on exam. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia, clear lungs. Extremities warm initially then mottled; capillary refill delayed. No focal neurologic deficits aside from altered mental status consistent with sepsis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has septic shock and pyelonephritis not responding to oral antibiotics. After resuscitation and IV antibiotics, what imaging best identifies a complicated, life-threatening renal infection?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
Renal ultrasound alone
CT of the abdomen and pelvis
Voiding cystourethrogram
stages.explanation: In a septic diabetic with pyelonephritis failing therapy, CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the imaging modality of choice to detect complicated infection, including gas within the renal parenchyma (emphysematous pyelonephritis), abscess, or obstruction. So CT is correct. A plain KUB may occasionally show gas but is far less sensitive and cannot characterize the extent. Renal ultrasound is operator-dependent and can miss or poorly characterize intrarenal gas and parenchymal destruction. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates vesicoureteral reflux, not acute necrotizing renal infection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 21.8 ×10³/µL
Glucose 468 mg/dL
Lactate 4.6 mmol/L
Creatinine 3.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.6)
Platelets 96 ×10³/µL
Urinalysis: pyuria, gram-negative rods
CT abdomen/pelvis: gas within the right renal parenchyma extending into the collecting system; areas of parenchymal destruction; no fluid-filled abscess; no ureteral obstructing stone
Blood and urine cultures: pending (likely E. coli)
ABG: pH 7.26

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT shows gas within the right renal parenchyma extending into the collecting system in this poorly controlled diabetic. What is the diagnosis?
options: Simple acute pyelonephritis
Emphysematous pyelonephritis
Renal cell carcinoma with necrosis
Obstructing ureteral calculus with hydronephrosis
stages.explanation: Gas within the renal parenchyma in a poorly controlled diabetic with severe sepsis is emphysematous pyelonephritis, a necrotizing, gas-forming infection usually caused by E. coli or Klebsiella. So emphysematous pyelonephritis is correct. Simple acute pyelonephritis does not produce parenchymal gas and would not cause this fulminant course. RCC with necrosis is a solid enhancing mass, not a diffuse gas-forming infection. An obstructing calculus causes hydronephrosis, but the CT explicitly shows no obstructing stone and instead shows parenchymal gas—the hallmark of emphysematous pyelonephritis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is in septic shock with gas confined largely to the collecting system and limited parenchymal involvement. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate emergency nephrectomy for all cases
Oral antibiotics and discharge once afebrile
Resuscitation, IV antibiotics, glycemic control, and percutaneous drainage/nephrostomy
Observation off antibiotics to monitor progression
stages.explanation: Modern management of emphysematous pyelonephritis emphasizes aggressive resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, glycemic control, and kidney-preserving percutaneous drainage (nephrostomy/catheter drainage) when feasible, reserving nephrectomy for those who fail or have extensive parenchymal destruction. So resuscitation plus drainage is correct. Immediate nephrectomy in all cases is outdated; many kidneys are salvaged with drainage. Oral antibiotics and discharge are dangerously inadequate in septic shock. Observation off antibiotics is grossly negligent in a necrotizing infection and would be rapidly fatal.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite drainage and IV antibiotics, she remains in refractory shock with extensive parenchymal destruction and gas on repeat imaging. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Emergency nephrectomy
Switch to oral step-down antibiotics
Hyperbaric oxygen as definitive therapy
Continue current management unchanged
stages.explanation: When emphysematous pyelonephritis fails percutaneous drainage and antibiotics—persistent shock with extensive parenchymal destruction—emergency nephrectomy is the definitive life-saving procedure to remove the necrotic, gas-forming source. So nephrectomy is correct. Switching to oral antibiotics is inappropriate in a deteriorating, unstable patient. Hyperbaric oxygen is not definitive therapy for this condition and would not control the source. Continuing unchanged management in a patient failing therapy in refractory shock risks death; source control with nephrectomy is required.

cases.diagnosis: Emphysematous pyelonephritis in poorly controlled diabetes
pearls: Emphysematous pyelonephritis is a necrotizing, gas-forming renal infection occurring almost exclusively in poorly controlled diabetics; E. coli and Klebsiella are the usual organisms.
CT is the diagnostic test of choice, showing gas within the renal parenchyma and/or collecting system.
First-line management is resuscitation, IV antibiotics, glycemic control, and kidney-preserving percutaneous drainage when feasible.
Reserve emergency nephrectomy for patients who fail drainage or have extensive parenchymal destruction with refractory sepsis.

cases.title: Persistent Fever Despite Antibiotics for a Kidney Infection
cases.chiefComplaint: Ongoing fever and flank pain after several days of treatment for a kidney infection.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man was admitted four days ago with right pyelonephritis and started on IV ceftriaxone. Despite appropriate antibiotics, he continues to have daily fevers up to 39°C, persistent right flank pain, malaise, and night sweats. He initially had dysuria and frequency, now improved, but his flank pain has not abated. PMH includes type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and nephrolithiasis with a prior right ureteral stone; no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as a teacher. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for anorexia and a 2 kg weight loss this week. Blood cultures from admission grew E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone. He has been adherent to therapy and his glucose has been moderately controlled in-hospital. He has had no urinary instrumentation during this admission. The team is puzzled that he is not improving despite correct, susceptibility-guided antibiotics, prompting reassessment for a complication.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but not toxic. Persistent marked right costovertebral angle tenderness and a sense of fullness in the right flank. Abdomen soft with right upper quadrant/flank tenderness; no rebound or guarding. No suprapubic tenderness. No flank crepitus or skin changes. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No peripheral edema. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has persistent fever and flank pain despite 4 days of appropriate IV antibiotics for E. coli pyelonephritis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Broaden antibiotics empirically without imaging
Cross-sectional imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast)
Discharge on oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Stop antibiotics and observe for spontaneous resolution
stages.explanation: Failure to improve after several days of culture-appropriate antibiotics for pyelonephritis should prompt cross-sectional imaging (contrast CT of the abdomen/pelvis) to evaluate for a complication such as a renal or perinephric abscess, obstruction, or stone. So CT imaging is correct. Broadening antibiotics empirically ignores that the organism is already susceptible and that a drainable collection may be the problem. Discharge is unsafe in a persistently febrile patient. Stopping antibiotics is dangerous in ongoing infection. The key teaching point is that persistent sepsis despite correct antibiotics signals undrained source.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL (persistently elevated)
Blood cultures (admission): E. coli, ceftriaxone-sensitive
Urinalysis: improving pyuria
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: 5 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the right perinephric space extending beyond the renal capsule into the perirenal fat
No obstructing ureteral stone; mild perinephric fat stranding
Glucose 188 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT shows a 5 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the perinephric space extending beyond the renal capsule. What is the diagnosis?
options: Simple renal cyst
Perinephric abscess
Renal cell carcinoma
Resolving uncomplicated pyelonephritis
stages.explanation: A rim-enhancing fluid collection in the perinephric space extending beyond the renal capsule, in a patient with persistent fever despite antibiotics, is a perinephric abscess—often from rupture of a renal cortical infection into the perinephric fat. So perinephric abscess is correct. A simple renal cyst is thin-walled, nonenhancing, and asymptomatic, not rim-enhancing with fever. RCC is a solid enhancing mass, not a rim-enhancing fluid collection with surrounding inflammation. Resolving uncomplicated pyelonephritis would show clinical improvement and no organized fluid collection, which is incompatible with this clinical and imaging picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has a 5 cm perinephric abscess and persistent sepsis. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue IV antibiotics alone for two more weeks
Image-guided percutaneous drainage plus IV antibiotics
Immediate nephrectomy
Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge
stages.explanation: A perinephric abscess of this size with ongoing sepsis requires source control: image-guided percutaneous (or surgical) drainage combined with continued IV antibiotics is the standard of care. So drainage plus antibiotics is correct. Antibiotics alone often fail for a sizable organized abscess because antibiotics penetrate collections poorly—the patient is already failing antibiotics. Immediate nephrectomy is excessive first-line; it is reserved for a nonfunctioning, extensively destroyed kidney or failed drainage. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharge is unsafe with persistent sepsis and an undrained abscess.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful percutaneous drainage and a course of IV antibiotics, what is the most appropriate disposition plan?
options: Transition to appropriate oral antibiotics with imaging follow-up to confirm resolution
Stop all antibiotics immediately after drain placement
Keep the drain indefinitely with no antibiotics
Schedule elective nephrectomy regardless of response
stages.explanation: Once the abscess is drained and the patient improves (defervescence, falling WBC), the standard course is to complete therapy with culture-guided oral antibiotics and obtain follow-up imaging to confirm resolution before drain removal. So oral antibiotics with imaging follow-up is correct. Stopping all antibiotics right after drainage risks relapse since infection control requires continued therapy. Keeping a drain indefinitely without antibiotics is improper management. Elective nephrectomy regardless of response is unnecessary when the kidney recovers after drainage and antibiotics.

cases.diagnosis: Perinephric abscess complicating pyelonephritis
pearls: Persistent fever or flank pain after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics for pyelonephritis should prompt imaging to look for an abscess or obstruction.
A perinephric abscess is a rim-enhancing fluid collection extending beyond the renal capsule into perinephric fat; diabetes and nephrolithiasis are risk factors.
Source control with image-guided percutaneous drainage plus IV antibiotics is the cornerstone of treatment; antibiotics alone often fail for larger collections.
Nephrectomy is reserved for a nonfunctioning, severely destroyed kidney or when drainage fails; confirm resolution with follow-up imaging.

cases.title: Recurrent UTIs and Dull Flank Ache in a 49-Year-Old Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of recurrent urinary tract infections with persistent left-sided flank discomfort.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman presents to the urology clinic for recurrent urinary tract infections. Over the past nine months she has had five culture-proven UTIs, several treated with courses of antibiotics by her primary care doctor without lasting relief. She describes a constant dull ache in the left flank, occasional cloudy and foul-smelling urine, and intermittent low-grade fevers. She denies severe colicky pain or visible blood in the urine. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus (metformin 1000 mg twice daily) and prior neurogenic bladder dysfunction with incomplete emptying. She had one prior febrile UTI requiring hospitalization three years ago. Medications also include lisinopril 10 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker (10 pack-years, quit five years ago), drinks alcohol rarely, and works as a school administrator. Family history is notable for kidney stones in her father. She reports adequate fluid intake but admits her UTIs never fully clear on culture even after treatment, and her urine often smells strongly of ammonia.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no acute distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender without palpable masses. There is mild left costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion; the right CVA is nontender. No suprapubic tenderness or fullness. No peripheral edema. Genitourinary exam is unremarkable with no vaginal discharge. Skin is warm and dry without rash. Neurologic exam shows intact lower-extremity strength and sensation. There are no signs of sepsis, and the remainder of the cardiopulmonary exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test will most directly characterize this patient's recurrent infections and flank pain?
options: Cystoscopy under anesthesia
Urinalysis with urine culture and pH
Voiding cystourethrogram
Serum PSA measurement
stages.explanation: Urinalysis with culture and urine pH is the correct first step: it confirms infection, identifies the organism, and an alkaline pH (>7.0) strongly suggests a urease-producing organism driving struvite stone formation, directly tying together her recurrent UTIs and flank pain. Cystoscopy is invasive and not a first-line characterization for recurrent UTI with flank pain. Voiding cystourethrogram evaluates reflux, which is not the leading concern in an adult with these features. Serum PSA is irrelevant in a woman and does not evaluate the urinary tract for infection or stone disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: pH 8.2
Leukocyte esterase positive, nitrite positive
WBC 50–100/hpf, many bacteria
Struvite (coffin-lid) crystals present
Urine culture: Proteus mirabilis >100,000 CFU/mL
Serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Noncontrast CT abdomen/pelvis: branched calculus filling the left renal pelvis and extending into two calyces with moderate hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely composition of this patient's branched renal calculus?
options: Calcium oxalate monohydrate
Uric acid
Magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite)
Cystine
stages.explanation: Magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite) is correct. Proteus produces urease, which splits urea into ammonia and CO2, raising urine pH and precipitating struvite, classically forming large branched 'staghorn' calculi with coffin-lid crystals, exactly as seen here. Calcium oxalate stones form in acidic-to-neutral urine and are not associated with urease-producing infection. Uric acid stones form in persistently acidic urine and are radiolucent, not branched infection stones. Cystine stones occur in cystinuria, present with hexagonal crystals and a positive nitroprusside test, and are not driven by Proteus infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management of this large branched left renal calculus?
options: Long-term suppressive antibiotics alone
Shock wave lithotripsy as monotherapy
Percutaneous nephrolithotomy
Increased oral hydration and observation
stages.explanation: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) is the guideline-recommended first-line definitive treatment for staghorn calculi because complete stone clearance is essential to eradicate the infection nidus. Suppressive antibiotics alone cannot cure infection while the stone harbors organisms and only delays definitive care. Shock wave lithotripsy as monotherapy has poor clearance rates for large branched stones and risks leaving fragments that perpetuate infection. Hydration and observation are inadequate for a large staghorn stone and risk progressive renal damage, urosepsis, and loss of function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this staghorn calculus is left untreated, which complication is she at greatest long-term risk of developing?
options: Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis and renal destruction
Renal cell carcinoma of the contralateral kidney
Polycystic kidney disease
Bladder squamous metaplasia
stages.explanation: Untreated staghorn calculi cause chronic obstruction and persistent infection, predisposing to xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis, progressive renal parenchymal destruction, abscess, urosepsis, and eventual loss of the kidney. Renal cell carcinoma is not caused by struvite stones and would not be driven into the contralateral kidney by them. Polycystic kidney disease is a hereditary disorder unrelated to stone disease. Bladder squamous metaplasia is associated with chronic catheterization or schistosomiasis, not with an obstructing renal staghorn calculus.

cases.diagnosis: Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) staghorn calculus from urease-producing Proteus mirabilis infection
pearls: Urease-producing organisms (Proteus, Klebsiella, Staphylococcus saprophyticus) alkalinize urine and form branched struvite staghorn calculi.
Persistently alkaline urine (pH >7) with recurrent UTIs and coffin-lid crystals should raise suspicion for struvite stones.
Complete stone removal—typically via percutaneous nephrolithotomy—is essential because residual fragments perpetuate infection.
Untreated staghorn calculi can lead to xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis, sepsis, and loss of the kidney.

cases.title: Recurrent Renal Colic in a 55-Year-Old Man with Joint Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated episodes of right flank pain and a history of passing gravel-like stones.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to clinic for evaluation of recurrent kidney stones. Over the past two years he has had four episodes of right-sided colicky flank pain radiating to the groin, several with gross hematuria, and he has passed small reddish-brown stones at home. He has a long history of gout, with painful first-toe attacks roughly twice a year, managed intermittently. Past medical history also includes obesity (BMI 34), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. Medications include allopurinol 100 mg daily (poorly adherent), hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks 3–4 beers on weekends, eats a diet high in red meat and organ meats, and reports low water intake. He works as a long-haul truck driver and spends most of the day sweating in his cab with limited bathroom access. Family history includes gout in his brother and kidney stones in his mother. He has not had a metabolic stone workup before and wonders why a prior CT did not show a stone he clearly passed.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight and comfortable at rest. Abdomen is obese, soft, and nontender without guarding or rebound. There is mild right costovertebral angle tenderness; the left CVA is nontender. No suprapubic tenderness. The first metatarsophalangeal joint of the right foot shows a small nontender tophaceous nodule without active inflammation. There is no peripheral edema. External genitalia are normal. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. No skin rash. The remainder of the exam is within normal limits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a passed stone but an apparently negative prior plain radiograph, which imaging best explains this discrepancy?
options: The stone was radiolucent on plain film but visible on noncontrast CT
The plain film was simply misread and the stone is radiopaque
The stone dissolved completely before imaging
Renal ultrasound is more sensitive than CT for all stones
stages.explanation: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on plain abdominal radiographs (KUB) but are readily seen on noncontrast CT, which detects nearly all stone types by density. This explains a 'negative' x-ray despite a passed stone. A misread radiopaque stone is less likely given the clinical picture of gout and acidic urine favoring uric acid. Stones do not simply dissolve spontaneously without urinary alkalinization therapy. Ultrasound is operator-dependent and less sensitive than CT, especially for ureteral stones, so it is not more sensitive than CT for all stones.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: pH 5.0
Urate (rhomboid) crystals present
No bacteria, leukocyte esterase negative
Serum uric acid 9.4 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Noncontrast CT: 6 mm right mid-ureteral stone, Hounsfield density ~450 HU; KUB obtained same day shows no calcification
24-hour urine: volume 1.0 L/day, uric acid elevated, pH 5.1

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely stone composition in this patient?
options: Calcium phosphate
Struvite
Uric acid
Cystine
stages.explanation: Uric acid is correct: a radiolucent stone (invisible on KUB but visible on CT with relatively low Hounsfield density), persistently acidic urine (pH ~5), urate crystals, hyperuricemia, and gout together point strongly to uric acid lithiasis. Calcium phosphate stones are radiopaque and form in alkaline urine. Struvite forms in alkaline, infected urine with urease-producing organisms, which is absent here. Cystine stones occur in cystinuria with hexagonal crystals and a positive nitroprusside test, typically presenting in childhood, not in this gouty middle-aged man with acidic urine.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond hydration, what is the most appropriate medical therapy to dissolve and prevent recurrence of this stone type?
options: Potassium citrate to alkalinize urine to pH 6.5–7.0
Ammonium chloride to acidify the urine
Thiazide diuretic uptitration
Long-term ciprofloxacin suppression
stages.explanation: Potassium citrate alkalinizes the urine toward pH 6.5–7.0, dramatically increasing uric acid solubility and enabling dissolution and prevention—the cornerstone of uric acid stone management along with hydration and allopurinol for hyperuricemia. Ammonium chloride acidifies urine and would worsen uric acid precipitation. Thiazides reduce calcium excretion for calcium stones and would not help uric acid stones; his HCTZ may even contribute to hyperuricemia. Long-term ciprofloxacin treats infection-related (struvite) stones, which this patient does not have.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional medication adjustment best targets the underlying metabolic driver of his stones and gout?
options: Discontinue metformin
Optimize allopurinol dosing and improve adherence
Add colchicine daily indefinitely
Start febuxostat without checking adherence or labs
stages.explanation: Optimizing allopurinol (a xanthine oxidase inhibitor) and ensuring adherence lowers serum and urinary uric acid, addressing both gout and uric acid stone formation; his uric acid is elevated and he is poorly adherent on a low dose. Discontinuing metformin does not affect urate metabolism and would worsen diabetes control. Daily colchicine prevents gout flares but does not lower uric acid or dissolve stones. Switching to febuxostat without first addressing adherence and rechecking labs is premature and bypasses optimizing first-line therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Uric acid nephrolithiasis (radiolucent stone) in the setting of gout and persistently acidic urine
pearls: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on KUB but visible on noncontrast CT (low Hounsfield density).
Persistently acidic urine (pH <5.5) is the key driver; alkalinization with potassium citrate to pH 6.5–7.0 can dissolve them.
Hydration, urinary alkalinization, and xanthine oxidase inhibition (allopurinol) are the pillars of management.
Look for associated conditions: gout, metabolic syndrome, chronic diarrhea, and tumor lysis can all promote uric acid stones.

cases.title: Lifelong Recurrent Kidney Stones in a 17-Year-Old Girl
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent kidney stones since early childhood with another recent episode of flank pain.
cases.history: A 17-year-old girl is referred to urology for recurrent nephrolithiasis. She passed her first stone at age 6 and has had at least eight stone episodes since, including two requiring ureteroscopic intervention and one shock wave lithotripsy. Episodes feature severe colicky flank pain, nausea, and occasional gross hematuria. She is otherwise healthy with no diabetes, gout, or bowel disease. She takes no daily medications and has no known drug allergies. She drinks limited water and consumes a typical teenage diet. She is a competitive swimmer. Family history is striking: a younger brother also forms stones beginning in childhood, and her parents are first cousins. There is no family history of gout. On reviewing prior records, several of her stones were described as faintly radiopaque and difficult to fragment with lithotripsy. She has never had a formal metabolic stone analysis and is frustrated by how early and how often her stones recur compared with peers, prompting genetic and metabolic evaluation.
cases.exam: The patient is a well-developed, well-nourished adolescent in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender without masses or organomegaly. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally today. No suprapubic tenderness. External genitalia are normal for age. Skin is clear without rash. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Growth and development are appropriate for age. There are no dysmorphic features. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly suggests a hereditary, single-gene cause of her stones?
options: Her competitive swimming and low fluid intake
Childhood onset, recurrence, affected sibling, and consanguineous parents
Faintly radiopaque stones on imaging
History of gross hematuria during episodes
stages.explanation: Early childhood onset, frequent recurrence, an affected sibling, and consanguinity point to an autosomal recessive inherited stone disease such as cystinuria. Low fluid intake and athletics can promote stones but are nonspecific and present in many patients without a genetic disorder. Faint radiopacity is a clue to composition but is not itself proof of a hereditary cause. Gross hematuria is common to nearly all stone episodes regardless of etiology and does not indicate a genetic mechanism.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: pH 6.0
Hexagonal crystals on microscopy
Urinary cyanide-nitroprusside test: positive
24-hour urine cystine: markedly elevated
Serum creatinine 0.7 mg/dL
Noncontrast CT: bilateral renal stones, faintly opaque, largest 8 mm in left kidney
Stone analysis (prior fragment): cystine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying defect responsible for this patient's stone disease?
options: Defective intestinal and renal tubular transport of dibasic amino acids
Overproduction of uric acid from purine metabolism
Distal renal tubular acidosis with alkaline urine
Chronic urease-producing bacterial infection
stages.explanation: Cystinuria results from an autosomal recessive defect in the renal tubular and intestinal transporter for dibasic amino acids (cystine, ornithine, lysine, arginine), causing excess urinary cystine that precipitates into hexagonal crystals and stones, confirmed by the positive nitroprusside test. Uric acid overproduction causes uric acid stones in acidic urine, not cystine stones. Distal RTA causes calcium phosphate stones in alkaline urine. Urease-producing infection causes struvite stones, none of which explain hexagonal crystals or a positive nitroprusside test.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line preventive therapy for this patient?
options: Allopurinol and a low-purine diet
High fluid intake, urinary alkalinization, and sodium restriction
Thiazide diuretic and calcium restriction
Long-term suppressive antibiotics
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for cystinuria is aggressive hydration to keep urine dilute, urinary alkalinization with potassium citrate (cystine is more soluble at pH >7), and dietary sodium restriction (which reduces cystine excretion). Allopurinol and a low-purine diet target uric acid stones, not cystine. Thiazides and calcium restriction address calcium stones, not cystine. Suppressive antibiotics treat infection-related struvite stones. If conservative measures fail, thiol-binding agents such as tiopronin or penicillamine are added.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If high fluids, alkalinization, and sodium restriction fail to control stone formation, what is the next appropriate pharmacologic step?
options: Add a thiol-binding agent such as tiopronin
Start ammonium chloride to acidify urine
Begin lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
Add febuxostat
stages.explanation: When conservative measures fail, thiol-binding (chelating) agents such as tiopronin or D-penicillamine convert cystine into a more soluble mixed disulfide, markedly reducing free urinary cystine and stone formation. Ammonium chloride acidifies urine and would decrease cystine solubility, worsening stones. Prophylactic antibiotics address infection stones, not cystinuria. Febuxostat lowers uric acid and has no role in cystine stone prevention. Thiol agents require monitoring for side effects but are the established escalation step.

cases.diagnosis: Cystine nephrolithiasis due to cystinuria (autosomal recessive dibasic amino acid transport defect)
pearls: Suspect cystinuria in childhood-onset, recurrent stones, especially with an affected sibling or consanguinity.
Hexagonal crystals and a positive urinary cyanide-nitroprusside test are classic diagnostic clues.
Cystine stones are only faintly radiopaque and notoriously resistant to shock wave lithotripsy.
Management: high fluids, urinary alkalinization (pH >7), sodium restriction; escalate to thiol-binding agents (tiopronin/penicillamine).

cases.title: Multiple Stone Episodes in a 45-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Referral for prevention after a third episode of kidney stones in two years.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents for a metabolic stone evaluation after his third symptomatic kidney stone in two years. Each episode caused severe right or left colicky flank pain radiating to the groin with hematuria; two stones passed spontaneously and one required ureteroscopy. A prior captured stone was analyzed as calcium oxalate. He is otherwise healthy, with no gout, inflammatory bowel disease, or prior bariatric surgery. Medications are none, and he has no known drug allergies. His diet is high in sodium and animal protein, he drinks several caffeinated sodas daily, eats spinach and nuts frequently, and reports drinking little plain water. He works in construction and sweats heavily without replacing fluids. He drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. Family history includes kidney stones in his father and a paternal uncle. He is motivated to prevent further stones and wants to know what dietary and medical changes will help, and whether he needs to drastically cut his dietary calcium intake to avoid more stones.
cases.exam: The patient is a muscular, well-nourished man in no distress. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without masses or organomegaly. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally today. No suprapubic tenderness. External genitalia are normal. No peripheral edema. Skin is warm and dry without tophi or rash. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable, consistent with an asymptomatic interval between stone episodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this recurrent calcium oxalate stone former?
options: Empiric thiazide without further testing
A 24-hour urine collection and serum chemistries
Immediate parathyroidectomy referral
Restrict dietary calcium to near zero
stages.explanation: A 24-hour urine collection (volume, calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, sodium, pH) plus serum chemistries (including calcium) is the cornerstone of recurrent stone evaluation, identifying treatable abnormalities such as hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia, or hyperoxaluria. Empiric thiazide without testing skips diagnosis and may be inappropriate. Parathyroidectomy referral is premature without documented hypercalcemia and confirmed primary hyperparathyroidism. Severe calcium restriction is counterproductive—it increases oxalate absorption and bone loss and is not recommended for stone prevention.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
24-hour urine: volume 1.1 L/day (low)
Urine calcium 380 mg/day (elevated)
Urine citrate low
Urine oxalate mildly elevated
Urine sodium high
Serum calcium 9.6 mg/dL (normal)
Serum PTH normal
Urinalysis: pH 6.0, calcium oxalate (envelope-shaped) crystals

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given normal serum calcium and PTH with elevated urine calcium, what is the most likely metabolic diagnosis?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Idiopathic hypercalciuria
Distal renal tubular acidosis
Enteric hyperoxaluria
stages.explanation: Idiopathic hypercalciuria—elevated urinary calcium with normal serum calcium and PTH—is the most common metabolic abnormality in calcium oxalate stone formers and fits this profile. Primary hyperparathyroidism would show elevated serum calcium and inappropriately high PTH, both normal here. Distal RTA causes calcium phosphate stones with alkaline urine and a metabolic acidosis, not the acidic urine and oxalate crystals seen here. Enteric hyperoxaluria follows malabsorptive states (IBD, bypass surgery), which this patient does not have, and would feature markedly elevated oxalate as the dominant abnormality.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication is most appropriate to reduce urinary calcium and prevent recurrent calcium oxalate stones?
options: Furosemide
Allopurinol
A thiazide diuretic
Acetazolamide
stages.explanation: Thiazide diuretics enhance distal tubular calcium reabsorption, lowering urinary calcium and reducing stone recurrence in idiopathic hypercalciuria. Furosemide, a loop diuretic, actually increases urinary calcium excretion and would worsen stone risk. Allopurinol reduces uric acid and is reserved for hyperuricosuric calcium stone formers, not the primary therapy for hypercalciuria. Acetazolamide promotes an alkaline urine and bicarbonaturia, increasing calcium phosphate stone risk, and is not used for calcium oxalate stone prevention.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which dietary counseling is most appropriate for this patient?
options: Increase fluids, reduce sodium and animal protein, and maintain normal dietary calcium
Eliminate all dietary calcium
Increase oxalate-rich foods to build tolerance
Restrict fluids to concentrate the urine
stages.explanation: The correct counseling is to increase fluid intake (goal urine output >2–2.5 L/day), reduce dietary sodium and animal protein (both raise urinary calcium), and maintain normal dietary calcium, which binds gut oxalate and reduces oxalate absorption. Eliminating dietary calcium paradoxically increases oxalate absorption and stone risk and harms bone. Increasing oxalate intake raises urinary oxalate and stone formation. Restricting fluids concentrates the urine and directly promotes stone formation—the opposite of what is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis due to idiopathic hypercalciuria with low urine volume, hypocitraturia, and high sodium intake
pearls: A 24-hour urine study guides targeted prevention in recurrent calcium stone formers.
Idiopathic hypercalciuria has high urine calcium with normal serum calcium and PTH; thiazides lower urinary calcium.
Maintain—do not restrict—dietary calcium; low-calcium diets increase oxalate absorption and stone risk.
Increase fluids and reduce dietary sodium and animal protein to lower urinary calcium and raise citrate.

cases.title: Gross Hematuria and Flank Pain After a Car Crash in a 22-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Left flank pain and visibly bloody urine after a high-speed motor vehicle collision.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which he was the restrained driver struck on the left side. He complains of severe left flank and upper-quadrant pain and reports that his first void after the crash was grossly bloody. He denies loss of consciousness and has no chest or extremity deformity. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not use tobacco, drinks alcohol socially, and denies recreational drug use. He is a college student and lives alone. There is no personal or family history of bleeding disorders or kidney disease. On primary survey his airway is patent, breathing is unlabored, and he is hemodynamically responsive to an initial crystalloid bolus. Trauma team has been activated. He recalls his seatbelt and door intruding into his left side on impact and notes the flank pain is worse with movement and deep breathing.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented, mildly tachycardic but responsive to fluids. There is left flank ecchymosis and tenderness to palpation over the left upper quadrant and costovertebral angle. The abdomen is soft elsewhere without rigidity or rebound. There is no pelvic instability on compression. External genitalia are normal with no blood at the urethral meatus. Rectal tone is normal and the prostate is in normal position. Chest is clear with no crepitus. Extremities have full pulses without deformity. Visible gross hematuria is noted in the Foley collection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this hemodynamically stable blunt trauma patient with gross hematuria, what is the most appropriate imaging study?
options: Renal ultrasound
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast including delayed (excretory) phase
Retrograde urethrogram
Immediate exploratory laparotomy without imaging
stages.explanation: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast and delayed excretory-phase imaging is the gold standard for staging blunt renal trauma; it grades parenchymal injury and detects urinary extravasation and vascular injury, which guide management. Ultrasound cannot reliably grade renal injuries or detect urine leaks. Retrograde urethrogram evaluates urethral injury, which is not suggested here (no blood at the meatus, normal prostate). Immediate laparotomy without imaging is reserved for hemodynamically unstable patients; this patient is stable and responds to fluids, so he should be imaged.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
WBC 11.0 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Urinalysis: gross blood, too numerous to count RBCs
CT with contrast: 2.5 cm deep laceration of the left kidney extending into the collecting system with contrast extravasation on delayed images; moderate perinephric hematoma; no active arterial blush; both kidneys perfused

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a deep laceration extending into the collecting system with urinary extravasation, what is the AAST renal injury grade?
options: Grade II
Grade III
Grade IV
Grade V
stages.explanation: A laceration extending into the collecting system with urinary extravasation is an AAST Grade IV injury. Grade II is a laceration <1 cm without collecting-system involvement or a confined perirenal hematoma. Grade III is a laceration >1 cm into the parenchyma but without collecting-system extension or extravasation. Grade V denotes a shattered kidney or main renal artery/vein avulsion with devascularization. The collecting-system extension with contrast extravasation defines Grade IV here, while preserved perfusion and no arterial blush exclude Grade V.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given this Grade IV injury in a hemodynamically stable patient, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate open nephrectomy
Nonoperative management with bed rest, serial monitoring, and repeat imaging
Bilateral nephrostomy tubes
Emergent renal angioembolization regardless of stability
stages.explanation: Hemodynamically stable patients—even with Grade IV injuries—are best managed nonoperatively with bed rest, serial hemoglobin and vital-sign monitoring, and follow-up imaging; most heal without surgery, and operative exploration risks nephrectomy. Immediate nephrectomy is reserved for uncontrolled hemorrhage or instability. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes are unnecessary with a normal contralateral kidney and no obstruction. Angioembolization is indicated for active arterial bleeding (a contrast blush) or instability, neither of which is present here, so it is not the routine initial step.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which development during observation would most strongly indicate a need to escalate to angioembolization or surgery?
options: Stable mild flank tenderness
Persistent microscopic hematuria
Hemodynamic instability with dropping hemoglobin and a new contrast blush
Low-grade fever on day one
stages.explanation: Hemodynamic instability with an ongoing hemoglobin decline and a new arterial contrast blush signals active hemorrhage and mandates escalation to angioembolization or surgery. Stable mild flank tenderness is expected after renal trauma and does not require intervention. Persistent microscopic hematuria is common during healing and does not by itself warrant surgery. A low-grade fever on the first day is a nonspecific inflammatory response to injury and is not an indication for operative escalation absent other signs.

cases.diagnosis: AAST Grade IV blunt left renal laceration with collecting-system involvement and urinary extravasation, managed nonoperatively
pearls: Hemodynamically stable blunt trauma with gross hematuria warrants CT with delayed excretory-phase imaging to stage renal injury.
AAST Grade IV renal injury = laceration into the collecting system or a contained vascular injury; Grade V = shattered kidney or pedicle avulsion.
Most blunt renal injuries, including many high-grade ones, are managed nonoperatively if the patient is stable.
Escalate to angioembolization or surgery for hemodynamic instability or an active contrast blush.

cases.title: Lower Abdominal Pain and Inability to Urinate After a Crush Injury in a 30-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe lower abdominal pain and inability to pass urine after a pelvic crush injury.
cases.history: A 30-year-old construction worker is brought to the emergency department after a loaded pallet fell across his pelvis. He has severe suprapubic and lower abdominal pain and has been unable to void since the injury despite a strong urge. He recalls a full bladder at the time of the crush. He denies head injury or loss of consciousness. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has no prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries. There is no personal or family history of bleeding disorders. On arrival the trauma team is activated; he is alert and complaining bitterly of abdominal pain that has progressively worsened and spread across the lower abdomen. He notes a single small amount of bloody urine he was able to express early on, after which he could not void at all. He reports the pain is worse with any movement and that his abdomen feels increasingly distended and tender.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and diaphoretic. There is marked suprapubic and diffuse lower abdominal tenderness with guarding and early rebound, suggesting peritoneal irritation. The pelvis is tender and feels unstable on gentle compression. There is no blood at the urethral meatus. Rectal exam shows normal tone with a normally positioned, palpable prostate. External genitalia are intact without scrotal hematoma. Lower-extremity pulses and sensation are intact. The bladder is not distended to palpation despite his inability to void. Chest is clear and the upper abdomen is soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a pelvic fracture, inability to void, but NO blood at the meatus and a normally positioned prostate, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Blind insertion of a large-bore suprapubic tube
Gentle attempt at urethral catheterization, then CT cystogram if it passes
Immediate exploratory laparotomy
Discharge with outpatient urology follow-up
stages.explanation: With no blood at the meatus and a normally positioned prostate, urethral injury is unlikely, so a gentle catheterization attempt is appropriate; if it passes, a CT cystogram (retrograde bladder filling) evaluates for bladder rupture. A blind suprapubic tube is unnecessary when a urethral catheter can be safely placed and is not the first step. Immediate laparotomy is premature before imaging characterizes the injury in a stable patient. Discharge is unsafe given a pelvic fracture, peritonism, and inability to void.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urethral catheter passes easily; returns ~80 mL grossly bloody urine
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
Pelvic x-ray: diastasis of the pubic symphysis and pubic rami fractures
CT cystogram: contrast instilled into bladder extravasates around bowel loops and into the paracolic gutters, outlining intraperitoneal structures

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cystogram shows contrast outlining bowel loops and tracking into the paracolic gutters. What is the diagnosis?
options: Extraperitoneal bladder rupture
Intraperitoneal bladder rupture
Posterior urethral disruption
Ureteral avulsion
stages.explanation: Contrast outlining bowel loops and pooling in the paracolic gutters indicates free intraperitoneal spillage—an intraperitoneal bladder rupture, classically from a blow to a full bladder causing dome rupture. Extraperitoneal rupture shows a flame-shaped contrast collection confined to the perivesical space, not tracking among bowel loops. Posterior urethral disruption presents with blood at the meatus, a high-riding prostate, and inability to pass a catheter—absent here. Ureteral avulsion would not fill from retrograde bladder instillation and is rare in blunt trauma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management of intraperitoneal bladder rupture in this patient?
options: Catheter drainage alone for 10–14 days
Surgical exploration with operative repair of the bladder
Percutaneous drain placement and observation
Ureteral stenting
stages.explanation: Intraperitoneal bladder rupture requires surgical exploration and operative (multilayer) repair because urine leaks freely into the peritoneum, causing chemical peritonitis and not healing with drainage alone. Catheter drainage alone is the treatment for uncomplicated extraperitoneal rupture, not intraperitoneal injury. A percutaneous drain does not repair the defect and leaves ongoing intraperitoneal urine leak. Ureteral stenting treats ureteral injury and has no role in repairing a ruptured bladder dome.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How does management differ if a cystogram instead shows an extraperitoneal (flame-shaped perivesical) leak without associated injuries?
options: Mandatory immediate surgical repair
Catheter drainage for ~10–14 days with follow-up cystogram
Immediate cystectomy
Bilateral nephrostomy tubes
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated extraperitoneal bladder ruptures usually heal with catheter drainage alone for about 10–14 days, followed by a confirmatory cystogram before catheter removal. Mandatory immediate surgery is not required unless there is bladder neck involvement, bone fragments in the bladder, or concurrent abdominal exploration. Cystectomy is never appropriate for a traumatic rupture. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes do not address a bladder injury and are unnecessary when a urethral catheter provides adequate drainage.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If an intraperitoneal rupture is missed, which complication is most likely to develop?
options: Urinary ascites with chemical peritonitis and rising creatinine
Renal cell carcinoma
Vesicoureteral reflux
Erectile dysfunction
stages.explanation: A missed intraperitoneal rupture allows continuous urine spillage into the peritoneal cavity, producing urinary ascites, chemical peritonitis, sepsis, and a spuriously elevated serum creatinine from peritoneal reabsorption of urinary solutes. Renal cell carcinoma is unrelated to acute bladder trauma. Vesicoureteral reflux is a congenital or anatomic ureterovesical problem, not a sequela of dome rupture. Erectile dysfunction is more associated with posterior urethral/pelvic fracture injuries, not isolated intraperitoneal bladder rupture.

cases.diagnosis: Intraperitoneal bladder rupture from blunt pelvic trauma to a full bladder, requiring operative repair
pearls: Pelvic fracture plus inability to void: assess for urethral injury (blood at meatus, high-riding prostate) before catheterizing.
CT cystogram differentiates intraperitoneal (contrast among bowel loops, paracolic gutters) from extraperitoneal (flame-shaped perivesical) rupture.
Intraperitoneal rupture requires surgical repair; uncomplicated extraperitoneal rupture is treated with catheter drainage.
Missed intraperitoneal rupture causes urinary ascites, chemical peritonitis, and a falsely elevated creatinine from peritoneal reabsorption.

cases.title: Blood at the Urethra and Inability to Void After a Pelvic Fracture in a 35-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Blood at the tip of the penis and inability to urinate after a motorcycle crash with pelvic fracture.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man arrives by ambulance after a high-speed motorcycle crash in which he struck a guardrail and sustained a pelvic injury. He reports severe pelvic and perineal pain and notes blood at the tip of his penis. He has a strong urge to urinate but is unable to void. He denies head or chest injury and was wearing a helmet. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. He works as a mechanic. There is no history of prior urologic procedures or strictures, and no family history of bleeding disorders. On arrival the trauma team is activated and he remains hemodynamically stable after an initial fluid bolus. He keeps trying to void without success and is anxious about the bleeding he sees at the urethral opening, asking the team repeatedly when he will be able to pass urine.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and in pain. There is blood at the urethral meatus. The pelvis is tender and feels unstable on gentle compression. On digital rectal exam, the prostate is high-riding (boggy and displaced superiorly) and not palpable in its normal position. There is perineal and scrotal swelling. The bladder is palpable and distended above the pubic symphysis. There is no gross blood per rectum, and rectal tone is intact. Lower-extremity pulses and sensation are preserved. The abdomen is soft above the distended bladder.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given blood at the meatus, a high-riding prostate, and a pelvic fracture, what should NOT be done, and what is the correct next step?
options: Insert a urethral Foley immediately, then image
Avoid blind catheterization and obtain a retrograde urethrogram
Discharge with oral antibiotics
Perform digital urethral dilation at the bedside
stages.explanation: Blood at the meatus, a high-riding prostate, and pelvic fracture are classic for posterior urethral injury; blind catheterization can convert a partial tear into a complete disruption and is contraindicated. The correct step is a retrograde urethrogram to define the injury. Inserting a Foley immediately risks worsening the injury and false passage. Discharge is unsafe with a likely urethral disruption and urinary retention. Bedside urethral dilation is dangerous and inappropriate in acute trauma.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Retrograde urethrogram: contrast extravasation at the membranous (posterior) urethra above the urogenital diaphragm with no contrast reaching the bladder
Pelvic x-ray: bilateral pubic rami fractures with symphyseal diastasis
Hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Bladder palpably distended; no contrast filling on RUG

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The retrograde urethrogram shows contrast extravasation at the membranous urethra with no filling of the bladder. What is the diagnosis?
options: Anterior (bulbar) urethral injury
Posterior (membranous) urethral disruption
Intraperitoneal bladder rupture
Ureteral injury
stages.explanation: Extravasation at the membranous urethra (above the urogenital diaphragm) with a high-riding prostate and pelvic fracture indicates a posterior urethral disruption, the classic injury from pelvic-fracture shearing forces. Anterior (bulbar) injuries occur below the urogenital diaphragm, typically after straddle trauma, with a butterfly perineal hematoma. Intraperitoneal bladder rupture would show contrast leaking among bowel loops on cystogram, not at the membranous urethra. Ureteral injury is not assessed by retrograde urethrography and is rare in this mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management to relieve this patient's urinary obstruction?
options: Repeated attempts at urethral Foley placement
Suprapubic catheter placement for urinary diversion
Immediate open primary urethral repair
Observation with no diversion
stages.explanation: Suprapubic catheter placement provides safe urinary diversion in posterior urethral disruption without manipulating the injured urethra, allowing the patient to drain a distended bladder.
