Repeated Foley attempts risk converting a partial to complete injury and creating false passages. Immediate open primary repair of posterior urethral disruption is generally avoided acutely because of poor outcomes (stricture, incontinence, impotence); delayed reconstruction is preferred. Observation without diversion leaves the patient in obstructive retention and is unsafe.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complications are most characteristic of posterior urethral disruption?
options: Urethral stricture, erectile dysfunction, and incontinence
Vesicoureteral reflux and recurrent pyelonephritis
Renal artery stenosis
Testicular torsion
stages.explanation: Posterior urethral disruption is associated with urethral stricture, erectile dysfunction (from injury to cavernosal nerves near the membranous urethra), and urinary incontinence—the well-known triad of late complications. Vesicoureteral reflux and recurrent pyelonephritis relate to ureterovesical anatomy, not urethral trauma. Renal artery stenosis is a vascular/renal disorder unrelated to this injury. Testicular torsion is an acute scrotal emergency unrelated to pelvic-fracture urethral disruption.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior (membranous) urethral disruption associated with pelvic fracture
pearls: Blood at the meatus, high-riding prostate, and pelvic fracture = suspected posterior urethral injury—do NOT blindly catheterize.
Retrograde urethrogram is the diagnostic study of choice for urethral injury.
Manage acutely with suprapubic diversion; definitive urethral reconstruction is delayed.
Late complications include urethral stricture, erectile dysfunction, and incontinence.

cases.title: Perineal Bruising and Painful Urination After a Fall in a 28-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Perineal pain, bruising, and blood at the urethra after falling astride a metal railing.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department after slipping while climbing a fence and falling astride a horizontal metal railing, striking his perineum directly. He has significant perineal pain, swelling, and bruising, and noticed blood at the tip of his penis. He attempted to urinate once afterward with pain and difficulty. He denies any pelvic crush mechanism, abdominal pain, or back pain. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no recreational drugs. He works as a warehouse stocker and lives with roommates. There is no prior urologic surgery or urethral instrumentation and no family history of bleeding disorders. He remains hemodynamically stable throughout the evaluation. He is mainly concerned about the worsening swelling and discoloration spreading across his scrotum and lower abdomen, and the burning sensation when he tried to pass urine.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and in moderate discomfort. There is blood at the urethral meatus. The perineum shows extensive ecchymosis, and bruising extends in a 'butterfly' distribution across the perineum and into the scrotum and lower abdominal wall, consistent with hematoma contained by Colles fascia. The pelvis is stable to compression with no bony tenderness. Digital rectal exam reveals a normally positioned, non-displaced prostate with intact rectal tone. There is no gross blood per rectum. Lower-extremity pulses and sensation are intact. The abdomen is soft and nontender above the swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a straddle mechanism with blood at the meatus but a stable pelvis and normally positioned prostate, what is the best diagnostic next step?
options: Immediate urethral catheter placement
Retrograde urethrogram
CT cystogram
Renal ultrasound
stages.explanation: Blood at the meatus after a straddle injury suggests anterior (bulbar) urethral trauma; a retrograde urethrogram is the diagnostic study of choice and must precede any catheterization. Immediate catheter placement risks worsening a urethral tear before the injury is defined. CT cystogram evaluates bladder rupture, which is less likely with this isolated perineal straddle mechanism and a stable pelvis. Renal ultrasound assesses the kidneys and would not evaluate the suspected anterior urethral injury.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Retrograde urethrogram: contrast extravasation at the bulbar (anterior) urethra below the urogenital diaphragm; some contrast reaches the bladder
Pelvic x-ray: no fracture
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Scrotal/perineal hematoma in a butterfly pattern

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The urethrogram shows contrast extravasation at the bulbar urethra below the urogenital diaphragm. What is the diagnosis?
options: Posterior (membranous) urethral disruption
Anterior (bulbar) urethral injury
Extraperitoneal bladder rupture
Renal pelvis laceration
stages.explanation: Extravasation at the bulbar urethra below the urogenital diaphragm after a straddle injury, with a butterfly perineal hematoma confined by Colles fascia, defines an anterior (bulbar) urethral injury. Posterior membranous disruption is associated with pelvic fracture and a high-riding prostate, both absent here. Extraperitoneal bladder rupture shows perivesical contrast on cystogram, not bulbar urethral extravasation. A renal pelvis laceration is a renal injury unrelated to this perineal straddle mechanism and would not be shown on a retrograde urethrogram.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this partial anterior urethral injury, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Immediate extensive open urethroplasty
Urinary diversion (suprapubic or carefully placed catheter) with delayed reassessment
Bilateral nephrostomy tubes
Discharge without urinary drainage
stages.explanation: Most anterior urethral injuries are managed with urinary diversion—suprapubic cystostomy or a gently/atraumatically placed urethral catheter—allowing the urethra to rest, with delayed evaluation for stricture. Immediate extensive urethroplasty is not performed acutely due to edema and hematoma; reconstruction is staged if a stricture develops. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes do not address a urethral injury. Discharge without drainage risks worsening extravasation, urinoma, infection, and Fournier gangrene and is unsafe.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which late complication should this patient be counseled about after a bulbar urethral injury?
options: Urethral stricture causing obstructive voiding symptoms
Vesicoureteral reflux
Renal cell carcinoma
Bladder diverticulum from detrusor failure
stages.explanation: Urethral stricture is the principal late complication of anterior (bulbar) urethral injury as the healing urethra scars, producing obstructive voiding symptoms (weak stream, straining) that may need later urethroplasty. Vesicoureteral reflux is a ureterovesical problem unrelated to anterior urethral trauma. Renal cell carcinoma is unrelated to urethral injury. A bladder diverticulum can result from chronic outlet obstruction but is not the characteristic direct sequela of an acute bulbar urethral injury, where stricture is the classic concern.

cases.diagnosis: Anterior (bulbar) urethral injury from perineal straddle trauma with butterfly hematoma
pearls: Straddle injuries damage the bulbar (anterior) urethra; pelvic-fracture shearing damages the posterior urethra.
A 'butterfly' perineal/scrotal hematoma confined by Colles fascia is the classic sign of anterior urethral injury.
Retrograde urethrogram is the diagnostic study of choice; obtain it before any catheterization.
Urethral stricture is the major late complication of anterior urethral injuries.

cases.title: Severe Scrotal Pain and Swelling After Being Kicked in a 23-Year-Old Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe right scrotal pain and rapid swelling after a direct blow during a soccer game.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man presents to the emergency department after being kicked forcefully in the scrotum during a recreational soccer match. He felt immediate severe right testicular pain followed by rapid swelling, nausea, and one episode of vomiting. The pain has not improved over the past two hours. He denies fever, dysuria, or prior testicular problems, and there is no antecedent intermittent pain to suggest torsion. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no recreational drugs. He is a graduate student and is sexually active with one partner. There is no family history of bleeding disorders or testicular tumors. He is otherwise healthy and athletic. He is very anxious about the swelling and pain and reports the scrotum has become tense, firm, and exquisitely tender, with the discomfort radiating to his lower abdomen.
cases.exam: The patient is in significant distress from pain. The right hemiscrotum is markedly swollen, tense, and ecchymotic. The right testis is exquisitely tender, and its contour is difficult to define because of a tense surrounding fluid/blood collection (hematocele). The cremasteric reflex is difficult to assess due to pain and swelling. The left testis is normal in size, lie, and consistency, with a normal cremasteric reflex and no tenderness. There is no penile discharge, no blood at the meatus, and no inguinal hernia. The abdomen is soft with mild lower-quadrant discomfort referred from the scrotum.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for this patient with blunt scrotal trauma and a tense, swollen hemiscrotum?
options: Scrotal MRI
Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound
CT abdomen/pelvis
Retrograde urethrogram
stages.explanation: Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound is the first-line study for blunt scrotal trauma; it rapidly assesses testicular integrity, the tunica albuginea, blood flow, and the presence of a hematocele, guiding the decision to explore. Scrotal MRI is more sensitive in select equivocal cases but is slower and not first-line in acute trauma. CT of the abdomen/pelvis does not adequately evaluate the testis and adds radiation without benefit here. A retrograde urethrogram evaluates urethral injury, which is not suggested by an isolated scrotal blow without meatal blood.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound: disruption of the tunica albuginea of the right testis with heterogeneous echotexture and extrusion of seminiferous tubules
Large surrounding hematocele
Reduced/absent flow in the involved portion of the right testis
Left testis: normal echotexture and normal blood flow
Hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ultrasound shows tunica albuginea disruption with a hematocele and extruded tubules. What is the diagnosis?
options: Epididymitis
Testicular rupture
Testicular torsion
Simple hydrocele
stages.explanation: Disruption of the tunica albuginea with extrusion of seminiferous tubules, heterogeneous testicular echotexture, and a hematocele after blunt trauma defines testicular rupture. Epididymitis is an infectious/inflammatory process with fever, dysuria, and increased flow—not tunica disruption after trauma. Testicular torsion shows absent flow with a twisted cord but an intact tunica and no traumatic hematocele; there was no intermittent prior pain here. A simple hydrocele is a clear, painless fluid collection without tunica disruption or blood.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of confirmed testicular rupture?
options: Scrotal support, ice, and outpatient follow-up
Emergent surgical exploration with evacuation of hematocele and tunica repair
Broad-spectrum antibiotics alone
Aspiration of the hematocele under ultrasound
stages.explanation: Confirmed testicular rupture requires emergent surgical exploration with evacuation of the hematocele, debridement of nonviable tissue, and repair of the tunica albuginea; early surgery (ideally within 72 hours) maximizes testicular salvage. Conservative management with support and ice is appropriate only for minor contusions without rupture and risks loss of the testis here. Antibiotics alone do not address the structural rupture. Needle aspiration of a hematocele does not repair the tunica and risks infection and incomplete treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the main benefit of timely surgical exploration in testicular rupture?
options: It eliminates the future risk of testicular cancer
It increases the rate of testicular salvage and preserves function
It is required to make the diagnosis, which imaging cannot suggest
It prevents contralateral testicular torsion
stages.explanation: Timely exploration (within about 72 hours) substantially increases testicular salvage rates and helps preserve hormonal and reproductive function, compared with delayed surgery or conservative management of rupture. It does not eliminate future testicular cancer risk, which is unrelated to trauma repair. Ultrasound can strongly suggest rupture preoperatively, so surgery is not the only way to diagnose it. Exploration of a ruptured testis does not prevent torsion of the opposite testis, which is a distinct condition.

cases.diagnosis: Testicular rupture with hematocele from blunt scrotal trauma, requiring emergent exploration
pearls: Color Doppler ultrasound is first-line for blunt scrotal trauma to assess tunica integrity and blood flow.
Tunica albuginea disruption with extruded seminiferous tubules and a hematocele indicates testicular rupture.
Testicular rupture is a surgical emergency—explore early (ideally within 72 hours) to maximize salvage.
A tense, painful hematocele after trauma should prompt urgent evaluation rather than conservative observation.

cases.title: Intermittent Flank Pain Worsened by Drinking in an 8-Year-Old Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of left-sided flank pain, sometimes after drinking large amounts of fluid.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother for recurrent left flank and upper abdominal pain over the past six months. The episodes are intermittent, last a few hours, and are sometimes accompanied by nausea and occasional vomiting. His mother has noticed that attacks seem worse after he drinks large amounts of fluid, such as after sports practice or chugging water. Between episodes he is completely well, active, and growing normally. He has no fever, dysuria, or hematuria, and no prior urinary tract infections. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He was born at term without complications. Immunizations are up to date. He is in second grade and doing well. Family history is unremarkable for kidney disease or stones. His mother is worried because the pain keeps recurring without explanation and seems clearly linked to episodes of high fluid intake, which prompted this referral.
cases.exam: The child is well-appearing, well-nourished, and in no distress between episodes. The abdomen is soft and nontender today, without guarding, rebound, or palpable masses; no flank mass is appreciated. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness at this visit. External genitalia are normal with both testes descended. There is no peripheral edema. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal, and the child is appropriately interactive and developmentally on track. Growth parameters track along his prior percentiles. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for intermittent flank pain and suspected obstruction in this child?
options: Noncontrast CT abdomen/pelvis
Renal and bladder ultrasound
Voiding cystourethrogram
Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
stages.explanation: Renal and bladder ultrasound is the preferred first-line imaging in children—it is radiation-free and effectively detects hydronephrosis, the hallmark of upper-tract obstruction. Noncontrast CT delivers significant radiation and is reserved for stone evaluation when ultrasound is inconclusive, not as the first study in a child. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates vesicoureteral reflux and the lower tract, not intermittent upper-tract obstruction. A plain KUB is insensitive for soft-tissue obstruction and hydronephrosis and would not characterize this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Renal/bladder ultrasound: moderate-to-severe left hydronephrosis with dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces; no ureteral dilation; normal bladder
Right kidney: normal
Urinalysis: no blood, no leukocytes, no nitrites
Creatinine 0.5 mg/dL
MAG3 diuretic renogram: delayed drainage from the left kidney with a prolonged t1/2 after furosemide; preserved differential function

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hydronephrosis of the renal pelvis without ureteral dilation, plus delayed drainage on diuretic renogram, points to obstruction at which site?
options: Ureterovesical junction
Ureteropelvic junction
Bladder outlet (posterior urethral valves)
Renal artery
stages.explanation: Pelvicalyceal dilation WITHOUT ureteral dilation, plus delayed washout on diuretic renography, localizes the block to the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ)—the classic congenital cause of pediatric hydronephrosis. Ureterovesical junction obstruction would dilate the ureter down to the bladder (hydroureteronephrosis). Posterior urethral valves cause bilateral hydronephrosis with a thick-walled bladder in boys, not isolated unilateral pelvic dilation. Renal artery pathology causes hypertension or ischemia, not an obstructive dilated collecting system with delayed drainage.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Why do this patient's episodes of flank pain worsen with high fluid intake?
options: High urine flow overwhelms the narrowed ureteropelvic junction, acutely distending the renal pelvis
Fluid directly irritates the bladder lining
Increased fluid raises blood pressure and renal artery flow
Water intoxication causes referred abdominal pain
stages.explanation: In UPJ obstruction, a brisk diuresis from high fluid intake increases urine production faster than the narrowed junction can drain, acutely distending the renal pelvis and producing intermittent (Dietl crisis–type) flank pain—exactly the pattern described. Direct bladder irritation does not explain upper-tract pelvic distension and flank pain. Increased renal artery flow does not cause obstructive flank pain. Water intoxication causes hyponatremia with neurologic symptoms, not localized obstructive flank pain triggered by drinking.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given symptomatic UPJ obstruction with delayed drainage, what is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics only
Pyeloplasty to reconstruct the ureteropelvic junction
Immediate nephrectomy
Observation with no follow-up imaging
stages.explanation: Pyeloplasty—surgical reconstruction of the narrowed ureteropelvic junction—is the definitive treatment for symptomatic UPJ obstruction with impaired drainage, relieving pain and protecting renal function with excellent success rates. Prophylactic antibiotics alone do not relieve mechanical obstruction. Nephrectomy is reserved for a poorly or non-functioning kidney, not one with preserved differential function as here. Observation without follow-up is inappropriate for symptomatic obstruction and risks progressive functional loss; even watched cases require serial imaging and renography.

cases.diagnosis: Ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) obstruction causing intermittent obstructive left hydronephrosis
pearls: UPJ obstruction is the most common congenital cause of pediatric hydronephrosis—pelvicalyceal dilation WITHOUT ureteral dilation.
Intermittent flank pain provoked by high fluid intake (Dietl crisis) is a classic clue.
Renal/bladder ultrasound is first-line; a MAG3 diuretic renogram quantifies drainage and differential function.
Pyeloplasty is the definitive repair for symptomatic or function-threatening obstruction.

cases.title: High Blood Pressure and Blood in the Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: I had some blood in my urine last week and my home blood pressure readings have been high.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman presents for evaluation of an episode of painless gross hematuria one week ago that resolved spontaneously, along with elevated home blood pressure readings averaging 150/95 mmHg over the past two months. She reports intermittent dull bilateral flank discomfort but denies dysuria, fever, weight loss, or trauma. She has no prior surgeries. Medications include only a daily multivitamin; she takes no NSAIDs or anticoagulants and has no known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and works as an accountant. Family history is striking: her father died at age 52 of a 'brain bleed' and required dialysis in his late forties, and a paternal aunt has kidney cysts. She has two children and no history of urinary tract infections or stones. Review of systems is otherwise negative apart from occasional morning headaches. She has never had her kidneys imaged and is anxious about what she might have inherited from her father.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs; there is no S3 or jugular venous distension. The abdomen is soft and non-tender, but bilateral non-tender ballotable fullness is appreciated in the flanks. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion and no abdominal bruit. There is no peripheral edema. Skin shows no rashes or telangiectasias. Neurologic exam is grossly intact with no focal deficits and no visual field cuts. There is no hepatomegaly, though mild fullness in the right upper quadrant is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the painless gross hematuria, hypertension, bilateral flank fullness, and family history of kidney disease and fatal intracranial hemorrhage, which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Cystoscopy to evaluate for bladder malignancy
Renal ultrasound
24-hour urine protein quantification
Renal biopsy
stages.explanation: Renal ultrasound is the best initial test; it is noninvasive, inexpensive, and highly sensitive for the multiple bilateral cysts that define autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), which the family history and bilateral flank fullness strongly suggest. Cystoscopy targets lower-tract sources of hematuria and is unnecessary in a 40-year-old with a clear upper-tract clinical picture and no smoking risk. A 24-hour urine protein quantifies proteinuria but does not establish the structural diagnosis and would not explain the flank masses. Renal biopsy is invasive, risks bleeding into cysts, and is not needed when imaging is diagnostic of a hereditary cystic disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Renal ultrasound: bilaterally enlarged kidneys with innumerable cysts of varying sizes; right kidney 16.4 cm, left kidney 15.8 cm
Liver: several small hepatic cysts
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, no protein, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites; 8-10 RBC/hpf, no casts
Serum creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73 m²)
BUN: 16 mg/dL
Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L
Urine culture: no growth

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The imaging confirms multiple bilateral renal cysts with hepatic cysts. By Pei (modified Ravine) ultrasound criteria, what number of cysts supports the diagnosis of ADPKD in a 40-year-old with a positive family history?
options: A single cyst in either kidney
At least 2 cysts in each kidney
A total of at least 3 cysts (unilateral or bilateral)
More than 10 cysts in each kidney regardless of age
stages.explanation: The unified Pei criteria are age-stratified: ages 15–39 need ≥3 cysts total (unilateral or bilateral); ages 40–59 need ≥2 cysts in EACH kidney; ≥60 need ≥4 in each kidney. This 40-year-old falls in the 40–59 band, so ≥2 cysts in each kidney is diagnostic — and she far exceeds it with innumerable bilateral cysts plus hepatic cysts, confirming ADPKD. A single cyst is a common incidental finding; the ≥3-total threshold applies to younger (15–39) at-risk patients, not a 40-year-old.

stages.label: SCREENING
stages.question: Given her father's fatal 'brain bleed,' which screening study is most appropriate to offer regarding intracranial aneurysm?
options: Routine screening is not indicated because aneurysm risk is no higher than the general population
MR angiography of the brain, given a family history of intracranial aneurysm or hemorrhage
Lumbar puncture to detect xanthochromia
Carotid duplex ultrasonography
stages.explanation: Patients with ADPKD have an increased prevalence of intracranial aneurysms, and screening with MR angiography (no contrast needed) is recommended for those with a family history of intracranial aneurysm or subarachnoid hemorrhage, as in this patient whose father died of a brain bleed. Stating routine screening is not indicated is wrong here because the positive family history specifically raises her pre-test probability. Lumbar puncture detects subarachnoid blood after rupture, not unruptured aneurysms. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis, not intracranial aneurysms.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which antihypertensive class is first-line for blood pressure control in ADPKD and also slows progression of kidney disease?
options: ACE inhibitor (e.g., lisinopril)
Hydrochlorothiazide
Amlodipine
Doxazosin
stages.explanation: ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) are first-line in ADPKD because RAAS activation drives the hypertension, and these agents reduce proteinuria and slow progression while controlling blood pressure; guideline targets favor rigorous control (e.g., ≤110/75 in younger patients who tolerate it). Thiazides treat hypertension but lack the renoprotective, RAAS-directed benefit and may worsen volume contraction. Amlodipine controls pressure but does not preferentially reduce intraglomerular pressure or proteinuria. Doxazosin is an alpha-blocker reserved for refractory cases and is not renoprotective. Tolvaptan can additionally be considered to slow cyst growth in rapidly progressive disease.

cases.diagnosis: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD)
pearls: ADPKD presents with hypertension, hematuria, flank pain, and bilateral enlarged kidneys; renal ultrasound is the first-line diagnostic test using age-adjusted Pei criteria.
Extrarenal manifestations include hepatic cysts, intracranial (berry) aneurysms, mitral valve prolapse, and colonic diverticula.
Screen for intracranial aneurysm with MR angiography in patients with a personal or family history of aneurysm/subarachnoid hemorrhage.
ACE inhibitors/ARBs are first-line antihypertensives; tolvaptan can slow cyst growth in rapidly progressing disease.

cases.title: Blood Pressure That Won't Come Down and a Creatinine Bump
cases.chiefComplaint: My blood pressure stays high even on three medicines, and my last labs showed my kidney number went up.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents with poorly controlled hypertension despite three agents at maximal doses. His regimen is lisinopril 40 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, and chlorthalidone 25 mg daily, yet home readings remain around 160/95 mmHg. He reports good adherence using a pill organizer. Two weeks ago his primary physician increased the lisinopril, and a recheck showed his serum creatinine had risen from 1.2 to 1.9 mg/dL. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or edema. Past medical history includes a 45-pack-year smoking history, peripheral artery disease with prior claudication, and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin. He has no allergies. He drinks two beers nightly. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. He denies excessive licorice intake, decongestant use, or snoring suggestive of sleep apnea. He reports no flushing, palpitations, or episodic sweating. He is frustrated that his pressure has worsened over the past year despite lifestyle changes including reduced sodium intake.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight and in no acute distress. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rate and rhythm with an S4 gallop and no murmur. A systolic-diastolic bruit is audible in the epigastrium that lateralizes to the left upper quadrant. Peripheral pulses are diminished in both lower extremities with a faint femoral bruit on the right. There is no peripheral edema, no jugular venous distension, and lungs are clear to auscultation. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without palpable masses or pulsatile expansion. Fundoscopic exam reveals arteriolar narrowing and arteriovenous nicking consistent with chronic hypertension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 63-year-old smoker with diffuse atherosclerosis has resistant hypertension, an abdominal bruit, and a creatinine that rose from 1.2 to 1.9 mg/dL after the ACE inhibitor was uptitrated. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Primary hyperaldosteronism
Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis
Pheochromocytoma
Fibromuscular dysplasia
stages.explanation: The picture of resistant hypertension, an abdominal bruit, diffuse atherosclerosis in an older smoker, and an acute creatinine rise after ACE inhibitor uptitration is classic for atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis. In significant stenosis, glomerular filtration depends on angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction; blocking it drops GFR and raises creatinine. Primary hyperaldosteronism causes resistant hypertension but typically with hypokalemia and no bruit or ACE-induced creatinine spike. Pheochromocytoma causes episodic spells of palpitations, sweating, and headache, absent here. Fibromuscular dysplasia causes renovascular hypertension but in younger women, not an older atherosclerotic man.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.2 mg/dL)
eGFR: 38 mL/min/1.73 m²
Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
Sodium: 140 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood, bland sediment
Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio: low (renin elevated)
Duplex ultrasound: elevated peak systolic velocity in the left main renal artery (>200 cm/s) with a renal-aortic ratio >3.5; small left kidney (8.9 cm) vs right kidney (11.2 cm)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The creatinine rose sharply after the ACE inhibitor dose was increased. What is the most appropriate immediate medication adjustment?
options: Continue the current lisinopril dose and recheck in 3 months
Reduce or discontinue the ACE inhibitor and substitute an alternative agent
Add a second ACE inhibitor for additive RAAS blockade
Start an ARB in addition to the ACE inhibitor
stages.explanation: A creatinine increase greater than about 30% after RAAS blockade strongly suggests hemodynamically significant renal artery stenosis (especially bilateral or in a solitary functioning kidney), and the offending agent should be reduced or stopped to protect GFR, substituting an agent such as a calcium channel blocker. Continuing the lisinopril risks further functional decline and possible acute kidney injury. Adding a second ACE inhibitor is never appropriate and compounds the harm. Combining an ACE inhibitor with an ARB doubles RAAS blockade, increasing the risk of hyperkalemia and acute kidney injury, and is contraindicated in this setting.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: To confirm the diagnosis noninvasively after the abnormal duplex study, which imaging modality is most appropriate given his eGFR of 38?
options: CT angiography with iodinated contrast
MR angiography with gadolinium
Catheter-based digital subtraction angiography as the first confirmatory test
Duplex ultrasonography findings are sufficient; proceed without further confirmatory cross-sectional imaging if intervention is not planned
stages.explanation: Duplex ultrasonography already demonstrates a hemodynamically significant lesion (elevated peak systolic velocity, elevated renal-aortic ratio, and a small ipsilateral kidney); when revascularization is not being pursued, these findings can suffice without additional contrast-based imaging. CT angiography uses iodinated contrast that risks contrast nephropathy at eGFR 38. Gadolinium MR angiography historically raised nephrogenic systemic fibrosis concerns at low eGFR (newer agents are lower-risk but still given cautiously). Catheter angiography is the gold standard but is invasive and generally reserved for when intervention is planned, not as the first confirmatory step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis like this, what is the guideline-supported first-line management strategy in most patients?
options: Optimal medical therapy with risk factor modification
Percutaneous renal artery angioplasty with stenting for all patients
Surgical aortorenal bypass
Nephrectomy of the affected kidney
stages.explanation: Major trials (CORAL, ASTRAL) showed that for most patients with atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis, optimal medical therapy—blood pressure control, statins, antiplatelet therapy, and smoking cessation—yields outcomes equivalent to stenting, so medical management is first-line. Routine stenting for all patients is not supported and adds procedural risk without benefit; it is reserved for refractory hypertension, flash pulmonary edema, or progressive renal dysfunction despite therapy. Surgical bypass is reserved for complex anatomy or failed endovascular options. Nephrectomy is not indicated and would sacrifice functional renal tissue.

cases.diagnosis: Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis
pearls: Suspect renovascular hypertension when resistant HTN, an abdominal bruit, and a >30% creatinine rise after ACE inhibitor/ARB coincide, particularly in older atherosclerotic patients.
Atherosclerosis is the most common cause in older patients and affects the proximal/ostial segment; fibromuscular dysplasia (string-of-beads, mid-distal artery) is the classic cause in young women.
Duplex ultrasound (peak systolic velocity, renal-aortic ratio) is a good initial test; reserve contrast angiography for when intervention is planned.
Optimal medical therapy equals stenting for most patients (CORAL, ASTRAL); reserve revascularization for refractory HTN, flash pulmonary edema, or declining renal function.

cases.title: Years of Pelvic Pain That Eases After Urinating
cases.chiefComplaint: I have constant pressure and pain in my bladder, and I'm urinating all day and night, but every urine test comes back normal.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman reports two years of suprapubic pressure and pelvic pain that worsens as her bladder fills and is transiently relieved by voiding. She urinates more than 15 times daily and wakes 3-4 times nightly. Symptoms flare with coffee, citrus, alcohol, and around menses, and intercourse is painful. She has seen multiple clinicians; repeated urine cultures have been negative and several empiric antibiotic courses gave no lasting relief. Past medical history includes irritable bowel syndrome and fibromyalgia. Medications include only ibuprofen as needed; she has no drug allergies. She is a non-smoker, drinks occasional wine, and works as a teacher. Gynecologic history is notable for normal Pap smears and no sexually transmitted infections. She denies fever, hematuria, weight loss, flank pain, or prior pelvic surgery. She reports anxiety and low mood related to her chronic symptoms and disrupted sleep. She has tried cranberry supplements and increased water intake without benefit and is seeking a definitive explanation.
cases.exam: The patient appears fatigued but is in no acute distress. Abdominal exam reveals suprapubic tenderness without rebound, guarding, or palpable masses; there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. On pelvic examination, there is marked tenderness of the anterior vaginal wall overlying the bladder base and tenderness of the pelvic floor levator muscles to palpation. There is no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass, and no abnormal vaginal discharge. External genitalia are normal. There are no vesicles, ulcers, or rashes. Rectal tone is normal and there is no rectal mass.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A woman has chronic bladder pain worsening with filling and relieved by voiding, urinary frequency, dyspareunia, and repeatedly negative cultures. What is the essential first step before labeling this a pain syndrome?
options: Begin pentosan polysulfate empirically
Exclude other causes with urinalysis, urine culture, and a careful history/exam (a diagnosis of exclusion)
Perform immediate cystectomy
Start long-term suppressive antibiotics
stages.explanation: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion: the essential first step is to rule out mimics—urinary tract infection, malignancy, stones, endometriosis, and other causes—using urinalysis, culture, and a thorough history and exam. Starting pentosan polysulfate before excluding treatable causes is premature and the drug has limited efficacy and retinal toxicity concerns. Cystectomy is a last-resort surgical option, never an initial step. Long-term suppressive antibiotics are inappropriate when cultures are repeatedly negative and there is no infection to treat; they expose her to resistance and side effects without benefit.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: clear, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, 0-2 WBC/hpf, no RBC
Urine culture: no growth
Urine cytology: negative for malignant cells
Post-void residual (bladder scan): 25 mL
Pregnancy test (urine hCG): negative
Voiding diary: 16 daytime voids, average voided volume 90 mL, 3 nocturnal voids

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cystoscopy with hydrodistension is performed and reveals reddened mucosal patches with central scars and radiating vessels that crack and bleed on bladder distension. What are these findings called, and what is their significance?
options: Trabeculations indicating bladder outlet obstruction
Hunner lesions, identifying the classic (ulcerative) subtype of IC/BPS
Squamous metaplasia indicating chronic irritation only
Carcinoma in situ requiring intravesical BCG
stages.explanation: The described reddened patches with central scarring, radiating vessels, and bleeding/cracking on distension are Hunner lesions, which identify the classic (ulcerative) subtype of IC/BPS and direct subtype-specific therapy such as lesion fulguration or triamcinolone injection. Trabeculations reflect detrusor hypertrophy from outlet obstruction, a different entity. Squamous metaplasia (e.g., trigonal) is a common incidental finding and is not the same as Hunner lesions. Carcinoma in situ can appear as a velvety red patch and must be excluded with biopsy, but the classic Hunner lesion morphology plus negative cytology points to IC/BPS rather than malignancy; biopsy confirms.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming IC/BPS, which first-line conservative measures should be implemented before escalating to medications or procedures?
options: Patient education, dietary trigger avoidance, stress management, and pelvic floor physical therapy
Immediate intravesical BCG instillation
Urinary diversion surgery
Chronic opioid therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of IC/BPS is conservative: patient education about the chronic course, behavioral modification including avoidance of dietary triggers (caffeine, citrus, alcohol, spicy/acidic foods), stress reduction, and pelvic floor physical therapy to address the levator tenderness found on exam. Intravesical BCG is not an established IC/BPS therapy and is used for bladder cancer. Urinary diversion is a last-resort surgery reserved for refractory, debilitating disease after all other measures fail. Chronic opioids are discouraged given the chronic non-malignant nature of the pain, risk of dependence, and lack of durable benefit.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her symptoms persist despite behavioral measures and amitriptyline. She has confirmed Hunner lesions. What is the most appropriate next, lesion-directed intervention?
options: Endoscopic fulguration or triamcinolone injection of the Hunner lesions
Systemic chemotherapy
Bilateral nephrostomy tubes
Indwelling Foley catheter placement
stages.explanation: For the Hunner-lesion subtype refractory to conservative and oral therapy, lesion-directed endoscopic treatment—fulguration (cautery/laser ablation) or intralesional triamcinolone injection—is the guideline-recommended next step and often produces marked symptom relief. Systemic chemotherapy has no role in this benign inflammatory condition. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes address upper-tract obstruction, which she does not have, and would not treat bladder pain. An indwelling Foley catheter would worsen bladder pain and infection risk and does not treat the underlying lesions; it is not a therapy for IC/BPS.

cases.diagnosis: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (Hunner-lesion subtype)
pearls: IC/BPS is a diagnosis of exclusion: chronic bladder pain related to filling and relieved by voiding, with frequency/urgency and negative cultures/cytology.
Hunner lesions on cystoscopy define the classic (ulcerative) subtype and warrant lesion-directed therapy (fulguration or triamcinolone injection).
Treatment is stepwise: education and behavioral/dietary changes plus pelvic floor PT first, then oral agents (amitriptyline, pentosan polysulfate), then intravesical and procedural options.
IC/BPS commonly coexists with other chronic pain conditions—IBS, fibromyalgia, and chronic pelvic pain.

cases.title: A Weakening Urine Stream That Sprays
cases.chiefComplaint: My urine stream has gotten weaker and weaker over the past year and now it sprays everywhere.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man reports a progressively weak, splayed urinary stream over the past year, with spraying, straining to void, post-void dribbling, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. He has had two urinary tract infections in the past six months. Notably, he underwent prolonged urethral catheterization during an ICU admission for trauma three years ago, and in his twenties he was treated for a urethral infection with discharge. He denies hematuria, fever, or flank pain currently. Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. He has no allergies. He is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) and drinks alcohol occasionally. He is sexually active with one partner. He denies new-onset nocturia out of proportion to his obstructive symptoms and has no neurologic complaints, leg weakness, or saddle anesthesia. He has tried tamsulosin prescribed by his primary physician for presumed prostate enlargement with no improvement, which prompted his referral for further evaluation.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing. The abdomen is soft; the suprapubic region is non-tender with no palpable distended bladder. Genitourinary exam shows a normal circumcised phallus with no meatal stenosis, no penile plaque, and no urethral discharge; gentle palpation along the ventral penile urethra reveals a focus of induration in the bulbar region without overlying erythema. The testes are descended bilaterally and non-tender with no masses. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, mildly enlarged, non-tender prostate without nodules. Neurologic exam shows normal lower-extremity strength, sensation, and intact perineal sensation with normal anal sphincter tone.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A man has a progressively weak, spraying stream, recurrent UTIs, a history of prolonged catheterization and prior urethritis, palpable urethral induration, and no benefit from tamsulosin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
Urethral stricture
Neurogenic bladder
Prostate cancer
stages.explanation: Spraying and a weak stream with a history of urethral instrumentation and prior urethritis (both classic causes of scarring), palpable bulbar induration, recurrent UTIs, and lack of response to an alpha-blocker point to urethral stricture. BPH causes obstructive symptoms but typically responds to alpha-blockers and does not cause a sprayed stream or palpable urethral induration. Neurogenic bladder is excluded by the normal neurologic exam, intact perineal sensation, and normal sphincter tone. Prostate cancer presents with a hard nodule on rectal exam and rarely causes obstruction at this stage; his prostate is smooth without nodules.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best confirms the location and length of a urethral stricture before planning treatment?
options: Retrograde urethrogram
Transrectal ultrasound of the prostate
Serum prostate-specific antigen
Renal ultrasound
stages.explanation: Retrograde urethrography is the standard imaging study to define the location, length, and severity of a urethral stricture, providing the anatomic detail needed for surgical planning (often combined with cystoscopy). Transrectal ultrasound evaluates the prostate, not the urethral lumen, and would miss the stricture. PSA screens for prostate cancer and gives no information about the urethra. Renal ultrasound assesses the upper tracts for hydronephrosis as a consequence of obstruction but does not localize or characterize the urethral lesion itself.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Uroflowmetry: maximum flow rate (Qmax) 7 mL/s with a flattened, plateau-shaped curve
Post-void residual: 110 mL
Retrograde urethrogram: 1.5 cm narrowing of the bulbar urethra with proximal dilation
Urinalysis: 1+ leukocyte esterase, 10-15 WBC/hpf
Urine culture: pending
Serum creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The retrograde urethrogram confirms a short 1.5 cm bulbar urethral stricture. For a short bulbar stricture, which definitive treatment offers the highest long-term success and lowest recurrence?
options: Repeated urethral dilation indefinitely
Direct vision internal urethrotomy as the definitive cure
Urethroplasty (surgical reconstruction)
Lifelong indwelling suprapubic catheter
stages.explanation: Urethroplasty—surgical excision and reanastomosis or substitution (e.g., buccal mucosal graft)—has the highest long-term success and lowest recurrence rate for bulbar strictures and is the definitive treatment, especially after prior failed minimally invasive attempts. Repeated dilation provides only temporary relief with high recurrence and progressive scarring. Direct vision internal urethrotomy can be tried for short strictures but has high recurrence after the first attempt and is not a durable cure. A lifelong suprapubic catheter is a palliative measure for nonoperative candidates, not a definitive correction of a treatable short stricture.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient's obstruction had gone untreated and he presented unable to void with a tensely distended, painful bladder, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Forcefully pass a large-caliber urethral catheter through the stricture
Suprapubic catheter placement to relieve retention
Emergency urethroplasty
Administer a diuretic to increase urine output
stages.explanation: With acute urinary retention from a stricture that prevents urethral catheter passage, suprapubic catheter placement (cystostomy) safely decompresses the bladder above the obstruction. Forcefully passing a large catheter through a tight stricture risks creating a false passage, urethral injury, and worsening scarring. Emergency urethroplasty is inappropriate in the acute setting; reconstruction is planned electively once inflammation settles and imaging is complete. A diuretic would increase urine production into an already obstructed, distended bladder, worsening pain and pressure without relieving the obstruction.

cases.diagnosis: Urethral stricture (bulbar)
pearls: Suspect urethral stricture with a weak, spraying stream plus a history of instrumentation, trauma, or prior urethritis; failure to respond to alpha-blockers is a clue against BPH.
Retrograde urethrography defines stricture location and length and guides treatment planning.
Urethroplasty offers the most durable cure; dilation and internal urethrotomy have high recurrence rates.
For acute retention when a urethral catheter cannot be passed, place a suprapubic catheter rather than forcing the stricture.

cases.title: Penile Discharge and Burning a Few Days After a New Partner
cases.chiefComplaint: I have thick yellow discharge from my penis and it burns when I pee.
cases.history: A 24-year-old man presents with three days of thick, copious yellow-green penile discharge and burning with urination. Symptoms began about four days after unprotected vaginal intercourse with a new female partner. He denies fever, joint pain, rash, eye symptoms, scrotal pain, or hematuria. He has had no prior sexually transmitted infections and does not use condoms consistently. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and reports no drug allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, denies injection drug use, and is a non-smoker. He has had two female partners in the last six months and no male partners. He has not been vaccinated against HPV and has never been tested for HIV. He is anxious about the diagnosis and asks whether his partner needs treatment. He reports the discharge has been staining his underwear and is profuse rather than scant. He has taken no antibiotics recently.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and afebrile. Genitourinary exam reveals a circumcised penis with abundant purulent yellow-green discharge expressed from the urethral meatus; the meatus is mildly erythematous and edematous. There is no penile ulcer, vesicle, or wart. The testes and epididymides are non-tender and without swelling or masses; there is no scrotal erythema. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the skin exam is unremarkable with no rash on the palms, soles, or trunk. Joints are non-tender with full range of motion, and there is no conjunctival injection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young man has profuse purulent urethral discharge and dysuria days after unprotected intercourse. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, with urethral Gram stain
Blood cultures
Voiding cystourethrogram
Serum PSA
stages.explanation: NAAT of a first-catch urine or urethral swab for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis is the most sensitive and specific test for urethritis and detects both common pathogens simultaneously; a urethral Gram stain provides rapid point-of-care evidence. Blood cultures are reserved for suspected disseminated gonococcal infection (fever, arthritis, rash), which he lacks. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates reflux and anatomy, irrelevant to acute urethritis. Serum PSA screens for prostate pathology and has no role in a 24-year-old with classic sexually transmitted urethritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urethral Gram stain: numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes with gram-negative intracellular diplococci
First-catch urine NAAT: Neisseria gonorrhoeae detected; Chlamydia trachomatis pending
Urinalysis: 3+ leukocyte esterase, many WBC/hpf
HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab: pending
RPR (syphilis): pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Gram stain shows gram-negative intracellular diplococci within neutrophils. What organism does this confirm?
options: Chlamydia trachomatis
Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Trichomonas vaginalis
Escherichia coli
stages.explanation: Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within neutrophils on a urethral Gram stain are diagnostic of Neisseria gonorrhoeae in a symptomatic man, with high sensitivity and specificity in this setting. Chlamydia trachomatis is an obligate intracellular organism that does not stain on Gram stain and produces a negative Gram stain with mucoid discharge. Trichomonas vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan seen as motile organisms on wet mount, not diplococci. Escherichia coli is a gram-negative rod, not a diplococcus, and is a cause of urinary tract infection rather than sexually transmitted urethritis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for uncomplicated gonococcal urethritis per current CDC guidance?
options: Oral azithromycin 1 g alone
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg once
A single intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone (500 mg for patients under 150 kg)
Oral doxycycline alone for 7 days
stages.explanation: Current CDC guidance recommends a single 500 mg intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone for uncomplicated gonococcal infection (1 g if ≥150 kg); if chlamydial coinfection has not been excluded, add doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days. Azithromycin alone is no longer recommended monotherapy due to rising resistance and was removed as the co-agent in favor of doxycycline. Ciprofloxacin and other fluoroquinolones are not used empirically because of widespread gonococcal resistance. Doxycycline alone treats chlamydia but is inadequate for gonorrhea and would leave the gonococcal infection untreated.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond treating the patient, which public health step is essential?
options: No partner notification is needed once the patient is treated
Treat recent sexual partners (expedited partner therapy where permitted) and counsel on STI testing including HIV and syphilis
Mandatory hospitalization for observation
Repeat the same antibiotic daily for two weeks
stages.explanation: Partner management is essential: recent sexual partners should be evaluated and treated, using expedited partner therapy where legally permitted, and the patient should be tested for other STIs including HIV and syphilis and counseled on condom use and abstaining from sex until treatment is complete. Skipping partner notification leads to reinfection and continued transmission. Hospitalization is unnecessary for uncomplicated urethritis. Repeating the antibiotic daily for two weeks is incorrect dosing—gonococcal urethritis is treated with a single ceftriaxone dose plus doxycycline for chlamydia coverage if indicated.

cases.diagnosis: Gonococcal urethritis
pearls: Profuse purulent urethral discharge with gram-negative intracellular diplococci on Gram stain is diagnostic of gonococcal urethritis.
First-line treatment is a single IM dose of ceftriaxone 500 mg; add doxycycline if chlamydial coinfection is not excluded.
Always test for coinfections (chlamydia, HIV, syphilis) and arrange partner treatment to prevent reinfection.
Disseminated gonococcal infection presents with the triad of migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, and pustular skin lesions.

cases.title: Watery Discharge and Mild Burning After a New Relationship
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a clear, sticky discharge from my penis and a little burning when I urinate.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents with one week of intermittent clear-to-white mucoid penile discharge, most noticeable in the morning, and mild dysuria. Symptoms began roughly two weeks after starting a new sexual relationship; he uses condoms inconsistently. The discharge is scant rather than profuse, and he denies fever, scrotal pain, joint symptoms, rash, or eye redness. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He is a college student, drinks alcohol socially, and denies tobacco or injection drug use. He has had three female partners in the past year and no male partners. He has never been tested for sexually transmitted infections and has not received the HPV vaccine. He is worried because a friend recently was diagnosed with chlamydia. He has taken no antibiotics recently and reports no prior episodes of urethral discharge. He has no penile lesions or sores and no testicular swelling.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and afebrile. Genitourinary exam reveals an uncircumcised penis with scant mucoid (clear to whitish) discharge expressed from the meatus on stripping the urethra; the meatus is minimally erythematous. There are no penile ulcers, vesicles, or warts, and the glans appears normal after foreskin retraction. The testes and epididymides are non-tender and normal in size with no swelling. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. The skin shows no rash on the palms, soles, or trunk, and there is no conjunctival injection or joint swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young man has scant mucoid urethral discharge and mild dysuria. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?
options: Treat empirically for a urinary tract infection with nitrofurantoin
Obtain a urethral Gram stain and NAAT for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis
Order a voiding cystourethrogram
Reassure and discharge without testing
stages.explanation: Scant mucoid discharge with dysuria in a sexually active young man is urethritis until proven otherwise; a urethral Gram stain (to look for neutrophils and diplococci) plus NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia is the appropriate workup and identifies the pathogen. Empiric nitrofurantoin treats cystitis, not sexually transmitted urethritis, and would miss the diagnosis. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates anatomy/reflux and is irrelevant here. Reassurance without testing risks untreated infection, complications such as epididymitis, and ongoing transmission to partners.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urethral Gram stain: 8 polymorphonuclear leukocytes per high-power field; NO gram-negative intracellular diplococci seen
First-catch urine NAAT: Neisseria gonorrhoeae NOT detected; Chlamydia trachomatis detected
Urinalysis: 2+ leukocyte esterase, moderate WBC/hpf
HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab: negative
RPR (syphilis): non-reactive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Gram stain shows neutrophils but no diplococci, and the picture is consistent with nongonococcal urethritis. Which organism is the most common identified cause of nongonococcal urethritis?
options: Chlamydia trachomatis
Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Treponema pallidum
Klebsiella pneumoniae
stages.explanation: Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common identified cause of nongonococcal urethritis; it is an obligate intracellular organism that does not appear on Gram stain, explaining the neutrophils without visible diplococci. Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes gonococcal urethritis, which is by definition excluded when diplococci are absent and NAAT is negative. Treponema pallidum causes syphilis (painless chancre, not urethritis). Klebsiella pneumoniae is a urinary tract pathogen, not a typical cause of sexually transmitted urethritis. Mycoplasma genitalium is another important NGU cause when chlamydia is negative.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for chlamydial (nongonococcal) urethritis per current CDC guidance?
options: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg once
Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
Metronidazole 2 g orally once
Penicillin G benzathine 2.4 million units IM once
stages.explanation: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is the preferred first-line regimen for chlamydial urethritis under current CDC guidance, having shown superior cure rates to single-dose azithromycin (azithromycin 1 g once remains an alternative, e.g., in pregnancy or adherence concerns). Intramuscular ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea, which was excluded here. Metronidazole treats trichomoniasis or bacterial vaginosis, not chlamydia. Penicillin G benzathine is the treatment for syphilis and has no activity against C. trachomatis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What additional counseling and management is most appropriate for this patient?
options: No follow-up or partner treatment is necessary
Treat sexual partners, advise abstaining from sex for 7 days after treatment and until partners are treated, and recommend retesting in about 3 months
Admit for IV antibiotics
Begin lifelong suppressive doxycycline
stages.explanation: Partners should be evaluated and treated (expedited partner therapy where permitted); the patient should abstain from sex for 7 days after single-dose therapy or until a 7-day regimen is completed and partners are treated, and chlamydia retesting (test of reinfection) is recommended at about 3 months given high reinfection rates. Skipping partner treatment and follow-up causes reinfection and ongoing transmission. Admission for IV antibiotics is unnecessary for uncomplicated urethritis. Lifelong suppressive doxycycline is inappropriate—chlamydial urethritis is cured with a short course.

cases.diagnosis: Nongonococcal (chlamydial) urethritis
pearls: Scant mucoid discharge with neutrophils but no diplococci on Gram stain suggests nongonococcal urethritis; Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common identified cause.
C. trachomatis is an obligate intracellular organism that does not appear on Gram stain—diagnose with NAAT.
First-line treatment is doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days; azithromycin 1 g is an alternative.
Always evaluate/treat partners, screen for coinfections, and retest at ~3 months for reinfection; consider Mycoplasma genitalium when chlamydia is negative.

cases.title: A Sore, Red, Itchy Glans in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: The head of my penis has been red, sore, and itchy for the past two weeks.
cases.history: A 58-year-old uncircumcised man presents with two weeks of redness, soreness, and itching of the glans penis, with a whitish material accumulating under the foreskin and mild discomfort with retraction. He reports increased thirst and urination over recent months. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, which he admits has been poorly controlled; he takes metformin 1000 mg twice daily but has not checked his glucose regularly or seen his physician in over a year. He has no drug allergies. He is married and monogamous, denies new sexual partners, and his wife has no symptoms. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not smoke. He denies urethral discharge, dysuria, penile ulcers, fever, or systemic symptoms. He has tried over-the-counter antifungal cream sporadically with partial relief. He reports the foreskin has become slightly harder to retract over the same period. He has no prior history of similar episodes and no recent antibiotic use.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight and in no distress. Genitourinary exam reveals an uncircumcised penis; on retracting the foreskin, the glans and inner prepuce are erythematous and edematous with satellite papules and pustules at the periphery and a curd-like white exudate in the coronal sulcus. There are no ulcers, vesicles, or indurated plaques, and no urethral discharge is expressed. The foreskin retracts with mild difficulty but is not trapped. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no scrotal swelling. The remainder of the skin exam shows no widespread rash and no oral thrush.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An uncircumcised, poorly controlled diabetic man has an erythematous glans with curd-like exudate and satellite pustules. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Candidal balanitis
Primary syphilis (chancre)
Penile squamous cell carcinoma
Genital herpes
stages.explanation: Candidal balanitis is most likely: an uncircumcised man with poorly controlled diabetes is predisposed to Candida overgrowth, and the curd-like white exudate with satellite papules/pustules and an erythematous, itchy glans is the classic presentation. A syphilitic chancre is a solitary painless ulcer, not a diffuse erythematous glans with exudate. Penile squamous cell carcinoma presents as a non-healing ulcerated or exophytic lesion/plaque, typically over a longer course. Genital herpes produces painful grouped vesicles and ulcers, not satellite pustules with curd-like exudate. The diabetic context strongly favors Candida.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Random plasma glucose: 268 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c: 10.2%
KOH preparation of subpreputial exudate: budding yeasts with pseudohyphae
Urinalysis: 2+ glucose, negative leukocyte esterase, negative nitrites
HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which bedside test best confirms the suspected organism in this presentation?
options: Dark-field microscopy
Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation showing budding yeast with pseudohyphae
Tzanck smear for multinucleated giant cells
Gram stain for gram-negative intracellular diplococci
stages.explanation: A KOH preparation of the subpreputial exudate dissolves keratin and reveals budding yeast with pseudohyphae, confirming Candida and explaining the curd-like discharge and satellite lesions. Dark-field microscopy detects motile Treponema pallidum spirochetes in syphilitic chancres, not relevant here. A Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells in herpes simplex/varicella infections, which do not fit this picture. Gram stain for gram-negative intracellular diplococci diagnoses gonococcal urethritis, an entirely different condition without the glans findings or curd-like exudate seen in candidal balanitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for candidal balanitis along with the key underlying issue to address?
options: Topical antifungal (e.g., clotrimazole) plus improved glycemic control and hygiene
Oral acyclovir
Intramuscular ceftriaxone
Immediate circumcision as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: Treatment is a topical antifungal such as clotrimazole or miconazole (oral fluconazole for severe or refractory cases), combined with addressing the predisposing factor—improving glycemic control—and good local hygiene with gentle foreskin retraction and drying. Oral acyclovir treats herpes, not candidiasis. Intramuscular ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea and has no antifungal activity. Circumcision is not first-line therapy; it is considered for recurrent balanitis or when phimosis develops and conservative measures fail, but acute candidal balanitis is first managed medically with antifungals and glycemic optimization.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If recurrent candidal balanitis leads to scarring of the foreskin so it can no longer be retracted over the glans, what is this complication called?
options: Paraphimosis
Phimosis
Priapism
Hypospadias
stages.explanation: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin over the glans, a recognized complication of recurrent balanitis from inflammatory scarring; persistent phimosis is an indication for circumcision. Paraphimosis is the opposite emergency—the retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans and cannot be reduced, causing constriction and edema. Priapism is a prolonged, often painful erection unrelated to foreskin scarring. Hypospadias is a congenital anomaly with a ventrally displaced urethral meatus, present from birth and unrelated to acquired inflammatory scarring of the foreskin.

cases.diagnosis: Candidal balanitis
pearls: Candidal balanitis classically affects uncircumcised men with poorly controlled diabetes—curd-like exudate with satellite papules/pustules and an itchy, erythematous glans.
Confirm with a KOH prep showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae; check glucose/HbA1c, as new balanitis can unmask diabetes.
Treat with topical antifungals and glycemic control; reserve oral fluconazole for severe/refractory disease.
Recurrent balanitis can scar the foreskin causing phimosis—an indication for circumcision; do not confuse with paraphimosis (a urologic emergency).

cases.title: A New Bend in the Erect Penis With Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Over the past few months my penis has developed a painful upward bend when erect, and it's making sex difficult.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man reports a gradual change over four to five months: his erect penis now bends sharply upward, he feels pain with erection, and he has noticed a firm lump on the top of the shaft. The curvature has made vaginal penetration difficult and intercourse painful for both him and his partner. He denies trauma but recalls occasional 'buckling' during intercourse months ago. He has erections adequate for activity but is increasingly distressed. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension; he takes metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has Dupuytren contractures of both hands. He has no drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally, does not smoke, and is in a stable marriage. Family history is notable for a father with hand contractures. He denies urethral discharge, dysuria, hematuria, or penile numbness. He is anxious that this may be cancer and asks whether surgery is the only option. He reports the curvature seems to have stabilized over the last month after initially worsening.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing. Examination of the flaccid penis reveals a firm, well-demarcated nontender plaque palpable along the dorsal midline of the penile shaft, approximately 1.5 cm, within the tunica albuginea. There is no urethral discharge and the meatus is normal. The glans is normal with no ulcer or mass. The testes are descended, normal in size, and non-tender without masses. Both palms show fixed flexion cords consistent with Dupuytren contractures. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Distal sensation is intact and peripheral pulses are normal. No penile ulceration or skin lesion is seen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A middle-aged man has progressive painful dorsal penile curvature, a palpable dorsal tunica plaque, and Dupuytren contractures. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Penile fracture
Peyronie disease
Penile squamous cell carcinoma
Priapism
stages.explanation: Peyronie disease is most likely: a fibrous plaque of the tunica albuginea causes penile curvature, pain with erection, and painful intercourse, and it is associated with other fibrosing disorders such as Dupuytren contractures, as seen here. Penile fracture is an acute traumatic event with a 'popping' sound, immediate detumescence, swelling, and an eggplant deformity—not a gradual plaque. Penile squamous cell carcinoma presents as a glans/prepuce ulcer or mass, not a deep tunical plaque with curvature. Priapism is a prolonged erection, unrelated to a fibrotic plaque or curvature.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Peyronie disease is largely a clinical diagnosis. When objective assessment of curvature and plaque is needed before considering intervention, which test is most useful?
options: Retrograde urethrogram
Penile duplex ultrasound (often with an erection induced) to assess plaque and curvature
Serum testosterone alone
Cystoscopy
stages.explanation: Penile duplex ultrasonography, often after inducing an erection with an intracavernosal vasoactive agent, characterizes the plaque (size, calcification) and the degree and direction of curvature, and assesses penile blood flow before planning treatment. A retrograde urethrogram images the urethra and is irrelevant to a tunical plaque. Serum testosterone may be checked because hypogonadism can coexist, but it does not assess the curvature or plaque. Cystoscopy evaluates the bladder and urethral lumen, providing no information about the tunica albuginea plaque or curvature.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Penile duplex ultrasound (after intracavernosal injection): dorsal tunica albuginea plaque 1.4 cm without dense calcification; dorsal curvature measured at 40 degrees
Intact cavernosal arterial inflow; no significant venous leak
Goniometer measurement (auto-photograph): 40 degrees dorsal curvature
Serum total testosterone: 410 ng/dL (normal)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is now several months in with stable curvature, pain has largely resolved, and the deformity impairs intercourse. The disease has stabilized out of the acute phase. What is the most appropriate next management consideration?
options: Surgical correction (e.g., plication or plaque incision/grafting) or intralesional collagenase, once disease is stable
Emergency surgical exploration
High-dose vitamin E as definitive cure
Begin a PDE5 inhibitor to straighten the plaque
stages.explanation: Once Peyronie disease has stabilized (typically ≥12 months from onset, with resolved pain and stable curvature) and the deformity impairs function, definitive options include surgery—plication for adequate length or plaque incision/excision with grafting for severe curvature—or intralesional collagenase clostridium histolyticum for appropriate curvatures. Emergency exploration is for acute trauma like penile fracture, not stable Peyronie disease. High-dose vitamin E is not an effective cure and is not recommended. PDE5 inhibitors improve erectile function but do not straighten the plaque or correct curvature, so they are not the corrective therapy for the deformity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which counseling point about the natural history of Peyronie disease is most accurate to share with this patient?
options: It is a premalignant condition that frequently progresses to penile cancer
It has an active (inflammatory, painful) phase followed by a stable (chronic) phase; pain usually improves but curvature often persists
Curvature always resolves completely without any treatment
It is a sexually transmitted infection requiring partner treatment
stages.explanation: Peyronie disease classically has an active/inflammatory phase with pain and evolving curvature, followed by a stable/chronic phase where pain typically subsides but the curvature usually persists or only modestly improves—accurate, reassuring counseling. It is not premalignant and does not progress to penile cancer. Spontaneous complete resolution is uncommon; most patients are left with residual deformity, so promising full resolution is misleading. It is not an infection and is unrelated to sexual transmission, so partner treatment is not relevant. Reassurance plus realistic expectations guides shared decision-making about intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Peyronie disease
pearls: Peyronie disease is a fibrous tunica albuginea plaque causing painful penile curvature; it is associated with Dupuytren contractures and Ledderhose disease.
It is mostly a clinical diagnosis; penile duplex ultrasound (with induced erection) characterizes plaque and curvature when intervention is considered.
There is an active painful phase then a stable phase—pain usually improves but curvature often persists; defer surgery until stable (~12 months).
Stable, function-limiting deformity is treated with plication, plaque incision/grafting, or intralesional collagenase; it is benign and not premalignant.

cases.title: Trouble Conceiving With No Sperm on the Semen Test
cases.chiefComplaint: My wife and I have been trying to conceive for two years, and my semen analysis showed no sperm at all.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man presents for evaluation after two years of failure to conceive despite regular unprotected intercourse; his partner has had a normal gynecologic workup. Two separate semen analyses showed azoospermia (no sperm). He reports normal erections, ejaculation, and libido, and notes a normal ejaculate volume. He denies prior testicular trauma, mumps orchitis, undescended testes, or chemotherapy/radiation. Past medical history is notable only for chronic sinopulmonary infections since childhood. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke, uses no anabolic steroids or testosterone supplements, and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as an electrician with no significant heat or toxin exposure. Family history is unremarkable for infertility but a cousin has cystic fibrosis. He reports no symptoms of hypogonadism such as decreased shaving frequency or hot flashes. He is otherwise healthy and is anxious to understand whether biological fatherhood is possible.
cases.exam: The patient is a well-developed man with normal male secondary sexual characteristics, normal body hair distribution, and no gynecomastia. Genitourinary exam reveals normally sized, descended testes bilaterally (each approximately 18 mL by orchidometer) with normal consistency. Critically, the vasa deferentia are non-palpable bilaterally on careful scrotal examination. The epididymides feel mildly full. There is no varicocele on standing or Valsalva and no inguinal hernia. The penis is normal with a normally positioned meatus. There is no scrotal mass or tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A man with azoospermia has normal-sized testes but bilaterally non-palpable vasa deferentia and a history of chronic sinopulmonary infection. Which initial labs best begin to separate obstructive from non-obstructive azoospermia?
options: Serum FSH and testosterone
PSA and uric acid
Coagulation studies
Liver function tests
stages.explanation: Serum FSH (with testosterone, often LH) is the key first step in azoospermia: normal FSH with normal-sized testes suggests obstruction (sperm production intact, outflow blocked), whereas elevated FSH with small testes suggests non-obstructive azoospermia (primary testicular/spermatogenic failure). Combined with non-palpable vasa, normal testes point toward an obstructive picture here. PSA and uric acid do not assess spermatogenesis or the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Coagulation studies and liver function tests are unrelated to the evaluation of azoospermia and would not distinguish obstructive from non-obstructive causes.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Repeat semen analysis: azoospermia; ejaculate volume 1.2 mL (low); semen pH 6.6 (acidic); fructose negative
Serum FSH: 4.5 mIU/mL (normal)
Serum LH: 4.0 mIU/mL (normal)
Serum total testosterone: 480 ng/dL (normal)
Scrotal/transrectal ultrasound: absent vasa deferentia; seminal vesicles hypoplastic
Karyotype: 46,XY

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Normal FSH and testosterone, normal-sized testes, low-volume acidic fructose-negative ejaculate, and bilaterally absent vasa deferentia indicate which diagnosis?
options: Non-obstructive azoospermia from Sertoli-cell-only syndrome
Obstructive azoospermia due to congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD)
Kallmann syndrome
Retrograde ejaculation
stages.explanation: The findings indicate obstructive azoospermia from congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD): normal FSH/testosterone and normal testes confirm intact spermatogenesis, while absent vasa and hypoplastic seminal vesicles cause a low-volume, acidic, fructose-negative ejaculate. CBAVD is strongly associated with CFTR mutations (note his chronic sinopulmonary infections and a cousin with cystic fibrosis). Sertoli-cell-only syndrome is non-obstructive with elevated FSH and small testes. Kallmann syndrome causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low FSH/LH/testosterone, anosmia). Retrograde ejaculation gives low volume but with sperm/fructose findings and post-ejaculate urine sperm.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the suspected diagnosis, which genetic test is most important before pursuing fertility treatment?
options: CFTR (cystic fibrosis) mutation testing of the patient and his partner
BRCA1/2 testing
Huntington gene testing
HFE hemochromatosis testing
