stages.explanation: CFTR mutation testing of both the patient and his partner is essential: CBAVD is associated with CFTR mutations, and if both partners carry mutations, offspring are at risk for cystic fibrosis, so genetic counseling guides reproductive decisions before assisted reproduction. BRCA1/2 testing assesses hereditary breast/ovarian cancer risk and is unrelated to CBAVD. Huntington gene testing is for a neurodegenerative disorder unrelated to this presentation. HFE testing evaluates hereditary hemochromatosis and has no bearing on azoospermia or vas deferens development.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Because spermatogenesis is intact but the outflow tract is congenitally absent, what is the most appropriate path to biological fatherhood?
options: Vasovasostomy (vasectomy reversal)
Surgical sperm retrieval (e.g., epididymal/testicular extraction) with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI)
Clomiphene to stimulate sperm production
Donor insemination is the only option
stages.explanation: Because spermatogenesis is intact (normal FSH/testes) and the obstruction is congenital absence of the vas, sperm can be harvested directly via microsurgical epididymal sperm aspiration or testicular sperm extraction and used with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), enabling biological fatherhood. Vasovasostomy reconnects a divided vas after vasectomy and is impossible when the vas is congenitally absent. Clomiphene stimulates gonadotropins/spermatogenesis in selected hypogonadal men but cannot overcome a complete anatomic outflow obstruction. Donor sperm is an alternative but is not the only option given that retrieval plus ICSI can use his own sperm.

cases.diagnosis: Obstructive azoospermia due to congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD)
pearls: In azoospermia, FSH and testicular size separate obstructive (normal FSH, normal testes) from non-obstructive (high FSH, small testes) causes.
Low-volume, acidic, fructose-negative ejaculate with absent vasa suggests CBAVD or ejaculatory duct obstruction.
CBAVD is strongly linked to CFTR mutations—test the patient and partner and offer genetic counseling before ICSI.
Obstructive azoospermia with intact spermatogenesis is treated with surgical sperm retrieval plus ICSI; karyotype and Y-microdeletion testing are key in non-obstructive cases.

cases.title: A Tall Teen With Small Testes and Breast Tissue
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm worried because I have some breast tissue and my testicles seem small compared to friends.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents with concerns about bilateral breast enlargement that began in adolescence and persisted, along with testicles he feels are smaller than expected. He reports sparse facial and body hair and shaves only every few weeks. He is notably tall with long legs relative to his torso. He had some learning difficulties and speech delay as a child and required extra help in school but is now in community college. He denies headaches, visual changes, or anosmia. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke or drink heavily. He is not yet sexually active and has had no children. Family history is non-contributory. He denies testicular pain, prior surgeries, or trauma. He has a girlfriend and is concerned about future fertility. He reports low energy and some difficulty building muscle despite going to the gym. He has never had hormone testing or a genetic evaluation.
cases.exam: The patient is tall (height at the 95th percentile) with a eunuchoid body habitus and an arm span exceeding his height. There is bilateral, symmetric, soft glandular breast tissue consistent with true gynecomastia. Body and facial hair are sparse. Genitourinary exam reveals a normal phallus but bilaterally small, firm testes (each approximately 3-4 mL by orchidometer) without masses; the vasa deferentia are palpable. There is no varicocele and no inguinal hernia. There is no thyromegaly. Neurologic exam is grossly normal. Visual fields are full to confrontation. No abdominal masses are palpable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A tall 19-year-old with eunuchoid proportions, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and a history of learning difficulties presents for evaluation. Which initial hormonal pattern would you most expect, and what does it indicate?
options: Low FSH, low LH, low testosterone (secondary hypogonadism)
High FSH, high LH, low testosterone (primary hypogonadism)
Normal FSH, normal LH, normal testosterone
High testosterone with suppressed gonadotropins
stages.explanation: This presentation suggests primary (hypergonadotropic) hypogonadism: testicular failure produces low testosterone, and loss of negative feedback drives FSH and LH high. Small firm testes, gynecomastia, eunuchoid proportions, and tall stature are classic for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), where seminiferous tubules are hyalinized. Low FSH/LH with low testosterone indicates secondary (central) hypogonadism, as in Kallmann syndrome (often with anosmia), which does not fit. Entirely normal hormones would not explain the phenotype. High testosterone with suppressed gonadotropins suggests exogenous androgen use, inconsistent with his small testes and gynecomastia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum FSH: 28 mIU/mL (elevated)
Serum LH: 22 mIU/mL (elevated)
Serum total testosterone: 210 ng/dL (low)
Estradiol: mildly elevated
Semen analysis: azoospermia
Karyotype: 47,XXY

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The karyotype returns as 47,XXY. What is the diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate confirmatory/standard test?
options: Kallmann syndrome; confirmed by GnRH stimulation testing
Klinefelter syndrome; karyotype (47,XXY) is the confirmatory test
Androgen insensitivity syndrome; confirmed by androgen receptor gene testing
Turner syndrome; confirmed by karyotype (45,X)
stages.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome is the diagnosis, and a karyotype demonstrating 47,XXY (an extra X chromosome) is the standard confirmatory test, as obtained here. Kallmann syndrome causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with anosmia and low gonadotropins—opposite to his elevated FSH/LH. Androgen insensitivity syndrome involves a 46,XY karyotype with androgen receptor dysfunction and a female or undervirilized phenotype, not this picture. Turner syndrome (45,X) occurs in phenotypic females with short stature and is incompatible with a 47,XXY male. The combination of primary hypogonadism plus 47,XXY karyotype is diagnostic of Klinefelter syndrome.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which long-term management is most appropriate for this patient's hypogonadism, with an important fertility caveat?
options: Testosterone replacement therapy to address hypogonadal symptoms—after considering sperm preservation, since exogenous testosterone suppresses any spermatogenesis
High-dose estrogen therapy
No treatment is needed since testosterone is only mildly low
Immediate orchiectomy
stages.explanation: Testosterone replacement therapy treats the hypogonadal manifestations (low energy, reduced virilization, bone health, and helps gynecomastia symptoms), but because exogenous testosterone suppresses gonadotropins and any residual spermatogenesis, fertility options such as sperm retrieval (microTESE) and cryopreservation should be discussed before starting therapy. High-dose estrogen would worsen feminization and gynecomastia. Withholding treatment ignores symptomatic, clearly low testosterone (210 ng/dL) with adverse long-term effects on bone and metabolism. Orchiectomy is not indicated; although Klinefelter slightly raises the risk of extragonadal germ cell tumors and breast cancer, prophylactic orchiectomy is not standard care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Klinefelter syndrome carries an increased lifetime risk of which malignancy that is otherwise rare in men?
options: Prostate cancer
Male breast cancer
Renal cell carcinoma
Testicular seminoma
stages.explanation: Men with Klinefelter syndrome have a markedly increased relative risk of male breast cancer, a malignancy that is otherwise rare in men, attributed to the hormonal milieu (relative estrogen excess) and the extra X chromosome; breast awareness and clinical surveillance are warranted, especially given persistent gynecomastia. Prostate cancer risk is actually not increased (and may be lower) given chronic androgen deficiency. Renal cell carcinoma is not characteristically associated with Klinefelter syndrome. While extragonadal (e.g., mediastinal) germ cell tumors are increased, testicular seminoma is not the classically emphasized association—male breast cancer is the hallmark increased malignancy.

cases.diagnosis: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY)
pearls: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents with tall/eunuchoid stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, azoospermia, and primary hypogonadism (high FSH/LH, low testosterone).
Karyotype showing 47,XXY is the confirmatory test; it is the most common cause of primary hypogonadism and congenital infertility in men.
Discuss fertility/sperm preservation (microTESE) before starting testosterone, which suppresses any residual spermatogenesis.
There is an increased risk of male breast cancer and extragonadal germ cell tumors; provide testosterone replacement and breast surveillance.

cases.title: Tired and Low Drive at 50
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive fatigue, low sex drive, and difficulty maintaining erections over the past year.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with roughly a year of worsening fatigue, decreased libido, and erectile difficulties. He reports low mood, poor concentration, and reduced motivation at work. He has noticed loss of morning erections and decreased shaving frequency. He denies headache, visual changes, galactorrhea, or testicular trauma. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.4%) and obesity (BMI 33). Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. He takes no opioids or glucocorticoids. No allergies. Social history: works as an accountant, drinks 3-4 beers on weekends, never smoked, no anabolic steroid use. He sleeps poorly and snores; his wife reports witnessed apneas. Family history: father with type 2 diabetes; no known pituitary disease. Review of systems is negative for hot flashes, weight loss, fevers, or bone pain. He and his wife are not planning further children but he is concerned about his sexual function and energy.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, centrally obese man in no distress. No temporal or visual field deficits on confrontation. No gynecomastia and no galactorrhea on chest exam. Thyroid normal. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. Abdomen soft with central adiposity. Genital exam: normal phallus, both testes descended and soft, estimated volume approximately 18 mL bilaterally, no masses or varicocele. Reduced body hair distribution. Neurologic exam intact with normal reflexes and sensation. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test to confirm suspected testosterone deficiency?
options: A single random afternoon total testosterone
Serum luteinizing hormone and FSH alone
A morning (8-10 AM) fasting total testosterone, repeated to confirm
Free testosterone by direct analog immunoassay
stages.explanation: Testosterone has a diurnal rhythm peaking in the morning, so a morning (8-10 AM) total testosterone is the recommended screening test, and a low value should be confirmed on a second morning sample before diagnosis. A single random afternoon level is unreliable because levels fall later in the day and may be falsely low. LH and FSH help localize the lesion (primary vs secondary) but do not establish testosterone deficiency on their own. Direct analog free-testosterone immunoassays are inaccurate; if free testosterone is needed (e.g., altered SHBG), equilibrium dialysis or a calculated free testosterone using SHBG and albumin is preferred. Therefore the confirmed low morning total testosterone is the correct first step.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total testosterone (8 AM, day 1): 210 ng/dL
Total testosterone (8 AM, day 2): 235 ng/dL
LH: 3.8 mIU/mL (normal)
FSH: 4.1 mIU/mL (normal)
Prolactin: 11 ng/mL (normal)
SHBG: 24 nmol/L
HbA1c: 7.4%
Hematocrit: 44%
PSA: 1.1 ng/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His confirmed low testosterone with inappropriately normal (non-elevated) LH and FSH is most consistent with which pattern?
options: Primary hypogonadism (testicular failure)
Secondary (hypogonadotropic) hypogonadism
Androgen insensitivity syndrome
Normal aging with no hypogonadism
stages.explanation: Low testosterone with gonadotropins that are low or inappropriately normal indicates secondary (hypogonadotropic) hypogonadism, where the pituitary-hypothalamic axis fails to up-regulate LH/FSH in response to low testosterone. In an obese diabetic man with obstructive sleep apnea, functional secondary hypogonadism is common and partly reversible with weight loss and OSA treatment. Primary hypogonadism would show elevated LH and FSH because the testes fail and feedback inhibition is lost. Androgen insensitivity presents with abnormal sexual development and high testosterone, not this picture. 'Normal aging' is incorrect because he has confirmed low levels plus symptoms; while levels decline with age, a confirmed low value with symptoms warrants the diagnosis and a search for reversible causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before starting testosterone replacement, which intervention should be prioritized in this particular patient?
options: Immediate testosterone gel without further evaluation
Address reversible contributors: weight loss and evaluation/treatment of obstructive sleep apnea
Start a dopamine agonist for the normal prolactin
Begin clomiphene to suppress LH
stages.explanation: He has functional secondary hypogonadism with strong reversible drivers, obesity and likely OSA (snoring, witnessed apneas). Weight loss and treating OSA can meaningfully raise endogenous testosterone and improve symptoms, so these should be prioritized and pursued alongside any decision about replacement. Starting testosterone immediately ignores reversible causes and can worsen untreated OSA and erythrocytosis. A dopamine agonist is wrong because prolactin is normal. Clomiphene is a SERM that raises (not suppresses) LH and testosterone and may be considered in men wishing to preserve fertility, but it is not the priority first step here and the answer mischaracterizes its mechanism. Optimizing reversible factors is the best initial management.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: If testosterone replacement is started, which monitoring and risk counseling point is most important?
options: Testosterone improves and protects fertility, so no contraception is needed
Monitor hematocrit and PSA; testosterone can cause erythrocytosis and may suppress spermatogenesis
Routine prostate biopsy is required before therapy in all men
Testosterone is contraindicated only if hematocrit exceeds 38%
stages.explanation: Exogenous testosterone suppresses LH/FSH and intratesticular testosterone, impairing spermatogenesis and reducing fertility, and it commonly raises hematocrit (erythrocytosis), so baseline and follow-up hematocrit and PSA monitoring are essential, typically at 3-6 months and then annually. Therapy should be held if hematocrit rises above roughly 54%. It is false that testosterone protects fertility, the opposite is true, which is why men desiring fertility may use clomiphene or hCG instead. Routine prostate biopsy is not required before therapy; PSA and DRE-guided surveillance suffice. The 38% threshold is incorrect; the relevant erythrocytosis cutoff is approximately 54%. Thus hematocrit and PSA monitoring with fertility counseling is the key point.

cases.diagnosis: Testosterone deficiency (functional secondary hypogonadism)
pearls: Confirm low testosterone with two morning (8-10 AM) total testosterone levels before diagnosing hypogonadism.
Low testosterone with low or inappropriately normal LH/FSH defines secondary hypogonadism; obesity and OSA are common reversible causes.
Treat reversible contributors (weight loss, OSA) before or alongside considering replacement.
Monitor hematocrit and PSA on therapy; exogenous testosterone suppresses spermatogenesis, so use clomiphene/hCG when fertility is desired.

cases.title: Spells of Pounding Headache and Sweating
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and drenching sweats with spikes in blood pressure.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman reports six months of paroxysmal episodes lasting 15-30 minutes featuring pounding headache, palpitations, profuse diaphoresis, pallor, and a sense of impending doom. Episodes are sometimes triggered by bending over or exertion and resolve spontaneously. Between spells she feels well. She has been told her blood pressure is 'all over the place,' running normal at times and 190/110 at others. She has lost 4 kg unintentionally. PMH: anxiety, otherwise healthy. Medications: none regular; she tried sertraline without benefit. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as a teacher. Family history is notable for a paternal uncle with 'an adrenal tumor' and a cousin with thyroid cancer. Review of systems: no chest pain, no syncope, occasional tremor and constipation during spells. She denies caffeine excess or stimulant use. She is worried these are panic attacks but notes her physical symptoms are dramatic and stereotyped.
cases.exam: Anxious, slender woman, currently mildly tachycardic. Skin warm with mild diaphoresis. No exophthalmos or thyromegaly. Cardiac exam: tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender, no palpable mass or bruit. Fine resting tremor of the hands. No café-au-lait macules or neurofibromas, and no mucosal neuromas noted. Fundoscopy without hypertensive hemorrhages. Neurologic exam nonfocal. No lower-extremity edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the classic triad of episodic headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis with paroxysmal hypertension, which is the best initial biochemical test?
options: Plasma or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines
Serum cortisol with overnight dexamethasone suppression
Aldosterone-to-renin ratio
Random serum catecholamine level during a symptom-free period
stages.explanation: Pheochromocytoma is best screened with fractionated metanephrines (plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines) because metanephrines are produced continuously by tumor metabolism and are far more sensitive than catecholamines, which are secreted episodically and can be normal between spells. A random catecholamine level during a symptom-free period is therefore likely to miss the diagnosis. Dexamethasone suppression screens for cortisol excess (Cushing), not catecholamine excess. The aldosterone-to-renin ratio screens for primary hyperaldosteronism, which causes sustained hypertension with hypokalemia rather than dramatic paroxysmal spells. Fractionated metanephrines are the high-sensitivity test of choice for this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Plasma free metanephrines: 4.8-fold above upper limit of normal
24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines: markedly elevated
Normetanephrine: markedly elevated
Serum potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
Glucose: 132 mg/dL
CT abdomen: 4.5 cm right adrenal mass, heterogeneous, unenhanced attenuation 38 HU, with avid contrast enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a 4.5 cm right adrenal mass with high unenhanced attenuation (38 HU). How do these imaging features fit the biochemical diagnosis?
options: High attenuation and avid enhancement argue strongly for a benign lipid-rich adenoma
The findings are characteristic of pheochromocytoma, which is lipid-poor with high unenhanced attenuation
Attenuation of 38 HU is diagnostic of myelolipoma
Low attenuation (<10 HU) would be expected for a pheochromocytoma
stages.explanation: Pheochromocytomas are typically lipid-poor and therefore show high unenhanced attenuation (usually >20 HU, here 38 HU), are often heterogeneous, and enhance avidly, fitting the biochemical confirmation. A benign lipid-rich adenoma is characterized by LOW unenhanced attenuation (<10 HU), the opposite of this lesion, so it is incorrect. Myelolipoma contains macroscopic fat and shows markedly negative attenuation (around -30 to -100 HU), not 38 HU. The claim that pheochromocytoma should be <10 HU is wrong; lipid-poor lesions like pheochromocytoma have high unenhanced attenuation. The imaging is fully consistent with a catecholamine-secreting adrenal pheochromocytoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient is scheduled for adrenalectomy. What is the most important preoperative preparation?
options: Start a beta-blocker first to control tachycardia, then add other agents
Begin alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) 10-14 days preoperatively, then add beta-blockade and volume expansion
Proceed directly to surgery with intraoperative blood pressure control only
Give high-dose diuretics to lower blood pressure before surgery
stages.explanation: Adequate preoperative alpha-blockade started 10-14 days before surgery is essential to prevent catastrophic intraoperative hypertensive crisis during tumor manipulation; beta-blockade is added only AFTER alpha-blockade to control reflex tachycardia, and liberal salt and fluid intake expands the contracted intravascular volume. Starting a beta-blocker first is dangerous because unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction can precipitate a hypertensive crisis and pulmonary edema. Proceeding directly to surgery without blockade risks lethal catecholamine surges. Diuretics worsen the already volume-contracted state and do not address catecholamine excess. Sequential alpha-then-beta blockade with volume expansion is the standard of care.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Given her family history (uncle with adrenal tumor, cousin with thyroid cancer), what additional step is most appropriate?
options: Reassure her that pheochromocytomas are never hereditary
Refer for genetic counseling and testing (e.g., RET, VHL, SDHx, NF1) and lifelong biochemical surveillance
Order only a one-time post-op metanephrine level and discharge
Begin empiric thyroidectomy because of the family history
stages.explanation: A substantial proportion of pheochromocytomas are hereditary, and her family history (a relative with an adrenal tumor and another with thyroid cancer, suggesting possible MEN2) makes germline testing important; referral for genetic counseling and testing for RET, VHL, SDHx, and NF1, plus lifelong biochemical surveillance for recurrence and contralateral or extra-adrenal disease, is appropriate. Saying these tumors are never hereditary is false. A single post-op metanephrine with discharge is inadequate because recurrence and metachronous tumors occur and require ongoing follow-up. Empiric thyroidectomy is inappropriate without first identifying a RET mutation and confirming medullary thyroid risk. Genetic evaluation with surveillance is the correct step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During induction of anesthesia she develops BP 250/140 mmHg with tachycardia from tumor manipulation. What is the best immediate agent?
options: Intravenous metoprolol
Intravenous phentolamine (or nitroprusside) titrated to blood pressure
Oral nifedipine
Intravenous normal saline bolus only
stages.explanation: An intraoperative hypertensive crisis from catecholamine release requires a rapidly titratable agent; intravenous phentolamine (a short-acting alpha-blocker) or sodium nitroprusside is ideal for acute control. Intravenous metoprolol is inappropriate as monotherapy in this setting because beta-blockade without adequate alpha-blockade leaves unopposed alpha vasoconstriction that can worsen the crisis. Oral nifedipine has slow, unpredictable onset and is unsuitable for an acute intraoperative surge. A saline bolus alone does not lower the dangerously high pressure and could worsen it. A rapid-acting IV vasodilator/alpha-blocker is the correct immediate treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Adrenal pheochromocytoma
pearls: The classic triad is episodic headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis with paroxysmal hypertension; screen with fractionated metanephrines.
Pheochromocytomas are lipid-poor: high unenhanced CT attenuation (>20 HU) and avid enhancement, unlike lipid-rich adenomas (<10 HU).
Preoperative alpha-blockade for 10-14 days precedes beta-blockade and volume expansion; never start beta-blockade first.
Many pheochromocytomas are hereditary (RET/MEN2, VHL, SDHx, NF1); offer genetic testing and lifelong surveillance.

cases.title: Unexpected Mass Found on a Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: An adrenal mass was discovered incidentally on a CT scan ordered for another reason.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is referred after a CT of the abdomen, obtained to evaluate a kidney stone with flank pain, incidentally revealed a left adrenal mass. He feels well and denies the episodic headaches, palpitations, sweating, weight changes, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness, or new hypertension. PMH: hypertension well controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily, and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No allergies. He has never had cancer. Social history: former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 10 years ago), occasional alcohol, retired electrician. Family history: father with prostate cancer; no known adrenal or endocrine tumors. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable; he reports stable weight, normal energy, and no abdominal pain other than the resolved stone symptoms. He takes no glucocorticoids and uses no supplements. He is anxious about whether the mass is cancer and what testing he needs.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. No Cushingoid features: no moon facies, supraclavicular fat pad, abdominal striae, or proximal muscle weakness. No virilization. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft, nontender, no palpable mass or bruit. No lower-extremity edema. Skin without hyperpigmentation or thinning. Neurologic exam intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For any incidentally discovered adrenal mass, the workup hinges on answering which two questions?
options: Is it painful, and does it cause flank tenderness?
Is it hormonally functional, and is it likely malignant?
Is it on the left or right, and is the patient symptomatic?
Does it enhance, and is the patient hypertensive?
stages.explanation: Every adrenal incidentaloma must be evaluated for two things: whether it is hormonally functional (biochemical workup) and whether it is malignant (imaging characteristics and size). These determine whether resection or surveillance is needed. Pain and flank tenderness are nonspecific and usually relate to the unrelated reason the scan was obtained, not to the adrenal mass itself. Laterality alone does not guide management, and symptom status can be misleading because subclinical hormone excess and early malignancy are often asymptomatic. While enhancement and hypertension are individual data points, the overarching framework is function plus malignancy risk, making that the correct answer.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which set of biochemical tests best screens an adrenal incidentaloma for hormonal function?
options: Only a morning cortisol level
1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test, plasma/urine fractionated metanephrines, and (if hypertensive) aldosterone-to-renin ratio
Thyroid function tests and prolactin
Serum calcium and PTH
stages.explanation: The standard functional screen for an adrenal incidentaloma includes a 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (autonomous cortisol secretion), plasma free or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines (pheochromocytoma), and, in hypertensive or hypokalemic patients, an aldosterone-to-renin ratio (primary aldosteronism). A morning cortisol alone is inadequate because it does not assess autonomous secretion. Thyroid tests and prolactin assess the pituitary-thyroid axes, not the adrenal. Calcium and PTH evaluate parathyroid disease, unrelated to adrenal function. The three-test adrenal panel is the correct screen, and given his hypertension the aldosterone-to-renin ratio is indicated.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression: morning cortisol 1.2 µg/dL (suppressed, <1.8)
Plasma free metanephrines: normal
Aldosterone-to-renin ratio: not elevated
Serum potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
Noncontrast CT: 2.2 cm left adrenal mass, homogeneous, unenhanced attenuation 6 HU, smooth margins

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The mass is 2.2 cm, homogeneous, with unenhanced attenuation of 6 HU, and all hormone testing is normal. What is the best interpretation?
options: Features of a benign, nonfunctioning lipid-rich adenoma
Imaging characteristic of adrenocortical carcinoma
Findings diagnostic of pheochromocytoma
Typical appearance of a metastasis
stages.explanation: An adrenal mass with low unenhanced attenuation (≤10 HU), small size, homogeneity, and smooth margins, combined with a negative hormonal workup, is characteristic of a benign nonfunctioning lipid-rich adenoma. Adrenocortical carcinoma is typically large (often >4-6 cm), heterogeneous, irregular, and high attenuation with rapid growth, none of which apply here. Pheochromocytoma is lipid-poor with high unenhanced attenuation and would have elevated metanephrines, which were normal. Metastases are usually high attenuation, often bilateral or in patients with known primary cancer, which he does not have. The constellation strongly favors a benign adenoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this benign-appearing, nonfunctioning 2.2 cm adenoma?
options: Immediate adrenalectomy
Image-guided biopsy of the adrenal mass
No surgery needed; reassurance with conservative follow-up since it is small, benign-appearing, and nonfunctioning
Lifelong daily glucocorticoid replacement
stages.explanation: A small (<4 cm), benign-appearing (≤10 HU), nonfunctioning adrenal mass does not require resection; reassurance with conservative management is appropriate, and many guidelines do not mandate repeat imaging for a clearly benign small adenoma, though some clinicians repeat hormonal/imaging assessment selectively. Immediate adrenalectomy is reserved for functional tumors, lesions with worrisome imaging, or size generally above about 4 cm. Adrenal biopsy is contraindicated as a routine step, both because it rarely distinguishes adrenal adenoma from carcinoma and because biopsy of an unsuspected pheochromocytoma can trigger a crisis. Lifelong glucocorticoids are unnecessary since the gland is functioning normally. Conservative management is correct.

cases.diagnosis: Adrenal incidentaloma (benign nonfunctioning adenoma)
pearls: Every adrenal incidentaloma needs two questions answered: is it functional, and is it malignant?
Functional screen: 1-mg dexamethasone suppression, fractionated metanephrines, and aldosterone-to-renin ratio if hypertensive/hypokalemic.
Unenhanced attenuation ≤10 HU plus small size and homogeneity strongly suggests a benign lipid-rich adenoma.
Avoid biopsy of adrenal masses; resect functional tumors, worrisome imaging, or size generally >4 cm.

cases.title: Stubborn High Blood Pressure and Cramps
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficult-to-control high blood pressure with muscle cramps and frequent urination.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman is referred for hypertension that remains elevated despite three medications. She reports muscle cramps, intermittent weakness, polyuria, and nocturia. She denies headache spells, palpitations, or diaphoresis. PMH: hypertension diagnosed 4 years ago. Medications: lisinopril 40 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; she takes no NSAIDs or licorice. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, minimal alcohol, works in administration; normal salt intake by report. Family history: mother with hypertension; no known endocrine tumors. Review of systems: no edema, no weight change, no flushing. A prior potassium of 3.2 mEq/L was attributed to the thiazide, but it remained low after the dose was held. She has no kidney disease history. She is frustrated that her blood pressure 'won't come down' and is concerned about the persistent leg cramps and frequent nighttime urination interfering with sleep.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman, mildly hypertensive in clinic. No Cushingoid features. Thyroid normal. Cardiac exam regular without murmurs; no S3 or S4. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft and nontender; no renal bruit appreciated. No peripheral edema. Mild diffuse muscle weakness noted on confrontation but no focal deficit. Deep tendon reflexes normal. No carpopedal spasm at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with resistant hypertension and unprovoked hypokalemia, what is the most appropriate screening test?
options: 24-hour urinary metanephrines
Plasma aldosterone concentration and plasma renin activity to calculate the aldosterone-to-renin ratio
Overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Renal artery duplex ultrasound first
stages.explanation: Resistant hypertension plus spontaneous (or easily provoked) hypokalemia is a classic trigger to screen for primary hyperaldosteronism, and the recommended test is the plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR): a high aldosterone with suppressed renin yields an elevated ratio. Urinary metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, which causes paroxysmal symptoms she lacks. Dexamethasone suppression evaluates cortisol excess (Cushing), not the aldosterone-renin axis. Renal artery imaging evaluates renovascular hypertension, which typically does not cause this degree of hypokalemia with high aldosterone and is not the first test for this biochemical pattern. The ARR is the correct screen.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Plasma aldosterone concentration: 28 ng/dL
Plasma renin activity: 0.3 ng/mL/hr (suppressed)
Aldosterone-to-renin ratio: 93 (markedly elevated)
Serum potassium: 3.1 mEq/L
Serum sodium: 144 mEq/L
Serum bicarbonate: 31 mEq/L
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The aldosterone-to-renin ratio is markedly elevated. What is the appropriate next step before deciding on treatment?
options: Proceed directly to adrenalectomy
Perform a confirmatory test (e.g., saline infusion or oral salt-loading) to demonstrate non-suppressible aldosterone
Start spironolactone immediately and stop the workup
Order a PET scan of the whole body
stages.explanation: A positive ARR is a screening result and should be confirmed with a test demonstrating that aldosterone fails to suppress with sodium loading (saline infusion test or oral salt-loading with 24-hour urinary aldosterone), establishing autonomous aldosterone secretion. Proceeding directly to adrenalectomy is premature without confirmation and without adrenal vein sampling to lateralize the source. Starting spironolactone and stopping the workup forgoes the chance to identify a surgically curable unilateral adenoma and interferes with subsequent testing. A whole-body PET scan is not part of the standard aldosteronism algorithm. Biochemical confirmation is the correct next step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirmation, CT shows a 1.4 cm left adrenal nodule. The patient is 48 and would consider surgery. What is the next step to guide treatment?
options: Adrenal vein sampling to determine whether aldosterone excess is unilateral or bilateral
Bilateral adrenalectomy
Immediate left adrenalectomy based on CT alone
Lifelong amlodipine monotherapy
stages.explanation: In patients who are candidates for surgery, adrenal vein sampling (AVS) is the gold standard to distinguish unilateral aldosterone hypersecretion (curable by unilateral adrenalectomy) from bilateral hyperplasia (treated medically), because CT can miss small adenomas or show a nonfunctioning incidentaloma that misleads surgical decisions. Bilateral adrenalectomy is rarely indicated and causes adrenal insufficiency. Operating on the CT nodule alone risks removing a nonfunctioning adenoma while leaving the true source. Amlodipine monotherapy would undertreat and ignore the surgically curable possibility. AVS appropriately lateralizes the source before surgery in a willing candidate.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: If AVS instead showed bilateral aldosterone hypersecretion (idiopathic hyperaldosteronism), what is the preferred treatment?
options: Unilateral adrenalectomy of the larger gland
A mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist such as spironolactone or eplerenone
Continue thiazide diuretic alone
High-dose loop diuretic monotherapy
stages.explanation: Bilateral disease (idiopathic hyperaldosteronism) is managed medically with a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist such as spironolactone or eplerenone, which controls blood pressure and corrects hypokalemia by blocking aldosterone action. Unilateral adrenalectomy does not cure bilateral disease and would leave residual hypersecretion. A thiazide alone does not address the underlying aldosterone excess and can worsen hypokalemia. Loop diuretic monotherapy similarly fails to block aldosterone and aggravates potassium wasting. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonism is the correct treatment for bilateral primary aldosteronism.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome)
pearls: Screen for primary aldosteronism in resistant hypertension with hypokalemia using the aldosterone-to-renin ratio (high aldosterone, suppressed renin).
Confirm a positive ARR with a sodium-loading/saline suppression test before treatment decisions.
Adrenal vein sampling lateralizes unilateral (surgical) vs bilateral (medical) disease and outperforms CT alone.
Unilateral adenoma is cured by adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia is treated with spironolactone or eplerenone.

cases.title: Fatty Kidney Mass in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: A kidney mass containing fat was found on imaging during a workup for vague flank discomfort.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman is referred after an abdominal ultrasound and CT obtained for intermittent right flank discomfort revealed a right renal mass. She reports mild dull flank ache but no gross hematuria, fevers, or weight loss. PMH: she has a history of seizures in childhood and was told she has 'a genetic skin condition.' She reports facial bumps since adolescence and some pale patches on her skin. Medications: none currently; no allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, social alcohol, works as a graphic designer; no occupational toxin exposure. Family history: a brother with epilepsy and learning difficulties. Review of systems: no flank pain crises, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea. She denies anticoagulant use. She is otherwise healthy and active. She is concerned the mass might be cancer and wants to understand whether it needs to be removed, especially given that she hopes to start a family soon.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Skin: multiple small reddish-brown papules over the malar region and nose (consistent with facial angiofibromas), several hypopigmented macules on the trunk, and a thickened skin patch on the lower back. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Abdomen soft, nontender, no palpable mass. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Neurologic exam nonfocal. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A renal mass containing macroscopic fat (negative Hounsfield units) on CT is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma
Angiomyolipoma
Simple renal cyst
Transitional cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: Macroscopic fat within a renal mass (attenuation around -20 HU or lower) is essentially diagnostic of angiomyolipoma, a benign tumor of vessels, smooth muscle, and fat. Clear cell renal cell carcinoma almost never contains macroscopic fat; the rare RCC with fat usually also contains calcification, which favors malignancy. A simple renal cyst is fluid-density (near 0 HU) and thin-walled, not fat-containing. Transitional cell (urothelial) carcinoma arises in the collecting system and presents as a soft-tissue filling defect, not a fat-containing parenchymal mass. The presence of macroscopic fat makes angiomyolipoma the correct diagnosis.

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: Her facial angiofibromas, hypopigmented macules, a thickened back lesion, childhood seizures, and an affected brother point to which associated condition?
options: Von Hippel-Lindau disease
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Neurofibromatosis type 1
Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome
stages.explanation: Facial angiofibromas, hypopigmented 'ash-leaf' macules, a shagreen patch (thickened skin on the back), and seizures are hallmark features of tuberous sclerosis complex, which is strongly associated with renal angiomyolipomas, often multiple and bilateral. Von Hippel-Lindau predisposes to clear cell RCC, hemangioblastomas, and pheochromocytoma, not these skin findings. Neurofibromatosis type 1 features café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules, not angiofibromas and ash-leaf spots. Birt-Hogg-Dubé causes fibrofolliculomas, lung cysts, and chromophobe/oncocytic renal tumors, not this constellation. Tuberous sclerosis is the correct association.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen: 3.2 cm right renal mass containing macroscopic fat (attenuation -28 HU), no calcification
Several additional small (<1 cm) fat-containing lesions in both kidneys
No lymphadenopathy, no renal vein thrombus
Hemoglobin: 13.4 g/dL
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL

stages.label: RISK
stages.question: Her dominant angiomyolipoma is 3.2 cm. What primarily determines the risk of spontaneous hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome)?
options: Patient age
Tumor size, with lesions larger than about 4 cm carrying higher bleeding risk
Presence of calcification
Unilateral versus bilateral disease
stages.explanation: Bleeding risk of angiomyolipoma correlates most strongly with size; lesions larger than approximately 4 cm (and those with large aneurysms >5 mm) carry a substantially higher risk of spontaneous, potentially life-threatening retroperitoneal hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome). Patient age is not the principal determinant of hemorrhage risk. Calcification is uncommon in angiomyolipoma and, when present in a renal mass, actually raises concern for malignancy rather than predicting AML bleeding. Whether disease is unilateral or bilateral (common in tuberous sclerosis) does not itself set the bleeding threshold; the size of individual lesions does. Size above roughly 4 cm is the key risk factor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a 3.2 cm asymptomatic angiomyolipoma below the high-risk size threshold, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate radical nephrectomy
Active surveillance with periodic imaging
Selective arterial embolization now to prevent any future growth
Systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic angiomyolipoma below the high-risk size threshold (roughly <4 cm) is managed with active surveillance using periodic imaging to monitor for growth, since these lesions are benign and small ones rarely bleed. Radical nephrectomy is overly aggressive for a benign tumor and unnecessarily sacrifices renal parenchyma, which is especially important in tuberous sclerosis where multiple bilateral lesions threaten long-term renal function. Prophylactic embolization is reserved for larger (>4 cm), growing, symptomatic, or bleeding lesions, not a stable small AML. Chemotherapy has no role for a benign angiomyolipoma (though mTOR inhibitors are used for large/multifocal TSC-associated AMLs). Surveillance is correct here.

cases.diagnosis: Renal angiomyolipoma (tuberous sclerosis-associated)
pearls: Macroscopic fat (negative HU) in a renal mass is essentially diagnostic of angiomyolipoma, a benign tumor.
Multiple/bilateral renal AMLs with angiofibromas, ash-leaf macules, shagreen patch, and seizures suggest tuberous sclerosis complex.
Hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome) risk rises with size, especially lesions >4 cm or aneurysms >5 mm.
Small asymptomatic AMLs are surveilled; embolization or nephron-sparing surgery (or mTOR inhibitors in TSC) is for large/symptomatic lesions.

cases.title: Solid Kidney Mass with a Central Scar
cases.chiefComplaint: A solid kidney mass was discovered on imaging during an unrelated workup.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is referred after a contrast-enhanced CT, obtained for evaluation of abdominal pain later attributed to diverticulitis, revealed a solid right renal mass. He has no flank pain, gross hematuria, weight loss, or fevers. PMH: hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily, and well-controlled GERD on omeprazole. No allergies. Social history: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), occasional alcohol, retired teacher. Family history: no known renal cancers or hereditary syndromes. Review of systems: no night sweats, no bone pain, normal appetite, stable weight. He reports good energy and exercises regularly. He has no history of kidney stones or recurrent urinary infections. He is anxious because he read online that solid kidney masses are usually cancer, and he wants to know whether the central scar seen on his scan means it is benign and whether he can avoid surgery.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. No scleral icterus. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft, nontender; no palpable flank mass and no costovertebral angle tenderness. No supraclavicular or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No lower-extremity edema. No varicocele. Skin without lesions. Neurologic exam nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A solid, enhancing renal mass with a central stellate scar on CT is described. How should this finding be interpreted?
options: A central scar is pathognomonic for a benign oncocytoma and excludes cancer
A central scar can occur in oncocytoma but also in renal cell carcinoma, so imaging cannot reliably distinguish them
A central scar confirms angiomyolipoma
A central scar means the lesion is a simple cyst
stages.explanation: A central stellate scar and spoke-wheel enhancement are classically associated with oncocytoma, but these features are not specific and can also be seen in renal cell carcinoma (particularly chromophobe RCC), so imaging cannot reliably distinguish a benign oncocytoma from malignancy. It is incorrect to call the scar pathognomonic or to say it excludes cancer. Angiomyolipoma is identified by macroscopic fat, not a central scar. A simple cyst is fluid-density and nonenhancing, the opposite of this solid enhancing mass. Because oncocytoma and RCC overlap on imaging, the diagnosis cannot be assumed from the scar alone.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: 4.6 cm solid right renal mass, avidly enhancing, with a central stellate low-density scar
No macroscopic fat
No renal vein or IVC thrombus
No lymphadenopathy or metastatic lesions
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR >60
Hemoglobin: 14.2 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The 4.6 cm solid enhancing mass has no macroscopic fat. Would percutaneous biopsy reliably resolve whether this is oncocytoma versus chromophobe RCC?
options: Yes, biopsy definitively separates oncocytoma from chromophobe RCC in all cases
No, oncocytoma and chromophobe RCC share overlapping (oncocytic) features and biopsy is often nondiagnostic or indeterminate
Biopsy is contraindicated for all renal masses
Biopsy should be replaced by urine cytology for this lesion
stages.explanation: Oncocytoma and chromophobe RCC both arise from intercalated cells and share overlapping oncocytic histologic features, so percutaneous biopsy is frequently nondiagnostic or cannot confidently exclude malignancy; hybrid oncocytic tumors further blur the distinction. Thus biopsy does not definitively separate them in all cases. Biopsy is not contraindicated for all renal masses, it is used selectively, but its limitation here is the inability to reliably exclude chromophobe RCC. Urine cytology detects urothelial (collecting-system) cancers, not parenchymal oncocytic tumors, so it cannot characterize this mass. The diagnostic overlap is the key teaching point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a 4.6 cm enhancing solid renal mass that cannot be reliably distinguished from RCC, what is the most appropriate management in this fit patient?
options: Active surveillance with imaging only
Surgical resection, preferably partial (nephron-sparing) nephrectomy
Empiric systemic immunotherapy
Radiation therapy to the renal mass
stages.explanation: Because oncocytoma cannot be reliably distinguished from renal cell carcinoma on imaging or biopsy, an enhancing solid renal mass of this size in a fit patient is treated with surgical resection, and nephron-sparing partial nephrectomy is preferred for a localized mass to preserve renal function while providing definitive histology. Active surveillance is reserved for small masses (often <2 cm) or poor surgical candidates, not a 4.6 cm indeterminate lesion in a healthy man. Empiric systemic immunotherapy is for metastatic RCC, not a localized resectable mass of unknown histology. Radiation is not standard primary therapy for a resectable renal mass. Resection is correct.

stages.label: OUTCOME
stages.question: Final pathology confirms a renal oncocytoma with no malignant features. What is the appropriate prognosis and follow-up message?
options: It is a benign tumor; surgical excision is curative and aggressive systemic therapy is not needed
It is metastatic and requires chemotherapy
It will inevitably recur and a second nephrectomy is needed
He now requires lifelong dialysis
stages.explanation: Renal oncocytoma is a benign neoplasm; complete surgical excision is curative and no adjuvant chemotherapy, immunotherapy, or radiation is required, though routine post-nephrectomy renal-function and imaging follow-up is reasonable. It is not metastatic and does not warrant chemotherapy. Oncocytomas do not inevitably recur after complete excision, so a planned second nephrectomy is unnecessary. A partial nephrectomy preserves the kidney, and with a normal contralateral kidney the patient will not require dialysis. Reassurance that the lesion was benign and the operation curative is the correct message.

cases.diagnosis: Renal oncocytoma
pearls: A central stellate scar suggests oncocytoma but is not specific and overlaps with renal cell carcinoma (especially chromophobe).
Oncocytoma and chromophobe RCC share oncocytic features, so biopsy is often nondiagnostic and cannot exclude malignancy.
Indeterminate enhancing solid renal masses are resected; partial (nephron-sparing) nephrectomy is preferred for localized tumors.
Confirmed oncocytoma is benign and cured by excision; no adjuvant systemic therapy is needed.

cases.title: Blood in the Urine and a Filling Defect
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless visible blood in the urine prompting imaging that showed an abnormality in the kidney's collecting system.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents with three episodes of painless gross hematuria over two months. She denies dysuria, fever, or flank pain, though she had one episode of mild right flank ache. No weight loss. PMH: hypertension and prior recurrent urinary tract infections. She has a 40 pack-year smoking history and worked for years in a textile/dye facility. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily; she takes no anticoagulants. No allergies. Social history: current smoker, social alcohol. Family history: noncontributory; no Lynch syndrome known, though her mother had colon cancer in her 60s. Review of systems: no bone pain, no hemoptysis. She has not had recent instrumentation. Urinalysis confirmed microscopic and gross hematuria without infection. Given painless hematuria in an older smoker, she was referred for a full hematuria evaluation. She is anxious about the cause and asks whether her smoking and dye exposure are relevant.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. No scleral pallor. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft, nontender; mild right flank fullness without a discrete mass, no costovertebral angle tenderness at rest. No suprapubic tenderness. No lymphadenopathy. No lower-extremity edema. Pelvic exam unremarkable. Neurologic exam nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For painless gross hematuria in a 68-year-old smoker, what is the most appropriate initial imaging and evaluation?
options: Renal ultrasound alone
CT urography plus cystoscopy (with urine cytology)
Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
MRI brain
stages.explanation: Gross hematuria in an older smoker mandates a complete evaluation of the entire urinary tract: CT urography to image the upper tracts (renal parenchyma and collecting systems) and cystoscopy to inspect the bladder and urethra, typically with urine cytology. Renal ultrasound alone is insufficient because it poorly evaluates the urothelium and small upper-tract lesions and does not assess the bladder. A KUB cannot detect soft-tissue urothelial tumors. MRI brain is irrelevant to hematuria. The combination of CT urography and cystoscopy is the standard hematuria workup and is required here given her risk factors.

stages.label: IMAGING & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
CT urography: 1.8 cm soft-tissue filling defect within the right renal pelvis with associated mild right hydronephrosis
No macroscopic fat; the lesion enhances
No stone; no renal parenchymal mass
Cystoscopy: bladder mucosa normal, no bladder tumor
Urine cytology: positive for high-grade malignant urothelial cells
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A soft-tissue filling defect in the renal pelvis with positive high-grade cytology and a normal bladder is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Renal cell carcinoma of the parenchyma
Upper tract urothelial carcinoma
Radiolucent uric acid stone
Angiomyolipoma
stages.explanation: An enhancing soft-tissue filling defect within the collecting system (renal pelvis) plus positive high-grade urinary cytology indicates upper tract urothelial carcinoma, which arises from the urothelium lining the renal pelvis and ureter. Renal cell carcinoma arises in the parenchyma as a solid mass, not as a collecting-system filling defect, and is usually cytology-negative. A uric acid stone is radiolucent on plain film but is detectable on CT as a calculus, does not enhance, and would not produce malignant cytology. Angiomyolipoma contains macroscopic fat and is a parenchymal mass. The collecting-system filling defect with malignant cytology defines upper tract urothelial carcinoma.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best next step to confirm the diagnosis and assess the upper tract lesion directly?
options: Percutaneous renal mass biopsy
Diagnostic ureteroscopy with biopsy and selective (ureteral) cytology
Repeat urinalysis in 3 months
PET-CT as the sole confirmatory test
stages.explanation: Diagnostic ureteroscopy allows direct visualization of the upper tract lesion, biopsy for histologic confirmation and grading, and selective ureteral washings/cytology, which guide management of upper tract urothelial carcinoma. Percutaneous renal biopsy is avoided for suspected urothelial tumors because of the risk of tract seeding and because it does not access the collecting-system lesion well. Repeating urinalysis delays diagnosis of a high-grade malignancy and is inappropriate. PET-CT may aid staging in select cases but is not the confirmatory diagnostic test for the primary lesion and does not provide tissue. Ureteroscopy with biopsy is the correct confirmatory step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ureteroscopy confirms a high-grade upper tract urothelial carcinoma in the renal pelvis with a normal contralateral kidney. What is the standard treatment?
options: Radical nephroureterectomy with excision of the ipsilateral ureter and bladder cuff
Partial nephrectomy of the lower pole
Active surveillance
Bladder BCG instillation alone
stages.explanation: The standard treatment for high-grade or invasive upper tract urothelial carcinoma with a normal contralateral kidney is radical nephroureterectomy, removing the kidney, the entire ipsilateral ureter, and a cuff of bladder around the ureteral orifice, because urothelial cancer is multifocal and the distal ureteral stump is at high risk of recurrence. Partial nephrectomy addresses parenchymal tumors, not collecting-system urothelial cancer, and would leave at-risk urothelium. Active surveillance is inappropriate for high-grade disease. Intravesical BCG treats non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, not an upper tract tumor. Nephron-sparing/endoscopic options are reserved for low-grade or solitary-kidney situations, which do not apply here.

cases.diagnosis: Upper tract urothelial carcinoma
pearls: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker requires CT urography plus cystoscopy with cytology.
An enhancing soft-tissue filling defect in the renal pelvis/ureter with positive high-grade cytology suggests upper tract urothelial carcinoma.
Confirm with ureteroscopy and biopsy; avoid percutaneous biopsy due to seeding risk.
High-grade/invasive disease with a normal contralateral kidney is treated by radical nephroureterectomy including a bladder cuff.

cases.title: Worsening Back Pain in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive low back pain now severe enough to limit walking.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man is admitted with three months of progressive low back and hip pain, worse at night and not relieved by acetaminophen. He reports fatigue and a 5 kg weight loss. He has noticed weaker urinary stream and nocturia over the past year. He denies fevers, leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, or incontinence. PMH: hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and acetaminophen as needed. No allergies. Social history: former smoker, retired carpenter, lives with his wife. Family history: brother with prostate cancer. He has not had a PSA checked in years and never had a prostate biopsy. Review of systems: no chest pain or dyspnea, no recent trauma. On admission he was found to have anemia and abnormal labs prompting further evaluation. He is worried the pain is 'just arthritis' but his physicians are concerned about a more serious cause given the systemic features and urinary symptoms.
cases.exam: Older man in moderate discomfort from back pain. Conjunctival pallor. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft, nontender; no flank mass. Spine: tenderness over the lower lumbar vertebrae and right hip. Digital rectal exam: enlarged, firm prostate with a hard nodule and loss of the median sulcus. Lower-extremity strength is full (5/5) bilaterally with intact sensation and normal reflexes; no focal neurologic deficit. No lower-extremity edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a hard prostate nodule, weight loss, anemia, and bone pain, which initial test is most appropriate?
options: Serum PSA
Serum CA 19-9
24-hour urinary metanephrines
Serum testosterone only
stages.explanation: An elderly man with a hard prostate nodule, systemic symptoms, and bone pain is highly suspicious for advanced prostate cancer, so serum PSA is the appropriate initial test; it is markedly elevated in metastatic disease and guides further workup. CA 19-9 is a pancreaticobiliary/GI marker, not relevant here. Urinary metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, which does not fit this presentation. Testosterone alone does not diagnose prostate cancer, though it becomes relevant when planning and monitoring androgen deprivation therapy. PSA is the correct first step and will frame the rest of the evaluation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
PSA: 240 ng/mL
Hemoglobin: 10.1 g/dL
Alkaline phosphatase: 410 U/L (elevated)
Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL
Plain films and CT: multiple sclerotic (osteoblastic) lesions in the lumbar spine and pelvis
Bone scan: increased uptake in lumbar vertebrae, pelvis, and ribs

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: PSA is 240 ng/mL with diffuse osteoblastic bone lesions and an elevated alkaline phosphatase. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Multiple myeloma
Metastatic prostate cancer to bone
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Osteoarthritis with incidental findings
stages.explanation: A very high PSA with diffuse osteoblastic (sclerotic) bone metastases and elevated alkaline phosphatase from increased bone formation is classic for metastatic prostate cancer, which characteristically produces osteoblastic lesions. Multiple myeloma causes lytic ('punched-out') lesions, not sclerotic ones, and is not associated with high PSA. Primary hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcemia with bone resorption, not osteoblastic metastases, and his calcium is normal. Osteoarthritis cannot explain a PSA of 240, anemia, weight loss, and widespread bone uptake. The osteoblastic pattern with markedly elevated PSA confirms metastatic prostate cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of initial systemic therapy for metastatic prostate cancer?
options: Radical prostatectomy
Androgen deprivation therapy (medical or surgical castration), often combined with an additional agent
Exogenous testosterone supplementation
Pelvic radiation alone with no systemic therapy
stages.explanation: Prostate cancer is androgen-driven, so the cornerstone of treatment for metastatic disease is androgen deprivation therapy via medical castration (GnRH agonist/antagonist) or surgical castration (orchiectomy), and contemporary practice intensifies up-front with an additional agent such as an androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor or docetaxel. Radical prostatectomy is a local therapy for organ-confined disease, not metastatic cancer. Exogenous testosterone would fuel tumor growth and is contraindicated. Pelvic radiation alone does not address widespread metastases. Systemic androgen deprivation, often with combination therapy, is the correct initial approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: When starting a GnRH agonist (e.g., leuprolide) for symptomatic bony metastatic disease, what early complication must be prevented?
options: Immediate tumor lysis syndrome
Testosterone 'flare' that can transiently worsen bone pain or cord compression, prevented by adding an antiandrogen
Severe hyperkalemia
Acute pancreatitis
stages.explanation: GnRH agonists initially stimulate the pituitary, causing a transient surge ('flare') in testosterone over the first 1-2 weeks that can worsen bone pain or precipitate spinal cord compression in patients with extensive bony disease; this is prevented by co-administering an antiandrogen (e.g., bicalutamide) around the time of the first injection, or by using a GnRH antagonist, which does not cause a flare. Tumor lysis syndrome is a concern in bulky hematologic malignancies, not typical prostate ADT initiation. Hyperkalemia and acute pancreatitis are not characteristic complications of starting a GnRH agonist. Preventing the testosterone flare is the key consideration.

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE CARE
stages.question: Which supportive measure reduces skeletal-related events in metastatic prostate cancer to bone?
options: Calcium and vitamin D restriction
A bone-targeted agent such as zoledronic acid or denosumab (with calcium and vitamin D supplementation)
Routine prophylactic spinal fusion
High-dose vitamin A
stages.explanation: Bone-targeted agents, the bisphosphonate zoledronic acid or the RANK-ligand inhibitor denosumab, reduce skeletal-related events (fractures, cord compression, need for radiation/surgery) in metastatic prostate cancer to bone and are given with calcium and vitamin D to prevent hypocalcemia. Restricting calcium and vitamin D would worsen bone health and risk hypocalcemia with these agents. Prophylactic spinal fusion is not a routine supportive measure and is reserved for specific instability. High-dose vitamin A has no role and can be toxic. A bone-targeted agent with calcium/vitamin D supplementation is the correct supportive therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic prostate cancer with osteoblastic bone metastases
pearls: A hard prostate nodule with bone pain, weight loss, and high PSA suggests metastatic prostate cancer; osteoblastic lesions are characteristic.
Androgen deprivation therapy (medical or surgical castration), often intensified up front, is the backbone of metastatic disease treatment.
GnRH agonists cause an early testosterone flare; prevent it with an antiandrogen or use a GnRH antagonist.
Zoledronic acid or denosumab (with calcium/vitamin D) reduces skeletal-related events in bony metastatic disease.

cases.title: Sudden Leg Weakness and Trouble Urinating
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly worsening leg weakness with inability to urinate over the past day.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man with known metastatic prostate cancer presents to the emergency department with two days of worsening mid-back pain and one day of progressive bilateral leg weakness, now barely able to stand. He reports numbness in both legs and was unable to urinate this morning, feeling increasingly full and uncomfortable. He also notes new constipation. PMH: prostate cancer with bone metastases, on leuprolide; hypertension. Medications: leuprolide injections, amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen and oxycodone for pain. No allergies. Social history: former smoker, lives with his daughter. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: no fever, no chest pain. The back pain is band-like and worse when lying flat. He is distressed by the weakness and inability to void. His daughter reports he was walking normally a week ago, making the rapid decline alarming and prompting urgent evaluation for a neurologic emergency.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man lying still due to back pain. Suprapubic fullness with a palpable, tender distended bladder. Spine: focal tenderness over the mid-thoracic vertebrae. Lower extremities: weakness graded 3/5 bilaterally at the hips and knees, with a sensory level around the umbilicus and decreased sensation distally. Reflexes are brisk at the knees with bilateral upgoing toes (Babinski). Reduced rectal tone on digital exam. Upper-extremity strength and sensation are normal. No fever or meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A man with metastatic prostate cancer has back pain, bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level, and urinary retention. What must be suspected immediately?
options: Simple benign prostatic obstruction
Metastatic epidural spinal cord compression
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy
Acute gout flare
stages.explanation: Back pain with bilateral lower-extremity weakness, a sensory level, upper motor neuron signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing toes), reduced rectal tone, and urinary retention in a cancer patient is a textbook presentation of metastatic epidural spinal cord compression, an oncologic emergency requiring immediate action. Benign prostatic obstruction can cause retention but does not produce a sensory level, leg weakness, or upper motor neuron signs. Diabetic neuropathy is a slowly progressive distal sensory process, not an acute myelopathy with a sensory level. An acute gout flare causes joint pain, not cord compression with myelopathy. Cord compression must be suspected and addressed urgently.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive imaging, what is the most appropriate immediate medical intervention?
options: Withhold all treatment until MRI is completed
Administer intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) promptly
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics
Give a bolus of intravenous testosterone
stages.explanation: When metastatic epidural spinal cord compression is clinically suspected, high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) should be given immediately to reduce vasogenic edema and help preserve neurologic function while definitive imaging and treatment are arranged; do not wait for the MRI to start steroids. Withholding all treatment risks irreversible paralysis, since neurologic recovery depends on rapid intervention. Antibiotics would be indicated for an epidural abscess, but there is no fever or infectious feature here and they do not treat tumor compression. Intravenous testosterone is contraindicated and would stimulate prostate cancer. Prompt corticosteroids are the correct immediate step.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the imaging study of choice to confirm and localize the cord compression?
options: Plain radiographs of the spine
Urgent MRI of the whole spine with and without contrast
Abdominal ultrasound
Non-contrast head CT
stages.explanation: Urgent MRI of the entire spine is the imaging study of choice because it best visualizes the spinal cord, epidural space, and extent of compression, and imaging the whole spine detects multiple noncontiguous lesions that are common in metastatic disease and change the radiation/surgical field. Plain radiographs may show bony metastases or vertebral collapse but cannot adequately assess the cord or epidural soft tissue. Abdominal ultrasound does not evaluate the spinal canal. A head CT addresses intracranial pathology, not spinal cord compression. Whole-spine MRI is the correct confirmatory study.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He also has acute urinary retention with a tense, palpable bladder. What is the most appropriate immediate management of the bladder?
options: Restrict fluids and observe
Place a urethral (or suprapubic) catheter to decompress the bladder
Give a diuretic to increase urine output
Start an alpha-blocker and discharge
stages.explanation: Acute urinary retention with a tense, painful bladder, here a neurogenic consequence of cord compression, requires immediate decompression by placing a urethral catheter (or suprapubic catheter if urethral access fails) to relieve pain, prevent bladder injury, and protect the upper tracts. Fluid restriction and observation do not relieve an obstructed, full bladder and risk hydronephrosis and renal injury. A diuretic increases urine production into an outlet-obstructed bladder, worsening distension. Starting an alpha-blocker and discharging is inappropriate in this emergency with neurologic compromise; the patient needs admission, catheterization, and definitive treatment of the cord compression. Catheter decompression is the correct immediate step.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: After steroids and MRI confirm epidural cord compression, what is the definitive treatment approach?
options: Observation alone
Urgent radiation therapy and/or surgical decompression, with oncology and neurosurgery involvement
Increase the leuprolide dose only
Outpatient physical therapy referral
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of metastatic epidural cord compression is urgent radiation therapy and/or surgical decompression and stabilization, decided with oncology and neurosurgery/radiation oncology; surgery followed by radiation is favored for selected patients with spinal instability, a single area of compression, or good performance status, while radiation is used when surgery is not feasible. Observation alone risks permanent paralysis. Simply increasing leuprolide does not relieve the mechanical compression and the patient is already on androgen deprivation. Outpatient physical therapy is wholly inadequate for an acute neurologic emergency. Urgent decompressive treatment with multidisciplinary input is the correct approach.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic prostate cancer with malignant epidural spinal cord compression
pearls: Back pain with bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level, UMN signs, and retention in a cancer patient is cord compression until proven otherwise.
Give IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) immediately on clinical suspicion, before MRI confirmation.
Whole-spine MRI is the imaging of choice to localize compression and detect multilevel disease.
Definitive therapy is urgent radiation and/or surgical decompression; relieve coexisting urinary retention with a catheter.

cases.title: Blood in the Urine with Failing Kidney Numbers
cases.chiefComplaint: Visible blood in the urine and rising kidney function tests found on admission.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is admitted after weeks of intermittent painless gross hematuria and clots, now with right flank discomfort. Outpatient labs showed a rising creatinine, prompting admission. He has had increasing urinary frequency and a sensation of incomplete emptying. He denies fever or dysuria. PMH: hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He has a 45 pack-year smoking history and prior occupational exposure to industrial chemicals. Medications: metformin (held on admission), lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily; no anticoagulants. No allergies. Social history: current smoker, retired factory worker. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: mild fatigue, no weight loss reported, no bone pain. He has not had prior cystoscopy. On admission imaging and cystoscopy were planned to evaluate the hematuria and the cause of his worsening renal function. He is anxious about the blood and clots and about why his kidney numbers are getting worse.
cases.exam: Older man in mild discomfort. No pallor. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft; mild right costovertebral angle fullness and tenderness. Suprapubic region nontender, no palpable distended bladder. No lower-extremity edema. Digital rectal exam: smooth, mildly enlarged prostate without nodule. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Painless gross hematuria with clots in a 70-year-old smoker requires which evaluation?
options: Cystoscopy plus upper-tract imaging (CT urography) with urine cytology
A trial of antibiotics for presumed cystitis
Reassurance and repeat urinalysis in 6 months
Renal biopsy as the first step
stages.explanation: Painless gross hematuria, especially with clots, in an older smoker demands a complete urinary tract evaluation: cystoscopy to inspect the bladder and urethra, CT urography to image the upper tracts, and urine cytology. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without evidence of infection and dangerously delay diagnosis of a likely malignancy. Reassurance with delayed repeat testing risks progression of an undiagnosed bladder cancer. Renal biopsy evaluates glomerular/parenchymal disease and is not the first step for gross hematuria with clots, which points to a urologic (structural) source. Cystoscopy with upper-tract imaging and cytology is the correct workup.

stages.label: IMAGING & ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
CT urography: large right-sided bladder mass near the right ureteral orifice with right hydroureteronephrosis
No upper-tract filling defect; left kidney normal
Cystoscopy: broad-based sessile bladder tumor at the right trigone obstructing the right ureteral orifice
Creatinine: 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
Potassium: 4.6 mEq/L
Urine cytology: high-grade malignant urothelial cells

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A trigonal bladder mass obstructs the right ureteral orifice, causing hydronephrosis and a rising creatinine. What explains the renal impairment?
options: Intrinsic glomerulonephritis
Postrenal (obstructive) acute kidney injury from ureteral orifice obstruction
Prerenal azotemia from dehydration
Acute tubular necrosis from contrast
stages.explanation: The bladder tumor obstructs the right ureteral orifice, producing right hydroureteronephrosis and postrenal (obstructive) acute kidney injury; with the left kidney's reserve, creatinine has risen as the obstructed side fails to drain. Glomerulonephritis would present with an active urinary sediment (dysmorphic RBCs, casts, proteinuria), not a mechanical filling defect obstructing the ureter. Prerenal azotemia from dehydration would not cause unilateral hydronephrosis with a visible obstructing mass. Contrast-induced acute tubular necrosis is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not explain the hydronephrosis seen before the obstruction is relieved. The obstructive (postrenal) mechanism is correct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which step both relieves the obstruction (protecting renal function) and provides tissue for diagnosis and staging?
options: Immediate radical cystectomy without prior biopsy
Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT), with ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy as needed to relieve obstruction
Start systemic chemotherapy before any tissue diagnosis
Observation with serial creatinine monitoring
stages.explanation: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) provides histologic diagnosis and determines whether the tumor invades the muscularis propria (critical for staging), and obstruction can be relieved with a ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy to recover renal function. Proceeding directly to radical cystectomy without tissue diagnosis and staging is inappropriate. Starting systemic chemotherapy before any tissue diagnosis is unsafe and uninformed, and many regimens require adequate renal function, which must first be restored by relieving obstruction. Observation alone allows ongoing renal injury and tumor progression. TURBT plus decompression is the correct step.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: TURBT confirms muscle-invasive (T2) high-grade urothelial carcinoma with no distant metastases. What is the standard definitive treatment?
options: Intravesical BCG alone
Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy followed by radical cystectomy (or bladder-preserving chemoradiation in selected patients)
Active surveillance
Topical chemotherapy instillation only
stages.explanation: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer (≥T2) without metastases is treated with neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy followed by radical cystectomy with urinary diversion, which improves survival; selected patients may pursue bladder-preserving trimodal therapy (maximal TURBT plus chemoradiation). Intravesical BCG is for non-muscle-invasive (high-risk Ta/T1/CIS) disease and is inadequate for muscle invasion. Active surveillance is not appropriate for invasive high-grade cancer. Topical/intravesical chemotherapy does not penetrate or treat muscle-invasive disease. Note that cisplatin eligibility depends on adequate renal function, underscoring why the obstruction was first relieved. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with radical cystectomy is the standard.

cases.diagnosis: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer with obstructive hydronephrosis
pearls: Painless gross hematuria with clots in an older smoker requires cystoscopy, CT urography, and urine cytology.
A tumor at the trigone can obstruct a ureteral orifice, causing hydronephrosis and postrenal acute kidney injury.
TURBT establishes diagnosis and muscle-invasion status; relieve obstruction with a stent or nephrostomy to recover renal function.
Muscle-invasive disease is treated with neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radical cystectomy (or chemoradiation in select cases).

cases.title: Confusion and Cloudy Urine in a Bedbound Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: New low-grade fever and increasing confusion in a patient with a chronic indwelling catheter.
