stages.explanation: Pathologic post-obstructive diuresis is defined by output that continues despite achieving euvolemia and is accompanied by evidence of volume depletion (orthostasis), electrolyte wasting (hypokalemia, natriuresis with urine Na 70), and worsening renal function, all present here. A first-hour output that quickly tapers describes the normal physiologic diuresis that needs only observation. A low urine sodium with concentrated urine indicates appropriate sodium avidity and argues against ongoing pathologic salt/water wasting. Resolution of the palpable bladder simply confirms successful drainage and says nothing about whether the diuresis is becoming harmful. The combination of hemodynamic and electrolyte derangement is what mandates active fluid and potassium management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate fluid replacement strategy for his ongoing post-obstructive diuresis?
options: Replace 100% of urine output milliliter for milliliter with 0.9% saline
Withhold all IV fluids and allow oral intake only to force the diuresis to stop
Replace roughly 50–75% of urine output with hypotonic fluid (e.g., 0.45% saline) plus potassium, titrated to volume status and labs
Give 5% albumin boluses until urine output falls below 50 mL/h
stages.explanation: The goal is to correct deficits without perpetuating the diuresis, so partial replacement (about half to three-quarters of output) using a hypotonic crystalloid with added potassium, retitrated to clinical and laboratory status, is correct; underreplacing deliberately lets the kidneys downregulate. Full milliliter-for-milliliter replacement feeds the diuresis and risks volume overload and prolongation. Withholding fluids entirely in a patient already orthostatic and azotemic risks hypovolemic acute kidney injury and shock. Albumin is not indicated; he has no oncotic deficit, and chasing an arbitrary output target with colloid is both costly and physiologically inappropriate. Frequent electrolyte checks guide ongoing adjustments as output normalizes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His diuresis settles, electrolytes and creatinine normalize, and he is voiding around the catheter. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Remove the catheter immediately and discharge without medication or follow-up
Start an alpha-blocker, plan a trial of void, and arrange urology follow-up for BPH
Schedule urgent transurethral resection of the prostate the same day
Leave the catheter indefinitely and refer to hospice
stages.explanation: Once stabilized, the standard pathway is to begin an alpha-blocker (e.g., tamsulosin) to relax the bladder neck, attempt a trial of voiding after a few days, and arrange urology follow-up to manage the underlying BPH and assess for definitive therapy if voiding fails. Immediate removal with no medication or follow-up ignores the high likelihood of recurrent retention from untreated obstruction. Same-day TURP is overly aggressive without a failed voiding trial, optimization, and proper evaluation. Indefinite catheterization with hospice referral is grossly inappropriate for reversible benign obstruction in a man with a good prognosis. Patient education on retention warning signs is also key before discharge.

cases.diagnosis: Post-obstructive diuresis following relief of chronic urinary retention due to BPH
pearls: Post-obstructive diuresis follows relief of bilateral or chronic obstruction from retained urea, sodium, and water plus transient tubular dysfunction; output often exceeds 200 mL/h or 3 L/day.

Distinguish physiologic diuresis (self-limited, resolves with euvolemia) from pathologic diuresis (persists with orthostasis, electrolyte wasting, and rising creatinine) that needs active replacement.
Replace only about 50–75% of output with hypotonic fluid plus potassium and monitor weights and electrolytes—full milliliter-for-milliliter replacement perpetuates the diuresis.
Do not rapidly clamp catheters to 'prevent' decompression hematuria; full drainage is safe, and treat the underlying obstruction with an alpha-blocker and a planned voiding trial.

cases.title: Tired, Confused, and a Belly That Won't Empty
cases.chiefComplaint: Fatigue, nausea, and confusion with no urge to urinate despite a swollen lower abdomen.
cases.history: A 73-year-old man is brought in by his daughter for two weeks of progressive fatigue, poor appetite, nausea, and intermittent confusion. He denies pain. On questioning he reports months of weak stream, nocturia five times a night, and constant dribbling of small amounts of urine day and night, which he assumed was normal aging; he denies any sense of bladder fullness or urgency. He has not seen a physician in years. Past medical history is notable for hypertension and presumed BPH, never formally evaluated. He takes only lisinopril 20 mg daily inconsistently and has no known drug allergies. He is a widower living alone, a former pipefitter, drinks one or two beers nightly, and quit smoking a decade ago after 40 pack-years. Family history is unknown. Review of systems is positive for ankle swelling, generalized weakness, and the continuous urinary leakage but negative for fever, flank or suprapubic pain, dysuria, gross hematuria, weight loss, or bone pain. His daughter notes he has seemed 'foggy' and has been sleeping much of the day.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing, mildly somnolent but rousable and oriented to person and place only. No acute distress and notably no facial grimacing. Mucous membranes moist. Cardiac exam regular without murmurs; mild bibasilar crackles; trace bilateral pedal edema. The lower abdomen is markedly distended with a firm, dull, palpable mass extending several centimeters above the umbilicus that is surprisingly nontender to palpation. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Genitourinary exam shows continuous dribbling of urine from the meatus. Digital rectal exam reveals a diffusely enlarged, smooth, nontender prostate without discrete nodules; rectal tone is intact. Asterixis is present. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the painless distended bladder, overflow leakage, and altered mentation, what is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Obtain a CT urogram before any catheter placement
Place a urethral catheter to decompress the bladder while sending renal function and electrolytes
Give intravenous furosemide to treat the lower-abdominal fullness and edema
Start hemodialysis emergently for presumed uremia
stages.explanation: A painless, hugely distended bladder with overflow incontinence and uremic symptoms points to chronic urinary retention causing obstructive nephropathy; the immediate, definitive intervention is bladder decompression with a catheter, which relieves the obstruction and begins reversing the renal failure, while you simultaneously check creatinine and electrolytes. CT urogram delays decompression and is unnecessary to diagnose obvious bladder outlet obstruction. Furosemide cannot drain an obstructed system and will worsen volume status and electrolytes if urine cannot exit. Dialysis may be needed for refractory hyperkalemia or acidosis but is premature before simply relieving the obstruction, which often corrects the metabolic derangements on its own once urine flows.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 134 mEq/L
K 5.9 mEq/L
HCO3 16 mEq/L
BUN 102 mg/dL
Creatinine 7.8 mg/dL (no prior available)
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL
Calcium 8.4 mg/dL
Phosphate 6.1 mg/dL
Venous pH 7.28
Urinalysis: bland, no nitrites, no significant blood
PVR after catheter: 2,100 mL drained
Renal ultrasound: bilateral moderate-to-severe hydronephrosis with cortical thinning; markedly distended bladder; prostate ~90 g

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation best classifies this patient's condition?
options: Low-pressure chronic retention with normal renal function
Acute urinary retention from a urethral stricture
High-pressure chronic retention with bilateral hydronephrosis and obstructive renal failure
Acute kidney injury from rhabdomyolysis
stages.explanation: Painless retention with continuous overflow incontinence, a 2,100 mL bladder, bilateral hydronephrosis with cortical thinning, and a creatinine of 7.8 defines high-pressure chronic retention: sustained elevated intravesical pressure transmits back to the upper tracts, producing hydronephrosis and obstructive nephropathy. Low-pressure chronic retention involves a compliant bladder with preserved kidneys and no hydronephrosis—the opposite of this picture. A urethral stricture usually causes painful acute retention and obstructive voiding rather than this slow painless overflow with massive volumes, and the enlarged prostate points to outlet obstruction. Rhabdomyolysis would show a markedly elevated CK and pigmented urine without bilateral hydronephrosis or a giant bladder, and does not explain the overflow incontinence.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His potassium is 5.9 with a venous pH of 7.28 and ECG shows peaked T waves. After bladder decompression, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Recheck potassium in 12 hours since decompression will fix everything
Give IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization plus insulin/dextrose, and monitor the post-obstructive diuresis
Administer potassium chloride to prevent rebound hypokalemia
Give a large bolus of lactated Ringer's to dilute the potassium
stages.explanation: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes is a medical emergency: stabilize the myocardium immediately with IV calcium, then shift potassium intracellularly with insulin and dextrose, all while anticipating the brisk post-obstructive diuresis that typically follows relief of bilateral obstruction. Waiting 12 hours is dangerous when there are ECG changes, even though decompression will help over time. Giving potassium chloride is contraindicated—he is hyperkalemic now, and any rebound hypokalemia is managed later by careful monitoring. Lactated Ringer's bolus contains potassium and does not reliably lower serum potassium; volume status must instead be guided by the developing diuresis with judicious partial replacement, not an indiscriminate large bolus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Over the next day his creatinine falls toward 3.0 and he produces 6 L of urine in 24 hours. What is the most appropriate ongoing plan?
options: Remove the catheter now that creatinine is improving and discharge home
Maintain catheter drainage, monitor and partially replace fluids/electrolytes for post-obstructive diuresis, and plan urologic follow-up for definitive outlet management
Stop all fluids and restrict him to 1 L oral intake daily
Proceed directly to bilateral nephrostomy tubes
stages.explanation: After relieving high-pressure chronic retention, the bladder must stay decompressed while you manage the expected post-obstructive diuresis with careful monitoring and partial hypotonic fluid/electrolyte replacement, then arrange urology follow-up for definitive treatment of the outlet obstruction (alpha-blocker, eventual TURP, or long-term management). Removing the catheter and discharging risks immediate re-obstruction, recurrent renal failure, and an uncontrolled diuresis at home. Severe fluid restriction during a brisk diuresis invites hypovolemic AKI and shock. Bilateral nephrostomies are unnecessary because urethral catheter drainage has already relieved the obstruction and the hydronephrosis is improving; nephrostomy is reserved for cases where lower-tract drainage cannot relieve the upper tracts.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Once stable, what counseling point about his bladder and kidneys is most accurate?
options: His detrusor will fully recover, so he can stop the catheter immediately after a single normal void
Some renal impairment may be permanent due to cortical thinning, and he may have prolonged retention requiring extended catheterization or intermittent self-catheterization
High-pressure chronic retention never recurs once treated
He should avoid all future surgery on the prostate because his kidneys are too fragile
stages.explanation: Because chronic high-pressure obstruction caused cortical thinning, some loss of renal function is often permanent, and detrusor recovery can be slow, so he may need extended catheter drainage or clean intermittent self-catheterization while outlet management proceeds. Expecting full immediate recovery after one void overestimates a chronically decompensated detrusor and risks recurrent retention. Claiming recurrence is impossible is false—untreated outlet obstruction predisposes to recurrence, which is exactly why definitive therapy is planned. Avoiding all prostate surgery is incorrect; relieving the outlet obstruction (e.g., TURP after optimization) is often precisely what protects the kidneys, and renal impairment is not an absolute contraindication once he is medically optimized.

cases.diagnosis: High-pressure chronic urinary retention with bilateral hydronephrosis and obstructive renal failure due to BPH
pearls: Painless retention with continuous overflow incontinence and uremic symptoms is the classic presentation of high-pressure chronic retention—palpate for a nontender distended bladder.

High-pressure chronic retention transmits elevated bladder pressure to the kidneys, causing bilateral hydronephrosis and obstructive nephropathy; immediate catheter decompression is the key intervention.
Treat life-threatening hyperkalemia (calcium, insulin/dextrose) concurrently, and anticipate a brisk post-obstructive diuresis after relieving bilateral obstruction.
Cortical thinning predicts that some renal impairment may be permanent; keep the bladder drained and arrange definitive outlet treatment to protect remaining function.

cases.title: Flank Pain and Bloody Urine in a Swollen Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: New left flank pain and visibly bloody urine while hospitalized for severe leg and facial swelling.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man admitted three days ago for marked lower-extremity and periorbital edema develops acute left flank pain and grossly bloody urine. He was being worked up for new-onset nephrotic-range proteinuria found after weeks of progressive swelling, frothy urine, and a 5-kg weight gain. The flank pain came on over a few hours, is constant and severe, and radiates toward the groin; he also reports mild left-sided pleuritic chest discomfort and breathlessness this morning. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no chronic medications; during admission he received furosemide 40 mg IV and a statin was started. No known drug allergies. He works in IT, does not smoke, drinks rarely, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for the new flank pain, gross hematuria, dyspnea, and pleuritic chest pain, and negative for fever, dysuria, recent trauma, prior stones, joint pains, rash, or hemoptysis. Inpatient labs had already shown heavy proteinuria and low serum albumin consistent with nephrotic syndrome under investigation.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable and mildly tachypneic. Periorbital puffiness and pitting edema to the thighs with anasarca; mild scrotal edema. Cardiac exam tachycardic and regular without murmurs or rub; jugular venous pressure not elevated. Lungs clear with slightly diminished breath sounds at the left base. Abdomen soft with marked left costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion; no rebound or guarding and no palpable masses. Genitourinary exam unremarkable aside from scrotal edema; testes nontender and normal in lie. There are no flank ecchymoses. Calves are symmetric, soft, and nontender without cords. Skin shows no rash or purpura.

stages.label: ADMISSION LABS
stages.content:
Urine protein:creatinine ratio 9.2 g/g
Serum albumin 1.8 g/dL
Total cholesterol 392 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, large blood, oval fat bodies, no casts of note
Antithrombin III: low
D-dimer: markedly elevated

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this man with nephrotic syndrome who now has acute flank pain and gross hematuria, which complication should be at the top of the differential?
options: Obstructing ureteral calculus
Renal vein thrombosis
Acute pyelonephritis
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome creates a hypercoagulable state—urinary loss of antithrombin III, increased hepatic procoagulant synthesis, and hemoconcentration—so acute flank pain with gross hematuria, especially with concurrent dyspnea and pleuritic pain suggesting pulmonary embolism, is classic for renal vein thrombosis. An obstructing stone can cause flank pain and hematuria but does not explain the hypercoagulable physiology, the low antithrombin, or the concurrent embolic chest symptoms. Pyelonephritis typically brings fever, pyuria, and bacteriuria, which are absent. Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis presents with rising creatinine, red cell casts, and oliguria over days to weeks, not sudden unilateral flank pain with thrombotic features; the clinical gestalt strongly favors a venous thrombotic event.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis (stone protocol)
Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
CT venography or contrast-enhanced CT/MRV of the renal veins (often with CT pulmonary angiography given dyspnea)
Retrograde pyelography
stages.explanation: Cross-sectional venous imaging—CT venography or contrast-enhanced CT/MR venography of the renal veins—is the test of choice to demonstrate thrombus and assess extension into the inferior vena cava, and given his hypoxia and pleuritic pain, simultaneous CT pulmonary angiography evaluates for the associated pulmonary embolism. A non-contrast stone-protocol CT is designed to detect calculi and will not reliably show venous thrombus. A KUB is insensitive for both stones and soft-tissue/vascular pathology. Retrograde pyelography evaluates the collecting system for filling defects and obstruction, not the venous drainage, and is invasive and inappropriate here. Renal function permitting, contrast venous imaging directly visualizes the clot and guides anticoagulation decisions.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Contrast-enhanced CT venography: filling defect in the left renal vein extending toward the IVC; enlarged, edematous left kidney with delayed nephrogram
CT pulmonary angiography: segmental pulmonary emboli in the left lower lobe
Lower-extremity Doppler: no DVT identified
Repeat creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms left renal vein thrombosis with segmental pulmonary emboli. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., heparin bridged to warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant)
Aspirin 81 mg daily alone
Emergent surgical thrombectomy of the renal vein
Ureteral stent placement
stages.explanation: Renal vein thrombosis with a documented pulmonary embolism is treated with full therapeutic anticoagulation—typically heparin bridged to warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant—continued while the nephrotic state persists and usually for at least several months. Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent and is inadequate for an established venous thromboembolism. Surgical or catheter thrombectomy is reserved for rare situations such as acute bilateral thrombosis with renal failure or massive IVC clot, not stable unilateral thrombosis that responds to anticoagulation. A ureteral stent addresses urinary outflow obstruction from stones or strictures and has no role in venous thrombosis. Treating the underlying nephrotic syndrome also reduces the ongoing thrombotic risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which underlying glomerular disease is most classically associated with renal vein thrombosis and should be sought on renal biopsy?
options: Minimal change disease
Membranous nephropathy
IgA nephropathy
Thin basement membrane disease
stages.explanation: Membranous nephropathy carries the strongest association with renal vein thrombosis among nephrotic glomerulopathies, likely reflecting its severe, sustained proteinuria and pronounced hypercoagulability, so renal biopsy to identify it (and guide immunosuppression and anticoagulation duration) is appropriate. Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children and can cause thrombosis but is far less classically linked to renal vein thrombosis. IgA nephropathy typically presents with hematuria and sub-nephrotic proteinuria rather than the heavy nephrotic-range losses driving this complication. Thin basement membrane disease causes isolated microscopic hematuria with a benign course and is not a cause of nephrotic-range proteinuria or thrombosis.

cases.diagnosis: Left renal vein thrombosis complicating nephrotic syndrome (with associated pulmonary embolism)
pearls: Nephrotic syndrome is prothrombotic from urinary loss of antithrombin III and increased procoagulant factors; renal vein thrombosis is a hallmark venous complication.
Suspect renal vein thrombosis with acute flank pain, gross hematuria, and an enlarged kidney in a nephrotic patient—concurrent dyspnea suggests pulmonary embolism.
Membranous nephropathy has the strongest association with renal vein thrombosis; confirm with contrast CT/MR venography of the renal veins.
Treat with therapeutic anticoagulation for as long as the nephrotic state persists; reserve thrombectomy for acute bilateral thrombosis with renal failure or massive clot burden.

cases.title: Sore Testicles a Few Days After a Swollen Jaw
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, swollen testicles that started a few days after his cheeks and jaw became swollen.
cases.history: An 18-year-old college freshman presents with three days of bilateral testicular pain and swelling, worse on the right. About five days before this, he developed painful swelling over both cheeks and angle of the jaw, low-grade fevers, and pain with chewing, which his roommate also recently had. He never received childhood vaccinations because of his family's preferences, and he recalls no prior similar illness. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and used acetaminophen for the facial pain. No known drug allergies. He is sexually active with one female partner, uses condoms inconsistently, and denies prior sexually transmitted infections, urethral discharge, or dysuria. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol on weekends, and uses no drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for malaise, headache, low-grade fever, the recent parotid swelling, and scrotal pain, and negative for urinary symptoms, penile discharge, abdominal pain, recent heavy lifting, scrotal trauma, neck stiffness, or photophobia. He is anxious about whether this could affect his future fertility.
cases.exam: Well-developed young man in mild discomfort. There is residual bilateral parotid swelling and tenderness anterior to and below the ears, obscuring the angle of the mandible. Oropharynx is moist without exudate. Neck is supple with no meningismus. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Genitourinary exam shows both testes enlarged, firm, and tender, the right more than the left, with a normal vertical lie and an intact cremasteric reflex bilaterally; there is reactive scrotal wall erythema and a small reactive hydrocele on the right. Prehn sign is equivocal. No urethral discharge is expressed. Inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the recent parotitis followed by bilateral orchitis in an unvaccinated young man, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis from a sexually transmitted infection
Mumps orchitis
Testicular torsion
Testicular germ cell tumor
stages.explanation: Bilateral orchitis appearing several days after parotitis in an unvaccinated adolescent, with fever and malaise during a likely outbreak, is the textbook presentation of mumps orchitis from the mumps paramyxovirus. Sexually transmitted epididymo-orchitis typically follows urethritis with discharge and dysuria and is usually unilateral, none of which fits the preceding parotitis. Testicular torsion is a sudden, severe, usually unilateral surgical emergency with a high-riding testis and absent cremasteric reflex—here the reflex is intact, lie is normal, and onset followed a viral prodrome. A germ cell tumor presents as a painless firm mass over weeks to months, not acute bilateral painful swelling after parotitis. The parotitis-to-orchitis sequence is the key diagnostic clue.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory test for the underlying infection?
options: Urethral nucleic acid amplification test for gonorrhea and chlamydia only
Mumps IgM serology and/or RT-PCR of a buccal/parotid duct swab
Scrotal ultrasound to diagnose the viral cause
Testicular biopsy
stages.explanation: Mumps is confirmed serologically with mumps-specific IgM (and/or a rise in IgG) or by RT-PCR of a buccal/parotid duct swab, which is also important for public-health reporting and outbreak control. A gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT is reasonable to exclude STI given his sexual history but does not confirm mumps, the leading diagnosis here. Scrotal ultrasound can show orchitis and, importantly, exclude torsion when the diagnosis is uncertain, but it shows nonspecific inflammation and cannot identify the virus. Testicular biopsy is invasive, risks injury, and is not indicated for a clinically classic viral orchitis. Confirming mumps guides counseling, reporting, and reassurance that antibiotics are unnecessary.

stages.label: RESULTS
stages.content:
Mumps IgM: positive
Buccal swab mumps RT-PCR: positive
CBC: WBC 6.4 ×10³/µL with relative lymphocytosis
Urinalysis: no pyuria, no bacteria
Urethral GC/chlamydia NAAT: negative
Scrotal ultrasound: enlarged hypervascular testes bilaterally with small right reactive hydrocele; normal arterial flow, no torsion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for his mumps orchitis?
options: Ceftriaxone plus doxycycline
Supportive care with rest, scrotal support/elevation, ice, NSAIDs, and analgesia
Oral acyclovir
Emergent surgical scrotal exploration
stages.explanation: Mumps orchitis is a self-limited viral illness, so management is supportive: bed rest, scrotal elevation and support, cold packs, NSAIDs, and adequate analgesia, with symptoms typically improving over one to two weeks. Ceftriaxone plus doxycycline treats bacterial/STI epididymo-orchitis and is unnecessary here given negative NAAT and a confirmed viral cause. Acyclovir targets herpesviruses and has no activity against the mumps paramyxovirus. Emergent exploration is the treatment for testicular torsion, which ultrasound has excluded with normal arterial flow. Counseling about the benign but uncomfortable course, expected resolution, and fertility considerations is an essential part of management.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He asks about long-term consequences. What is the most accurate counseling point?
options: Bilateral mumps orchitis guarantees permanent sterility
Mumps orchitis can cause testicular atrophy and subfertility, but complete sterility is uncommon; the MMR vaccine prevents mumps and he and close contacts should be vaccinated
Mumps orchitis substantially increases his risk of testicular cancer
There are no fertility implications whatsoever
stages.explanation: Mumps orchitis, especially when bilateral, can lead to some degree of testicular atrophy and impaired spermatogenesis, but outright sterility is uncommon; he should also be counseled that the MMR vaccine prevents mumps and that vaccination of him (after recovery) and susceptible contacts limits spread. Guaranteed sterility overstates the risk and is incorrect. A substantially increased testicular cancer risk is not an established consequence of mumps orchitis. Claiming no fertility implications at all is falsely reassuring given the real, if modest, risk of atrophy and subfertility. Honest, balanced counseling plus vaccine promotion is the appropriate approach.

cases.diagnosis: Mumps orchitis following mumps parotitis in an unvaccinated adolescent
pearls: Orchitis appearing 4–8 days after parotitis in an unvaccinated/post-pubertal male is classic mumps orchitis; it is bilateral in up to a third of cases.
Confirm with mumps IgM serology or buccal-swab RT-PCR and report cases; use scrotal ultrasound mainly to exclude torsion when the diagnosis is uncertain.
Treatment is supportive (rest, scrotal elevation, NSAIDs, ice)—antibiotics and antivirals are not indicated for the paramyxovirus.
Sequelae include testicular atrophy and subfertility, though sterility is uncommon; MMR vaccination prevents mumps and is the key public-health measure.

cases.title: A Painless Lump Above the Testicle
cases.chiefComplaint: A small, painless lump near the top of the left testicle noticed in the shower.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents after noticing a small, painless lump near the top of his left testicle while showering two weeks ago. It has not changed noticeably in size, does not hurt, and causes no urinary symptoms. He has no history of testicular trauma, infection, or prior masses. He denies fevers, weight loss, back pain, gynecomastia, or any sense of heaviness. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He is married, monogamous, has two children, works as an electrician, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke or use drugs. Family history is negative for testicular cancer. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no penile discharge, dysuria, hematuria, or constitutional symptoms. He is worried this could be cancer, which prompted him to come in promptly. He recalls a clinician once mentioning a small 'cyst' on a prior unrelated exam but is unsure of the details and had no follow-up imaging at that time.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and comfortable. Abdomen soft and nontender without masses or organomegaly; no inguinal lymphadenopathy or hernia. Genitourinary exam reveals a normal penis and bilaterally descended testes of normal size and consistency. At the superior pole of the left testis, within the region of the epididymal head, there is a smooth, soft, round, mobile, nontender mass approximately 1 cm in diameter that is clearly separate from and superior to the testis itself. The mass transilluminates brightly. The underlying testis is normal and nontender with no discrete intratesticular mass palpable. The right hemiscrotum is normal. There is no varicocele and no overlying skin change.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a soft, transilluminating, mobile mass at the epididymal head that is separate from a normal testis, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Testicular germ cell tumor
Spermatocele
Varicocele
Indirect inguinal hernia
stages.explanation: A spermatocele is a benign cystic collection of fluid and sperm arising from the head of the epididymis; the classic exam shows a smooth, soft, mobile, transilluminating mass that is distinctly separate from and superior to the testis, exactly as described. A germ cell tumor is a firm, solid intratesticular mass that does not transilluminate and is part of the testis, not separate from it. A varicocele feels like a 'bag of worms,' increases with Valsalva, often disappears when supine, and does not transilluminate. An indirect inguinal hernia presents as a groin/scrotal bulge that may extend to the scrotum, often reducible and with a cough impulse, not a discrete cystic lesion confined to the epididymal head.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Scrotal ultrasound
Serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis
Immediate radical inguinal orchiectomy
Empiric antibiotics and reassess in 6 weeks
stages.explanation: Scrotal ultrasound is the appropriate confirmatory study; it reliably shows a well-defined anechoic cyst at the epididymal head separate from a normal testis, distinguishing a benign spermatocele from a solid intratesticular tumor. Tumor markers and staging CT are appropriate only when ultrasound reveals a suspicious solid intratesticular mass, which is not the clinical picture here. Radical orchiectomy is reserved for suspected testicular malignancy and would be grossly inappropriate for a benign cyst. Empiric antibiotics treat infectious epididymitis, which presents with pain, tenderness, and inflammatory signs absent in this painless, well-defined mass. Ultrasound both confirms the benign nature and provides reassurance and a baseline.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Scrotal ultrasound: well-circumscribed anechoic cystic lesion, 1.1 cm, at the head of the left epididymis with posterior acoustic enhancement
No solid intratesticular mass; both testes normal in echotexture and vascularity
No varicocele; no hydrocele; normal Doppler flow

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The ultrasound confirms a simple epididymal cyst (spermatocele). The patient is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance and observation, with surgery only if it becomes symptomatic
Spermatocelectomy now to prevent malignant transformation
Sclerotherapy of the cyst to eliminate cancer risk
Start an alpha-blocker and repeat ultrasound in 3 months
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic spermatoceles are benign and require no intervention; reassurance and observation are appropriate, reserving surgery (spermatocelectomy) for cysts that become large, painful, or bothersome. Operating now to prevent malignant transformation is unnecessary because spermatoceles do not undergo malignant change, and surgery carries a risk of epididymal injury and possible effects on fertility. Sclerotherapy is not standard, is associated with recurrence and inflammation, and is certainly not indicated for an unfounded cancer concern. An alpha-blocker treats bladder outlet symptoms and has no role for an epididymal cyst, and routine short-interval re-imaging is unnecessary for a clearly benign, stable lesion. Counseling on self-exam and return precautions completes the visit.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which statement most accurately addresses his concern about future risk and fertility?
options: A spermatocele is premalignant and must be removed within a year
A spermatocele is benign and does not become cancerous; if it later needs removal, surgery carries a small risk of affecting epididymal function on that side
A spermatocele always causes infertility
A spermatocele will inevitably grow and rupture without treatment
stages.explanation: Spermatoceles are entirely benign and carry no malignant potential, so no removal timeline is needed; if a symptomatic one is later excised, the patient should know spermatocelectomy can occasionally disrupt epididymal patency on that side, a relevant point for men concerned about fertility. Calling it premalignant requiring removal within a year is factually wrong and would lead to unnecessary surgery. Claiming it always causes infertility is incorrect—an isolated spermatocele typically does not impair fertility. Asserting it will inevitably grow and rupture overstates the natural history; many remain small and stable indefinitely. Accurate, reassuring counseling prevents unnecessary anxiety and procedures.

cases.diagnosis: Spermatocele (epididymal cyst) of the left epididymal head
pearls: A spermatocele is a benign sperm-containing cyst at the epididymal head: soft, mobile, transilluminating, and—crucially—separate from and superior to the testis.
Scrotal ultrasound confirms a simple anechoic cyst and excludes a solid intratesticular tumor, which is the must-not-miss diagnosis for any scrotal mass.
Asymptomatic spermatoceles need only reassurance and observation; spermatoceles have no malignant potential.
Reserve spermatocelectomy for symptomatic lesions and counsel that surgery carries a small risk to ipsilateral epididymal function and fertility.

cases.title: A Blue Spot and a Sore Spot at the Top of the Testicle
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of gradually worsening pain at the top of the right testicle with a small blue spot on the scrotum.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for about 24 hours of right scrotal pain that began gradually, without trauma, and has slowly worsened. The pain is localized to the upper part of the testicle, and his mother noticed a small bluish discoloration through the thin scrotal skin. He has been able to walk and even attended part of school, and the pain is moderate rather than excruciating. He denies abdominal pain, and there has been no vomiting. Past medical history is unremarkable; he is fully immunized and developmentally normal. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He lives with both parents, is not sexually active, and has no urinary symptoms, fever, or recent illness. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no dysuria, frequency, penile discharge, or prior scrotal problems. His mother is worried because she read online that testicular pain can mean an emergency that requires surgery within hours.
cases.exam: Well-appearing boy in mild discomfort, walking without a markedly antalgic gait. Abdomen soft and nontender. Genitourinary exam shows a normally descended right testis in a normal vertical lie with a normal-sized, non-edematous scrotum and intact, brisk cremasteric reflex bilaterally. There is focal, well-localized tenderness at the superior pole of the right testis, where a small, firm, tender nodule is palpable; a 3- to 4-mm bluish discoloration is visible through the scrotal skin overlying this point (a 'blue dot'). The remainder of the testis and the epididymis are nontender, and the testis itself is not high-riding. The left hemiscrotum is normal. No inguinal hernia or lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In any boy with acute scrotal pain, which diagnosis must be excluded first because it is a time-critical surgical emergency?
options: Torsion of the appendix testis
Testicular (spermatic cord) torsion
Epididymitis
Inguinal hernia
stages.explanation: Spermatic cord (testicular) torsion is the must-not-miss diagnosis in acute scrotum because testicular salvage falls rapidly after about six hours of ischemia, so it must be excluded first in every case. Torsion of the appendix testis is a common and benign cause of acute scrotal pain but is not the immediate threat to the gonad. Epididymitis is also non-emergent and usually presents with a more gradual onset, dysuria, or pyuria. An inguinal hernia can cause groin/scrotal swelling but is not the priority unless incarcerated. Even when the picture suggests a benign cause, the clinician's first job is to confidently rule out cord torsion, by exam and ultrasound if needed, before settling on an alternative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given gradual onset, a preserved cremasteric reflex, focal upper-pole tenderness, and a 'blue dot' sign, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Testicular torsion
Torsion of the appendix testis
Acute bacterial epididymitis
Idiopathic scrotal edema
stages.explanation: Torsion of the appendix testis classically presents in prepubertal boys with gradual-onset pain localized to the upper pole, an intact cremasteric reflex, and the pathognomonic 'blue dot' sign—the infarcted appendage seen through thin scrotal skin—exactly as here. Testicular torsion typically causes sudden severe pain, a high-riding testis with horizontal lie, an absent cremasteric reflex, and diffuse tenderness, which are not present. Bacterial epididymitis is uncommon in this age group, usually involves dysuria/pyuria and posterior tenderness, and lacks the blue dot. Idiopathic scrotal edema causes painless or minimally tender scrotal wall swelling and erythema without a focal upper-pole nodule. The focal sign plus preserved reflex strongly favor the torsed appendage.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: The diagnosis is clinically suspected but you want to be sure the testis is not torsed. What is the best confirmatory study?
options: Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound
Non-contrast CT of the pelvis
Urethral swab for gonorrhea/chlamydia
Radionuclide testicular scan as first-line
stages.explanation: Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound is the test of choice: it demonstrates preserved testicular blood flow (excluding torsion) and may show a small, enlarged, hypervascular or avascular appendage at the upper pole with normal underlying testicular perfusion, confirming torsion of the appendix testis. A pelvic CT involves radiation, is not the imaging modality for the acute scrotum, and would not assess testicular flow well. A urethral STI swab is inappropriate in a non-sexually-active prepubertal child and does not address the torsion question. A radionuclide scan can assess perfusion but is slower, less available, and not first-line compared with rapid bedside-accessible Doppler ultrasound. Ultrasound efficiently confirms a benign appendiceal cause while ruling out the emergency.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound: normal symmetric arterial and venous flow to both testes
Right testis normal in size and echotexture
Small avascular hyperechoic nodule adjacent to the superior pole of the right testis with surrounding hyperemia (consistent with a torsed appendix testis)
Small reactive right hydrocele
No evidence of testicular torsion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms torsion of the appendix testis with normal testicular flow. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Emergent scrotal exploration and orchiopexy
Conservative management with rest, scrotal support, NSAIDs, and reassurance
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and admission
Manual detorsion at the bedside
stages.explanation: Torsion of the appendix testis is self-limited: the infarcted appendage necroses and the pain resolves over one to two weeks, so management is conservative with rest, scrotal support, NSAIDs, and reassurance once testicular torsion is excluded. Emergent exploration with orchiopexy is the treatment for spermatic cord torsion, not the benign appendiceal torsion confirmed here. IV antibiotics and admission are for serious bacterial infection, which this is not. Manual detorsion is a temporizing maneuver for testicular torsion and is irrelevant to a torsed appendage. Surgical excision of the appendage is reserved for the rare patient with severe or persistent pain that fails conservative care, but most resolve without an operation.

cases.diagnosis: Torsion of the appendix testis (with a positive blue dot sign)
pearls: The 'blue dot' sign—an infarcted appendage visible through thin scrotal skin at the upper pole—is pathognomonic for torsion of the appendix testis.
Gradual onset, focal upper-pole tenderness, and a preserved cremasteric reflex favor appendiceal torsion over testicular torsion, but always exclude cord torsion first.
Color Doppler ultrasound confirms normal testicular flow and an avascular paratesticular nodule, distinguishing the benign condition from the emergency.
Treatment is conservative (rest, scrotal support, NSAIDs); the appendage necroses and pain resolves in 1–2 weeks, with excision reserved for refractory cases.

cases.title: A Groin Bulge That Comes and Goes
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents notice an intermittent bulge in the groin and scrotum, mostly when he cries.
cases.history: A 1-year-old boy is brought to clinic because his parents have noticed a soft bulge in the right groin that sometimes extends into the scrotum, most prominent when he cries, strains during bowel movements, or stands. The bulge appears and disappears, and when it is present it does not seem to bother him; it flattens when he is calm or lying down. There has been no vomiting, no abdominal distension, no fevers, and no constipation, and he is feeding and growing well. He was born at 34 weeks' gestation and spent two weeks in the special care nursery. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable, immunizations are up to date, and he takes no medications. No known drug allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for irritability, poor feeding, bilious emesis, abdominal pain, or skin color change over the bulge. The parents are anxious because the bulge was visible this morning and they want to know whether it needs surgery and what danger signs to watch for.
cases.exam: Active, well-appearing, well-nourished toddler in no distress. Abdomen is soft, nondistended, and nontender with normal bowel sounds and no palpable masses. On standing and with a Valsalva maneuver elicited by crying, a soft, nontender, reducible bulge appears at the right groin extending toward the upper scrotum; it reduces easily with gentle pressure and a palpable thickening of the cord ('silk glove' sign) is appreciated. The overlying skin is normal in color without erythema or tenderness. Both testes are descended and normal. The left groin is normal at rest, though the contralateral side cannot be fully excluded. There is no abdominal distension or peritoneal sign.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this intermittent, easily reducible groin/scrotal bulge in a former premature infant?
options: Direct inguinal hernia
Indirect inguinal hernia from a patent processus vaginalis
Communicating hydrocele only
Femoral hernia
stages.explanation: In infants and children, an inguinal hernia that extends toward the scrotum is almost always an indirect hernia caused by a patent processus vaginalis, and prematurity markedly increases the risk—matching this former 34-weeker. A direct inguinal hernia results from acquired weakness of the abdominal wall and is rare in children. A communicating hydrocele also stems from a patent processus but transmits fluid (transilluminating, soft) rather than bowel; the palpable cord thickening and reducible bulge here suggest a hernia, and the two can coexist. A femoral hernia is uncommon in children, lies below the inguinal ligament, and would not produce a scrotal bulge. The developmental patent processus is the key mechanism.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: The bulge has reduced and is not currently visible, but the history is classic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis to confirm the hernia
Diagnose clinically (supported by the silk glove sign/history) and refer for elective surgical repair
Reassure the parents it will resolve on its own and no follow-up is needed
Prescribe a truss (external support garment) and observe
stages.explanation: Pediatric inguinal hernia is a clinical diagnosis; a convincing history of an intermittent reducible groin/scrotal bulge plus the silk glove sign is sufficient, and because childhood inguinal hernias do not resolve spontaneously and carry an incarceration risk, the standard is referral for elective surgical repair (high ligation of the patent processus). A CT scan exposes a child to radiation and is unnecessary; ultrasound, not CT, would be the imaging adjunct if needed. Reassuring that it will resolve without follow-up is wrong—unlike umbilical hernias, inguinal hernias do not close on their own. A truss is an obsolete, ineffective approach that does not address the patent processus and risks complications.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Surgical clinic ultrasound (performed for diagnostic clarity): patent processus vaginalis on the right with bowel loops entering the inguinal canal on Valsalva; reduces with rest
No bowel wall edema or free fluid; normal testicular flow bilaterally
Left side: small patent processus noted, no herniation at rest
Labs: unremarkable; child thriving on growth chart

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The hernia is reducible and the child is asymptomatic. Regarding timing and approach to repair, which statement is most appropriate?
options: Schedule elective repair promptly because childhood inguinal hernias carry a meaningful risk of incarceration, particularly in infants
Delay surgery until after puberty to allow the canal to mature
Repair only if the hernia becomes painful or irreducible
Recommend lifelong observation without surgery
stages.explanation: Because pediatric inguinal hernias do not close spontaneously and the risk of incarceration is highest in the first year of life, elective repair should be scheduled relatively promptly after diagnosis to avoid emergency surgery and complications such as bowel or testicular ischemia. Delaying until after puberty leaves the child exposed to incarceration risk for years and is not standard. Waiting until it becomes painful or irreducible means waiting for incarceration—precisely the dangerous outcome elective repair aims to prevent. Lifelong observation ignores the natural history; these hernias persist and threaten complications. Many surgeons also examine or address the contralateral side given the noted patent processus, depending on findings and practice.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What incarceration warning signs should you tell the parents to return immediately for?
options: A bulge that flattens completely when he lies down
A firm, tender, non-reducible bulge with redness, persistent crying, vomiting, or refusal to feed
The bulge appearing only when he cries
Mild intermittent fussiness that resolves quickly
stages.explanation: Parents must seek emergency care if the bulge becomes firm, tender, and non-reducible, especially with overlying redness, inconsolable crying, vomiting (potentially bilious), abdominal distension, or feeding refusal—these signal incarceration and possible strangulation requiring urgent intervention. A bulge that flattens when supine describes a normally reducible hernia, which is not an emergency. Appearance only with crying/straining is the expected behavior of a reducible hernia and not alarming by itself. Brief, self-resolving fussiness is nonspecific and not a danger sign. Clear teaching on these red flags is essential while awaiting elective repair so that incarceration is recognized and treated promptly.

cases.diagnosis: Indirect inguinal hernia from a patent processus vaginalis
pearls: Pediatric inguinal hernias are almost always indirect, arising from a patent processus vaginalis; prematurity is a major risk factor.
Diagnosis is clinical—an intermittent reducible groin/scrotal bulge plus the 'silk glove' sign over the cord—imaging is rarely required.
Childhood inguinal hernias do not resolve spontaneously and warrant elective surgical repair (high ligation), scheduled promptly given the incarceration risk in infancy.
Teach incarceration red flags: a firm, tender, non-reducible bulge with redness, vomiting, distension, or feeding refusal warrants emergency evaluation.

cases.title: An Unexpected Kidney Finding on a Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after a CT for abdominal pain showed a complicated cyst on the right kidney.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman is referred to urology after a CT performed for vague right upper-quadrant discomfort incidentally revealed a complex right renal cystic lesion. She has no flank pain, no gross hematuria, no weight loss, and no fevers. She feels well overall. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily; she has no known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, drinks wine occasionally, and works as a schoolteacher. There is no family history of renal cancer or hereditary cystic kidney disease. She had a normal screening colonoscopy two years ago. Review of systems is negative for urinary symptoms, hematuria, abdominal pain other than the mild RUQ discomfort that has since resolved, and constitutional symptoms. She is understandably anxious after being told the lesion is 'not a simple cyst' and wants to understand whether she needs surgery or can be monitored. She brought a disc with her CT images for review and has normal recent kidney function on outside labs.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without palpable masses or organomegaly; there is no costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. There is no peripheral edema. Genitourinary and pelvic complaints are absent. No lymphadenopathy is palpable. Skin shows no stigmata of hereditary syndromes (no facial angiofibromas, no café-au-lait macules). The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable, and she appears comfortable and well-perfused with no signs of systemic illness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to characterize an indeterminate complex renal cyst for risk stratification?
options: Renal ultrasound alone
Dedicated multiphase (pre- and post-contrast) CT or MRI of the kidneys with measurement of enhancement
Non-contrast CT only
FDG-PET scan
stages.explanation: Complex renal cysts are stratified with dedicated multiphase imaging—CT or MRI obtained before and after intravenous contrast—because the presence and degree of measurable enhancement of septa, walls, or nodules is the key determinant of malignant potential and drives the Bosniak classification. Ultrasound alone lacks the contrast quantification needed and is operator-dependent for subtle features, though it can complement. Non-contrast CT only cannot assess enhancement and therefore cannot reliably categorize the lesion. FDG-PET is not standard for characterizing renal cysts; many renal cell carcinomas are not avidly FDG-avid, and it does not provide the morphologic detail required. Proper pre- and post-contrast imaging is the foundation of Bosniak categorization.

stages.label: IMAGING REVIEW
stages.content:
Multiphase renal CT: 3.4 cm right renal cystic lesion
Multiple thickened, irregular enhancing septa and a slightly thickened enhancing wall
No measurable enhancing soft-tissue nodule
A few coarse calcifications within septa
Measured enhancement of septa: ~25 HU increase after contrast
Contralateral kidney normal; no lymphadenopathy; eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73 m²

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on multiple thickened, irregular, measurably enhancing septa without an enhancing nodule, which Bosniak category best fits this lesion?
options: Bosniak II
Bosniak IIF
Bosniak III
Bosniak IV
stages.explanation: Bosniak III lesions are indeterminate cystic masses with thickened, irregular walls or septa that show measurable enhancement, carrying a substantial malignancy risk (roughly 50%)—precisely this lesion with multiple thickened, irregular, enhancing septa. Bosniak II describes benign minimally complex cysts with a few hairline-thin septa or fine calcification and no measurable enhancement. Bosniak IIF applies to minimally complex cysts with some smooth thickening or more numerous hairline septa requiring follow-up but without the irregular, clearly enhancing features seen here. Bosniak IV requires a distinct enhancing soft-tissue nodular component, which is absent in this case. The combination of irregular enhancing septa without a discrete enhancing nodule places it firmly in category III.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a Bosniak III renal lesion in this otherwise healthy 58-year-old with normal renal function, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Routine surveillance imaging in 12 months only
Surgical resection, typically nephron-sparing partial nephrectomy, given the significant malignancy risk
Percutaneous sclerotherapy of the cyst
Empiric antibiotics for a presumed infected cyst
stages.explanation: Bosniak III lesions carry a high enough malignancy risk that intervention is generally recommended, and in a healthy patient with normal renal function and a 3.4 cm lesion, nephron-sparing partial nephrectomy is the preferred approach to remove the lesion while preserving kidney function (active surveillance is reserved for select patients with competing risks). Surveillance alone at 12 months underestimates the roughly 50% malignancy risk of a category III lesion. Sclerotherapy is a treatment for benign simple cysts and is inappropriate for a potentially malignant complex lesion. Empiric antibiotics treat an infected cyst, but there is no fever, leukocytosis, or clinical infection here. Definitive surgical excision provides both diagnosis and treatment.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If instead the multiphase imaging had shown only a few hairline-thin septa with minimal smooth wall thickening and no measurable enhancement (a Bosniak IIF lesion), what would be the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate partial nephrectomy
Active surveillance with follow-up imaging (e.g., at 6 months, then periodically) to detect any progression
Percutaneous sclerotherapy
No further imaging ever, as it is definitively benign
stages.explanation: Bosniak IIF lesions are 'follow-up' lesions—mostly benign but with a small malignant potential—so the correct approach is active surveillance with serial imaging (commonly around 6 months, then at intervals) to catch any morphologic progression that would prompt reclassification and intervention. Immediate partial nephrectomy overtreats a lesion that is usually benign and lacks measurable enhancement. Sclerotherapy is a procedure for symptomatic simple cysts, not for risk-stratified complex lesions. Declaring it definitively benign with no further imaging ignores the very reason the 'F' (follow-up) designation exists: a minority of IIF lesions progress and turn out to be malignant, which surveillance is designed to detect.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks whether a needle biopsy could spare her surgery. Which statement is most accurate regarding biopsy of complex cystic renal lesions?
options: Percutaneous biopsy is the definitive way to exclude cancer in cystic lesions and should replace surgery
Biopsy of cystic lesions has substantial sampling limitations and a meaningful false-negative rate, so a negative result does not reliably exclude malignancy in a Bosniak III/IV lesion
Biopsy is contraindicated because it always causes tumor rupture and dissemination
Biopsy is unnecessary because all Bosniak III lesions are benign
stages.explanation: Biopsy of cystic renal masses is limited by sampling error—malignant cells may be sparse within septa—so a negative biopsy cannot reliably exclude cancer in a Bosniak III or IV lesion, which is why surgical excision is generally favored for these categories. Claiming biopsy definitively excludes cancer and should replace surgery is wrong given those sampling limitations. Asserting biopsy always causes tumor seeding overstates the risk; needle-tract seeding is very rare, and biopsy has selective uses (e.g., suspected lymphoma, metastasis, or to guide ablation), but it is not relied upon to clear a complex cyst. Stating all Bosniak III lesions are benign is false—about half are malignant, which is the whole reason intervention is recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Bosniak III complex right renal cyst (managed with nephron-sparing partial nephrectomy)
pearls: Bosniak classification of renal cysts hinges on measurable contrast enhancement; characterize indeterminate cysts with dedicated pre- and post-contrast CT or MRI.

Bosniak categories: I (benign simple), II (minimally complex, benign), IIF (follow-up needed), III (irregular enhancing walls/septa, ~50% malignant), IV (enhancing soft-tissue nodule, mostly malignant).
Bosniak III and IV lesions generally warrant surgical resection—nephron-sparing partial nephrectomy when feasible—rather than surveillance in fit patients.
Negative percutaneous biopsy does not exclude malignancy in complex cystic lesions due to sampling error, so it rarely substitutes for excision in category III/IV cysts.

cases.title: Recurrent Kidney Stones and Blood in the Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Several episodes of kidney stones and intermittent blood in the urine over the past few years.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents to evaluate recurrent kidney stones; she has passed at least four small stones over five years and has noted intermittent painless microscopic and occasional gross hematuria between stone episodes. She has also had two urinary tract infections in the past year. She drinks adequate fluids and follows a typical diet. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable, with no gout, inflammatory bowel disease, or prior urologic surgery. She takes only a daily multivitamin and ibuprofen as needed for stone pain; she has no known drug allergies. She works as an accountant, does not smoke, drinks coffee daily, and exercises regularly. Family history is notable for kidney stones in her father. Review of systems is positive for the recurrent flank pain episodes, intermittent hematuria, and the recent UTIs, and negative for fevers currently, dysuria today, bone pain, abdominal pain, or constitutional symptoms. A prior outside renal ultrasound mentioned 'echogenic medullary pyramids,' and she brings records showing several small calcifications in both kidneys. She wants to understand why she keeps forming stones.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft and nontender without masses; no costovertebral angle tenderness today. There is no suprapubic tenderness. No peripheral edema. Skin shows no tophi or signs of systemic disease. Genitourinary exam unremarkable. The remainder of the general examination is normal. She is comfortable, well-hydrated in appearance, and without signs of acute infection or obstruction at this visit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given recurrent stones, hematuria, recurrent UTIs, and bilateral medullary calcifications, which imaging best characterizes the suspected underlying renal abnormality?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB) alone
CT urography (or IV urography), which can show ectatic collecting ducts and a 'paintbrush'/'bouquet of flowers' pattern in the papillae
Voiding cystourethrogram
DMSA renal cortical scan
stages.explanation: CT urography (or classic IV urography) best demonstrates the dilated, ectatic medullary collecting ducts that fill with contrast to produce the characteristic 'paintbrush' or 'bouquet of flowers' appearance and the medullary nephrocalcinosis of medullary sponge kidney. A KUB alone may show nephrocalcinosis but cannot reveal the ectatic ducts that define the condition. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates vesicoureteral reflux and the lower tract, not the medullary collecting ducts. A DMSA scan assesses cortical function and scarring, which is not the issue here. Urographic phase imaging is the key to visualizing the papillary tubular ectasia underlying her recurrent stones and hematuria.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
CT urography: bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis with dilated/ectatic papillary collecting ducts and contrast pooling producing a 'paintbrush' appearance in multiple papillae
Multiple small calculi within ectatic ducts
Urinalysis: pH 6.2, 2+ blood, no nitrites today
24-hour urine: elevated calcium, low citrate; normal oxalate
Serum calcium 9.6 mg/dL; PTH normal; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Urine culture: no growth today

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the imaging and clinical picture?
options: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease
Medullary sponge kidney
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Renal tubular acidosis type 1 alone
stages.explanation: Medullary sponge kidney is a developmental disorder of dilated medullary collecting ducts that classically causes the 'paintbrush'/'bouquet' urographic pattern, medullary nephrocalcinosis, recurrent calcium stones, hematuria, and recurrent UTIs—matching this patient with normal renal function and normal PTH. ADPKD produces numerous large cortical and medullary cysts that enlarge the kidneys, not ectatic papillary ducts, and would show different imaging. Primary hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcemia with elevated PTH, both absent here. Distal (type 1) renal tubular acidosis alone causes nephrocalcinosis but with a hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis and inappropriately alkaline urine; it can coexist with MSK, but it does not by itself produce the ectatic collecting-duct anatomy that defines this picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her 24-hour urine shows hypercalciuria and hypocitraturia. What is the most appropriate medical management to reduce stone recurrence?
options: Severe dietary calcium restriction
High fluid intake plus potassium citrate, with a thiazide diuretic to reduce hypercalciuria
Allopurinol as first-line therapy
Long-term prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: For recurrent calcium stones with hypercalciuria and hypocitraturia, the cornerstone is high fluid intake to dilute the urine, potassium citrate to correct hypocitraturia and raise urinary citrate (an inhibitor of stone formation), and a thiazide diuretic to reduce urinary calcium excretion. Severe calcium restriction is counterproductive—it can increase oxalate absorption and stone risk, so normal dietary calcium with reduced sodium is preferred. Allopurinol targets uric acid/hyperuricosuria and is not first-line for calcium stones with this metabolic profile. Long-term prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated for stone prevention and risk resistance; UTIs should be treated when they occur. Addressing the specific metabolic abnormalities directly lowers her recurrence risk.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks about her long-term prognosis. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Medullary sponge kidney usually progresses to end-stage renal disease
Medullary sponge kidney is generally benign with a good prognosis, though recurrent stones, hematuria, and UTIs require ongoing prevention and monitoring
It is a premalignant condition requiring nephrectomy
It will resolve completely with the medications prescribed
stages.explanation: Medullary sponge kidney generally carries a good prognosis and rarely progresses to renal failure; the main issues are recurrent calcium stones, hematuria, and urinary infections, which are managed with hydration, metabolic stone prevention, and prompt treatment of infections. Saying it usually progresses to ESRD is inaccurate—renal function is typically preserved. Calling it premalignant requiring nephrectomy is wrong; it is a benign developmental anomaly, not a cancer precursor. Claiming medications will make it resolve completely misrepresents the condition—the anatomic abnormality persists, and therapy reduces complications rather than curing the malformation. Realistic counseling emphasizes prevention and surveillance with reassurance about overall prognosis.

cases.diagnosis: Medullary sponge kidney with recurrent calcium nephrolithiasis
pearls: Medullary sponge kidney is dilation of the medullary collecting ducts producing a 'paintbrush'/'bouquet of flowers' pattern on urography and medullary nephrocalcinosis.
It classically presents with recurrent calcium stones, hematuria, and recurrent UTIs, often with hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia, and sometimes coexisting distal RTA.
Manage with high fluid intake, potassium citrate for hypocitraturia, and a thiazide for hypercalciuria; avoid severe calcium restriction.
Prognosis is generally good with preserved renal function—the focus is stone prevention, treating infections, and monitoring rather than cure.

cases.title: An Unusual Kidney Shape Found Incidentally
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after an ultrasound for abdominal discomfort showed his kidneys joined together at the bottom.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man is referred after an abdominal ultrasound, ordered for vague periumbilical discomfort, suggested his kidneys were fused at their lower poles. He feels generally well and has no urinary symptoms. He has had one prior episode of a kidney stone two years ago that he passed spontaneously, and one urinary tract infection. He denies flank pain currently, gross hematuria, fevers, or weight loss. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He is a software developer, exercises regularly, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is noncontributory, with no known congenital renal anomalies. Review of systems is negative apart from the resolved periumbilical discomfort and his stone/UTI history. He is curious whether this kidney shape will cause problems and whether he needs any restrictions or ongoing monitoring. He has no symptoms suggestive of the syndromic associations and reports normal growth and development.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young man in no distress. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. Abdomen soft, nontender, with no palpable mass, although deep palpation near the midline is mildly uncomfortable; no organomegaly. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Genitourinary exam normal with bilaterally descended testes and no masses. No peripheral edema. There are no dysmorphic features and no signs of an associated syndrome. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. He appears well, with no signs of obstruction, infection, or systemic illness at this visit.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An ultrasound shows the kidneys fused at their lower poles across the midline. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Crossed fused renal ectopia
Horseshoe kidney
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease
Bilateral duplicated collecting systems
stages.explanation: A horseshoe kidney is the fusion of the two kidneys—almost always at their lower poles—by an isthmus crossing the midline, the most common renal fusion anomaly, exactly fitting the imaging. Crossed fused renal ectopia involves both kidneys lying on the same side with one crossing the midline, a different configuration. ADPKD produces enlarged kidneys studded with numerous cysts, not lower-pole fusion, and typically has a family history. Bilateral duplicated collecting systems describe two ureters/collecting systems per kidney without fusion across the midline. The midline lower-pole fusion is the defining feature of the horseshoe kidney.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: He has a history of a stone and a UTI. Which complication is the horseshoe kidney most classically predisposed to, and worth evaluating for?
options: Renal cell carcinoma in the isthmus
Ureteropelvic junction obstruction with resultant stasis predisposing to stones and infection
Renal artery aneurysm
Spontaneous renal rupture
stages.explanation: The classic problems with a horseshoe kidney stem from abnormal anatomy: high ureteral insertion and the isthmus contribute to ureteropelvic junction obstruction and urinary stasis, which predispose to stones and infections—directly relevant to this patient's history. Renal cell carcinoma in the isthmus is not the typical concern; horseshoe kidneys do carry a modestly increased association with Wilms tumor in children and possibly certain tumors, but obstruction/stones/infection are the dominant clinical issues. A renal artery aneurysm is not a characteristic complication. Spontaneous renal rupture is not typical, though the lower-lying isthmus is somewhat more exposed to trauma. Evaluating for UPJ obstruction and stasis-related complications is most pertinent.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT urography: horseshoe kidney with lower-pole fusion via a parenchymal isthmus crossing anterior to the aorta, below the inferior mesenteric artery origin
High ureteral insertion bilaterally with mild right pelvicalyceal fullness suggestive of mild UPJ narrowing
A single 4 mm nonobstructing calculus in the right lower pole
Normal renal function (eGFR >90); no hydronephrosis requiring intervention
Multiple accessory renal arteries noted

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is asymptomatic with a small nonobstructing stone and only mild UPJ narrowing. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prophylactic nephrectomy of the affected moiety
Conservative management: hydration, stone-prevention counseling, treat infections promptly, and monitor; intervene only if obstruction or recurrent complications develop
Immediate pyeloplasty regardless of symptoms
Lifelong daily antibiotic prophylaxis
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic horseshoe kidney with a small nonobstructing stone and only mild UPJ narrowing is managed conservatively—encourage good hydration, provide stone-prevention advice, treat infections when they arise, and monitor for obstruction or recurrent problems—reserving surgery for clinically significant obstruction or refractory complications. Prophylactic nephrectomy is unnecessary and harmful for functioning renal tissue. Immediate pyeloplasty regardless of symptoms is overtreatment for mild, asymptomatic UPJ narrowing without significant obstruction. Lifelong antibiotic prophylaxis is not indicated for an isolated prior UTI and promotes resistance. Importantly, if surgery is ever needed, the surgeon must account for the anomalous vascular supply (accessory arteries) and isthmus.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point about his horseshoe kidney is most accurate?
options: Horseshoe kidney always leads to kidney failure and he should plan for dialysis
Most people with a horseshoe kidney live normal lives; key risks are stones, infections, and UPJ obstruction, and any future surgery must consider the variable blood supply
He must avoid all physical activity to prevent rupture
Horseshoe kidney is contagious and family members need screening
stages.explanation: Most individuals with a horseshoe kidney are asymptomatic and have a normal life expectancy; the practical issues are an increased risk of stones, urinary infections, and UPJ obstruction, and the importance of recognizing the anomalous and accessory vascular supply during any operation. Claiming it always causes kidney failure is false—renal function is usually preserved. Advising avoidance of all activity is unwarranted; while the lower-lying kidney has a slightly higher exposure to abdominal trauma, normal activity is fine and routine contact-sport counseling is individualized. Calling it contagious is absurd—it is a congenital fusion anomaly, not an infection. Reassurance plus awareness of the specific risks is the correct message.

cases.diagnosis: Horseshoe kidney (incidental, with a small nonobstructing stone and mild UPJ narrowing)
pearls: Horseshoe kidney is the most common renal fusion anomaly—lower poles joined by an isthmus that is trapped under the inferior mesenteric artery during ascent.
Abnormal anatomy (high ureteral insertion, isthmus) predisposes to UPJ obstruction, urinary stasis, stones, and infections.
It is associated with Turner syndrome and trisomy 18, and with an increased risk of Wilms tumor in children; most adults are asymptomatic with normal function.
Manage complications as they arise (hydration, stone prevention, treat infections); any surgery must account for the variable, often accessory, blood supply and the isthmus.
